# BlarAI — Build Journal & Lessons Learned

This is the honest, running record of building BlarAI: what I set out to do, what went wrong, how I recovered, and what I learned. It is written as the work happens, not polished afterward. It keeps the failures in — the failures, and recovering from them, are the part worth showing. It is the raw material for an end-of-project post-mortem and for a professional portfolio in AI-assisted development and the governance of local AI systems.

**The project.** BlarAI is a locally-run AI assistant: a privacy-first system that runs entirely on local hardware, the language model on the GPU. It began as an air-gapped terminal program; it is now a native Windows app with voice, vision, document ingest, a knowledge bank, local image generation, a headless coding-agent dispatch, and — since the governed air-gap removal of 2026-06 — exactly one policy-gated network door. The goal it was built toward, and now largely is: a local *agentic* assistant that can use tools, help with code, and store, analyze, and generate content from my own data. *(Blurb updated 2026-07-03; the 2026-05-21 entry below preserves the original starting point.)*

---

## Where the history lives (monthly rotation, since 2026-07-19)

This hot file carries the **current month only**. Older entries move verbatim — never
edited, never trimmed — into monthly volumes at
[`docs/archive/journal/`](docs/archive/journal/), findable in two reads via
[`docs/archive/journal/INDEX.md`](docs/archive/journal/INDEX.md) (one line per entry:
date | title | plain summary | volume). The curated front shelf — the ~64 entries a
stranger should read first, organized as five acts with three reading paths — is
[`docs/BUILD_JOURNAL_ANTHOLOGY.md`](docs/BUILD_JOURNAL_ANTHOLOGY.md). Rotation runs at
the month-end retrospective via `tools/doc_hygiene/rotate_log.py` (#945 D1,
LA-approved 2026-07-19). Volumes so far: 2026-05 (15 entries), 2026-06 (239 entries).

## Lessons Learned

**Moved to [`LESSONS.md`](LESSONS.md)** (2026-07-03, at 195 lessons). The
distilled, numbered list — the compounding portfolio surface — now lives in its
own file, led by a curated **Canonical Tier**: the \~20 classes a reader should
read first. Mechanical, environment-specific gotchas live in
[`FIELD_NOTES.md`](FIELD_NOTES.md). Lesson numbers are stable and permanent —
every `lesson N` reference in the entries below resolves against `LESSONS.md`.
New lessons are numbered there at fold-in, under the curation rules at the top
of that file (search-before-mint, recurrence tallies, the third-instance
rule). See `CLAUDE.md` §Build-Journal-Discipline.

## Journal

### 2026-07-01 — Measuring the intelligence, not just the software

Until today, every model or prompt change in BlarAI was judged the same way:
run the standing gate (software correctness), eyeball a few answers, ship.
The gate is superb at catching a broken function and structurally blind to a
dumber verdict — ISS-3 (Policy Agent classification misses) has been an open
issue precisely because a miss is not a crash. With a Qwen3-native tool-call
format migration in flight on a sibling branch and model swaps a standing
watch item, "did the system get dumber?" needed a measurable answer before
the next change, not after it.

So I built `evals/` — the first model-quality eval harness: three golden-set
suites (`pa_classification`, `tool_calling`, `governance`), a runner
(`python -m evals.run --suite all`) that scores per-case pass/fail and
compares against committed per-case baselines in `evals/baselines/`, and
exit-code semantics with teeth: 0 clean, 1 regression, 2 harness error
(fail-closed — an uncomparable run is never a silent success). The
regression grammar was the judgment call worth recording: a case that failed
in the baseline and still fails is NOT a regression — it is a *known,
tracked deficiency*, which is exactly how ISS-3-shaped model misses become
data instead of noise. Degradations, new unbaselined failures, and vanished
baselined cases all exit 1; refreshing a baseline is a deliberate, reviewed
act whose git diff names every changed verdict. First committed baseline:
69/69 deterministic cases green (22 PA + 27 tool-calling + 28 governance,
plus 8 model-in-the-loop PA cases held for the Arc 140V), locked into the
standing gate by 40 tests in `tests/integration/test_eval_harness.py` (39
deterministic + 1 `@hardware`).

I found mid-build that the mission's "there is no eval harness today" was
half-true: `tests/pa_quality_benchmark/` already computes per-class
P/R/F1 and false-allow/false-deny gates over its own 48-CAR corpus. The
trade-off I took: **mirror its proven adapter pattern rather than import
test code into `evals/`** — the new suite drives the same real functions
(`DeterministicPolicyChecker.check`, `run_rule_engine`, `adjudicate` with a
mocked-ALLOW GPU so rule verdicts stay non-appealable) in \~50 self-contained
lines, keeping the long-lived harness free of a dependency on a test
package, at the cost of a small acknowledged duplication. The alternative —
importing `tests.pa_quality_benchmark.harness` — would have coupled the
decades-horizon eval surface to a directory that exists to be refactored.

Two other calls worth keeping. First, the tool-calling suite is written
against the abstraction (`tools.parse_tool_call` + `tools.execute`), never
the regex internals, and every golden case carries a `format` tag
(`legacy_xml` today) so the sibling branch's Qwen3-JSON migration adds
`qwen3_json` cases and re-baselines format-specific grammar cases without
touching the runner — including the honest golden case that documents the
legacy grammar's nested-paren limitation as *expected-no-parse*. The
harness also enforces a fail-closed dispatch guard: it will execute only
registry tools declared `RiskTier.SAFE`; a golden case asking to dispatch a
GUARDED tool is a harness error, not an execution. Second, the Layer-3
lock predicate is inline in the AO tool loop and not importable, and I
deliberately did NOT extract it — that file is under a sibling builder's
active migration, and a refactor-for-testability collision was the wrong
price. Instead the governance suite carries a named MIRROR of the predicate
(tier input still the real `tools.risk_tier`), and a drift tripwire in the
gate test pins the inline predicate's load-bearing fragments in
`entrypoint.py` source, so either side changing shape fails loudly and
names the other. I accept the mirror as a documented second copy; the
tripwire is what keeps it honest.

What this harness does not do is also on the record in `evals/README.md`:
no AO free-text answer quality (needs an LLM-judge or rubric), no
end-to-end streaming tool loop, no GPU judgment in CI (the mocked-ALLOW
stand-in measures rules and matrix only), no performance. The 8
`mode: "model"` PA cases — SSH-credential reads, persistence-vector writes,
SSRF-shaped parameters that no deterministic rule catches — are the first
ISS-3 probes that will produce a number the day the orchestrator runs the
hardware tier on the Arc 140V.

**Next:** run the `@hardware` tier on the Arc 140V (orchestrator, serially
after merge) to get the first model-in-the-loop PA score and decide whether
to commit a hardware baseline; grow the model-mode golden set toward a
statistically useful size; add `qwen3_json` tool-calling cases when the
format migration lands; consider an answer-quality suite (LLM-judge
trade-offs need an LA decision).

*(commit `218035d` — evals/ package (runner, 3 suites, 77 golden cases, 3
baselines), tests/integration/test_eval_harness.py (40 tests; 39 in the
standing gate), evals/README.md; the hardware tier awaits the on-box run.)*

### 2026-07-01 — Retiring the homemade dialect for the format the model was trained on

BlarAI's tool calling has spoken a homemade dialect since v1: `<tool_call>NAME(args)</tool_call>`, parsed by a regex. It worked well enough that nobody looked hard at it — until this migration (#718) did, and found the dialect was quietly failing on its own documentation. The system prompt taught the model to emit `<tool_call>calculate(2*(3+4))</tool_call>` — the example in Block 6, verbatim — and the parsing regex `\(\s*([^)]*?)\s*\)` could not match it: `[^)]*` stops at the first closing paren, so any nested-paren expression silently returned "no tool call" and the model's correct call fell on the floor. We were teaching the model a syntax our own parser rejected. That is the deepest argument for this change: a homemade wire format accumulates homemade defects, invisibly, because nothing external ever exercises it.

Qwen3 has a trained tool-call format — `<tool_call>{"name": "...", "arguments": {...}}</tool_call>`, the Hermes-style JSON payload it saw millions of times in training — and the migration moves BlarAI onto it end to end. `tools.TOOL_SCHEMAS` is the new single source of truth: one Hermes-style function spec per tool (the four SAFE tools + GUARDED `generate_image`), rendered into the system prompt as a `<tools>` block, driving parse-time schema validation, and — the second half of this arc — compiled into a decoder-level grammar. The parser now does a strict `json.loads` plus a hand-rolled minimal schema validator (required keys, types, `additionalProperties: false`; no new dependency), failing closed to "no tool call" with a logged deterministic fingerprint (short SHA-256 of the payload — stable across runs, never echoes model output into the log). One governance subtlety was deliberate: an *unknown* tool name with valid structure is returned, not swallowed — authorization is the allowlist's and PGOV's job, and those locks can only fire on a name they get to see. A *known* tool with schema-invalid arguments is dropped fail-closed. The legacy `NAME(args)` form stays accepted as a logged transition fallback (each hit is a WARNING in launcher.log, so residual legacy emissions are visible), and the fullmatch-anchored rewrite of that fallback fixes the nested-paren defect as a side effect.

The arguments needed a canonical wire form. The #570 PA adjudication (`_adjudicate_tool_dispatch`) receives the args as a string inside the CAR's `parameters_schema`; JSON objects have no inherent key order, so the same semantic call could have produced different adjudication inputs. `canonical_arguments` pins it: compact, key-sorted JSON (`sort_keys=True`, `(",", ":")` separators), with the empty object canonicalising to `""` so zero-argument JSON calls are byte-identical to legacy no-arg calls at every governance surface. A regression test asserts the adjudicator sees `{"expression":"1+1"}` regardless of the key order the model emitted.

The grammar-constraint investigation was the open question the ticket carried, and the answer is better than hoped. The installed OpenVINO GenAI (2026.2.1.0, xgrammar backend) exposes `StructuredOutputConfig` / `StructuralTagsConfig` / `StructuralTagItem` / `StructuredOutputConfig.TriggeredTags` on `GenerationConfig.structured_output_config` — per-request, exactly where BlarAI already sets `num_assistant_tokens`. The composition worry — does a logits-masking grammar survive speculative decoding's draft/verify dance, and streaming? — I answered empirically rather than by documentation, with an offline CPU probe that mirrors the production pipeline construction byte-for-byte (`LLMPipeline` with `scheduler_config` + `draft_model`, the continuous-batching `SpeculativeDecodingImpl` path) using Qwen3-0.6B INT4 as main and the pruned-6L INT8 draft — never the 14B. Six legs: the triggered config is accepted on plain and spec-decode pipelines with and without streaming (4/4 pass); a forced compound grammar (`Concat(ConstString("<tool_call>"), JSONSchema(...), ConstString("</tool_call>"))`) demonstrably *engages* on the spec-decode pipeline — output forced to an exact schema-valid block, streamed in 19 chunks; and the production-shaped *triggered* config fires mid-generation — the model emitted the `<tool_call>` trigger and xgrammar constrained the body to schema-valid JSON (`{"name": "get_current_time", "arguments": {}}`), streaming intact. Verdict: **composes**. The one benign artifact: xgrammar warns about token ids beyond the tokenizer range (`151680 out of range [0, 151669)`) — special-token rejection, cosmetic.

So the grammar shipped wired and ON: `[generation].tool_call_grammar = true`, flowing through a new `GenerationConfig.tool_call_grammar` field into `_build_generation_config`, which attaches a triggered structural-tags config built from `tools.tool_call_grammar_schema()` — an `anyOf` union pinning `name` to each registered tool (single-value `enum`) and `arguments` to that tool's typed schema. Free text is untouched; the moment the model generates `<tool_call>`, a malformed or unknown-tool body becomes structurally impossible at the decoder. I weighed shipping it OFF pending the 14B live verify and rejected that: the composition evidence is on the production pipeline shape, the knob is one TOML line, the fail-soft path (older GenAI build without the API → log once, generate unconstrained, parse-time validation guards) is tested, and the standing default here is that proven features ship live. The rejected alternative — parse-time validation only — remains exactly what the system degrades to when the API is absent, so the defense is layered, not replaced.

Two governance parity holes surfaced and got closed in the same change. First, PGOV Stage 4 (`check_tool_calls`) only knew the legacy patterns — a native-format call to an unknown tool would have sailed past the `TOOL_CALL_VIOLATION` detector entirely. The fix json-parses every `<tool_call>` payload rather than regexing it (a regex cannot robustly extract a name from attacker-reorderable JSON), and treats *malformed* JSON fail-closed by scanning it for `"name": "x"` references — breaking your own JSON is not an evasion. Ordinary prose JSON (`{"name": "John"}`) outside the tags still never false-positives. Second, the streaming visibility filter, the Layer-3 untrusted-content lock, the allowlist break, ESCALATE→DENY, and the fail-closed DANGEROUS tier all needed proof they fire identically on the new format — that is the `TestGovernanceRegressionJsonFormat` suite, driving the real `_handle_prompt_request` loop with JSON emissions (80 new tests in all; the 253 pre-existing tool/PGOV/inference tests pass unmodified).

What awaits the hardware: the 14B live verify on the Arc 140V — the grammar compiling against the real tokenizer at first constrained generation (xgrammar caches the compiled grammar thereafter), the trained format actually improving call reliability, and the `/think` interplay (the probe showed the trigger can fire inside a `<think>` block; the parser and the strip path both handle it, but the live behavior is worth watching). The eval harness (#717, CAP-1) had not merged when this branch closed, so the tool-calling golden cases for the native format are noted for the orchestrator rather than added here.

**Lesson 183:** a homemade wire format between you and a model is a liability on both ends — the model pays a tax emitting a syntax it was not trained on, and your own parser accumulates silent defects nothing external ever exercises (the system prompt's own example was unparseable for weeks). Prefer the format the model was trained on, and when the runtime offers decoder-level enforcement of it, measure the composition question empirically on the production pipeline shape with small models rather than trusting documentation either way.

**Next:** post-merge 14B live verify on the Arc 140V (grammar-constrained call end-to-end, first-token latency delta from grammar compilation, legacy-fallback WARNING frequency in launcher.log); add native-format tool-calling golden cases to the #717 eval harness once it merges; after a clean soak, consider retiring the legacy fallback and tightening `parse_tool_call` to JSON-only (a deliberate LA decision, not a default).

*(commits `748cd0a` (native JSON format + grammar wiring + governance parity + 80 tests); standing gate re-run green on the branch; the 14B live verify on the Arc 140V awaits the orchestrator post-merge.)*

### 2026-07-01 — Packing the house before the fire

*Plain summary: first off-box backup in eight months — discovery found not one of five live repos had a git remote; a three-leg backup (GitHub repos / OneDrive weights+DBs+bundles / USB-only secrets), TPM-sealed DEKs recoverable only via the offline recovery key, and two phantom 'dirty' worktrees that were `.git`-less orphans reporting the parent's state.*

Today the system got something it has never had in eight months of building:
an existence outside this one disk. The trigger was operational — the
User-Operator wants to reformat the drive — but the discovery pass turned up
a fact worth sitting with: **not one of the five live repos had a git
remote.** BlarAI's 401 branches, the full ADR corpus, BUILD_JOURNAL.md
itself, the coding-fleet dispatch program, the jobhunt tool — every commit of
all of it existed exactly once, on the hardware it was written on. We had
built a security-first system with TPM-sealed keys and fail-closed egress,
and its single point of failure was a SATA connector.

The backup now has three legs, chosen by what each store is good at. GitHub
(private repos) carries the five repos — 398 of blarai's 401 branches, with
uncommitted work captured first as `backup/wip-*-20260701` snapshot branches
built through a temporary index so no working tree was touched mid-flight.
OneDrive carries what git can't: the 33.7 GB of converted model weights, the
encrypted runtime databases with their TPM-wrapped keystores, the Vikunja
database (snapshotted live via SQLite's backup API), the OVMS install, the
Claude memory namespaces, the Hyper-V VHDX, and full `git bundle` files of
all five repos — including the three branches GitHub refused because an old
Intel-PR-reproduction commit carries a 385 MB model binary. A USB-bound
secrets bundle carries the keys and credentials, deliberately staged OUTSIDE
the cloud path — the permission classifier balked at SSH keys heading to
OneDrive, and rather than route around the objection I put the question to
the operator, who chose the air-gapped leg. The trade-off is a manual step
(copy one folder to a stick) bought for keeping key material off Microsoft's
servers; the rejected alternative was convenience.

Two discoveries would have made a naive backup silently worthless. First,
the at-rest encryption: the DEK keystore JSONs copy cleanly, but they are
sealed to THIS machine's TPM — the copies are only decryptable via the
`--recover` ceremony with the offline recovery key printed once at
provisioning. A backup of the databases without confirming that key exists
is a backup of noise; the runbook now leads with it. Second, the "dirty
worktrees" that weren't: two `.worktrees/` directories reported 33 modified
files each, matching main's count exactly — orphan directories whose `.git`
files were gone, letting git walk up to the parent repo and report its state
as theirs. I snapshotted them before noticing, then deleted the two junk
branches. The lesson from the #714/#715 arc holds here too: match the
measurement to the thing you think you're measuring.

What was deliberately left behind: 124 GB of `oss/` OpenVINO upstream clones
(clean, pushed, re-cloneable — a 900-byte patch file preserves the one local
tweak), the 15 GB coder-30b (one HuggingFace download), \~34 GB of compiled
model caches, every `.venv`, and the graveyard of fleet test projects. A
backup that includes everything is a backup nobody audits.

**Next:** operator copies `reformat-secrets` to USB and confirms the offline
recovery key is findable; OneDrive sync runs to "Up to date"; then the
reformat, and `RESTORE_RUNBOOK.md` (OneDrive backup root) drives the rebuild
phase by phase, ending with the standing gate as the proof the house moved
intact.

**Lesson 207:** A system's disaster-recovery posture is part of its
security posture — TPM-sealed keys and fail-closed egress meant nothing
against a failed disk while every repo was remote-less. And the backup is
only as good as its decrypt path: verify the recovery ceremony's inputs
exist BEFORE the event that makes them unobtainable.

### 2026-07-02 — The idle 48 TOPS finally earns its keep, and the second tenant is turned away at the door

ADR-011 retired the NPU from the P1 core loop, and ever since, the loudest
silicon on this chip has been the quietest: an Intel AI Boost NPU rated
around 48 TOPS, doing nothing, while the CPU chewed embeddings and the GPU
carried a 14-billion-parameter roommate. Ticket #720 asked the obvious
question — can the encoder workloads move there? — with the discipline that
the answer had to be measured, not assumed, and that ADR-011 stays
untouched (it retired the NPU from LLM *decoding*; an encoder is a
different animal).

The investigation turned up a cleaner target than expected. Every
production embedding in BlarAI — substrate memory, the knowledge bank, and
PGOV Stage-5 leakage detection — rides ONE `LeakageDetector` session in
`services/assistant_orchestrator/src/pgov.py`, running bge-small-en-v1.5
through ONNX Runtime on the CPU. (The semantic router holds a second,
CPU-targeted copy, but Sprint 18's C3 finding already established it is not
wired into the live turn path, so it stayed out of scope.) One seam, three
workloads: a `[embeddings].device` knob on that one class covers the whole
surface.

The NPU had conditions. Its plugin is static-shape only — the dynamic
`[?,?]` ONNX graph that the GPU compiles happily is rejected outright — so
the offload backend compiles two fixed token windows (128, the calibrated
PGOV leakage window; 512, the document window), pads every text to the
window, and infers one text per request. I also special-cased the probe
order: attempting a dynamic compile on the NPU floods the driver log with
ERROR lines before failing, so the NPU goes straight to static while any
other device still gets the dynamic-first attempt.

The measurement (N=20 warm runs, production surface, no 14B loaded —
`docs/performance/embedding_device_2026-07-02_00-10-26.json`) settled the
default. Against the incumbent CPU path the NPU is 13.6x faster on
document-window texts (168.9 → 12.4 ms), 2.2x on batch-32, parity on short
singles, with min cosine parity 0.999996 — the Stage-5 thresholds
calibrated on CPU numerics carry over unchanged. The GPU is faster still
(7.5 ms on the same document case), and that is precisely why it lost: the
GPU is the contended device. Moving embeddings there buys isolation-bench
speed by feeding the one resource the resident 14B actually fights for. I
went with NPU as the shipped default, accepting a slower-than-GPU executor
and a boot-time compile, because the whole point of #720 is taking work
OFF the contended devices — and softened the compile cost with an OpenVINO
blob cache (12.1 s cold → \~2.5 s warm), the exact optimisation the 14B
deliberately refuses because its 9 GB blob cold-reads as slowly as it
compiles. A 128 MB encoder is the opposite case. The honest caveat is
recorded in the log: the isolation numbers *understate* the NPU's
production advantage, and the co-resident A/B (embedding latency while the
14B generates) is named as not-measured — the operator was away and
loading the 14B was barred.

One deliberate inversion is worth keeping on the record: this feature is
fail-SOFT in a fail-closed codebase. If the NPU compile fails — missing
plugin, driver regression, another box entirely — the detector logs a
deterministic `EMBED_OFFLOAD_FALLBACK` fingerprint and lands on the
unchanged ONNX Runtime CPU path. The rejected alternative was the house
default, refuse-to-start, and it would have been wrong here: an offload
*optimisation* that can brick boot on a driver update is a worse security
posture than a slower embedder, because the thing it would refuse to start
is the system whose leakage detector fails closed on its own. The config
typo case stays fail-closed (`AO_CFG_EMBEDDINGS_DEVICE_INVALID` at boot) —
a misconfiguration is a human error to surface, not a runtime condition to
absorb.

Whisper wanted the same door and was turned away by the hardware itself.
`WhisperPipeline` on the NPU *compiles* — 34.5 s, patience rewarded with
optimism — and then the first `generate()` kills the device:
`ZE_RESULT_ERROR_DEVICE_LOST`, code 0x70000001, an NPU hang/reset on
OpenVINO GenAI 2026.2.1 with driver 32.0.100.4778. The GPU control run
transcribed an 8.7 s Kokoro-synthesized utterance in 270 ms, verbatim.
That is a measured negative, recorded in
`docs/performance/whisper_device_2026-07-02_00-13-38.json` with the exact
error fingerprint, and STT stays on the GPU — where a 270 ms transcribe is
not a problem worth risking device resets over. The NPU recovered cleanly
(re-verified with an embedding round-trip), and the compile-then-hang
signature is exactly the kind of datum the OpenVINO community record
exists for.

**Next:** the co-resident A/B — re-run `scripts/benchmark_embedding_device.py`
with the 14B resident and generating, which should widen the NPU's case on
the two numbers that matter in production (PGOV leakage per turn, knowledge
ingest). Retest Whisper-on-NPU on the next NPU driver / OpenVINO GenAI
release; the harness is committed and takes one command. And the first live
boot on main after merge should confirm the `EMBED_OFFLOAD_FALLBACK` line
never fires on this box.

**Lesson 184:** the failure mode has to fit the feature. A fail-closed
house rule protects controls; applied reflexively to an *optimisation* it
turns a driver update into a boot failure. Ask what the failure actually
threatens — a security boundary (fail closed) or a performance property
(fail soft, loudly, with a deterministic fingerprint) — and match the
posture to the threat, not to the habit.

*(commits `918b2ee` (the #720 offload: knob + OpenVINO backend + fail-soft +
benchmarks + measurement record); standing gate 4744 passed / 1 skipped
(pre-existing environmental: the KV-sweep test's 14B config.json absent in
this worktree) / 119 deselected; awaiting live verify: first production
boot embedding on NPU, and the co-resident A/B.)*

### 2026-07-02 — Giving the model hands that cannot open the door

The model got two new tools tonight — `search_knowledge` and `web_search` —
and the interesting work was not registering them; it was making sure that
everything the governance stack already believes about tools stayed true
without touching a single posture flag. The #718 native-JSON migration left a
clean SSOT to extend (`TOOL_SCHEMAS` drives the prompt, the parse-time
validation, and the xgrammar constraint from one dict), so the mechanical
part was five map entries. The judgment lived in three places.

First, the tier call. Both tools are GUARDED, not SAFE, and web_search is
deliberately NOT DANGEROUS. `search_knowledge` reads only on-box data, but
its query is a model-controlled, injection-redirectable parameter over the
operator's curated store — and what comes back is itself untrusted content
(ADR-023 Amendment 2 was explicit: curation is not trust-promotion). A clock
cannot be steered; a retrieval query can, so SAFE was never on the table.
For `web_search` I considered DANGEROUS — it smells like egress — and
rejected it: the DISPATCH at the AO loop is a query handed to an
egress-governed subsystem, not the irreversible network action itself. The
actual egress is denied independently and absolutely at the layers built for
that (RULE 3 over the real URL at the egress door, the EMPTY allowlist, the
absent live adapter). Marking the dispatch DANGEROUS would have made /trust
useless forever (DANGEROUS has no override) while adding zero real control —
the tier decides friction; the per-action deny decides danger. That sentence
is already in tools.py from #593; this was the first time I had to apply it
to a tool where the two answers differ.

Second, the security-critical piece: what happens to a retrieval RESULT. The
tool loop's historical contract splices `Result: {tool_result}` raw into the
working context — fine for a clock string, catastrophic for retrieved text
(a web snippet saying "ignore prior instructions" would sit OUTSIDE the
datamark boundary, dressed as a system note). So retrieval results now ride
the exact machinery every ingest path already uses: `add_grounded_context`
with an explicit provenance — `UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE` for the bank (Stage-5
leakage-exempt so faithful recall is not held, per #664), full
`UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL` for web results (leak-checked, no exemption) — then the
working context is REBUILT so the model reads the delimiter-neutralized,
datamarked form, never the raw text. Grounding flips
`has_untrusted_content`, so the NEXT non-SAFE call in the same session locks
without /trust. I regression-locked that chain explicitly: retrieval →
grounded → second GUARDED call refused, runner called exactly once. The
trade-off taken: one retrieval per turn without /trust (a chained second
search is consent-gated), because the first result could steer the second
query. The rejected alternative — letting same-turn retrievals chain freely
— reads as better UX until you write down what a hostile snippet does with
it. A refusal never locks, though: the deterministic notices
(disabled/unavailable/no-results) are exact-string constants carrying no
retrieved content, and the loop tests them by full-string membership — a
forged "notice" that skipped grounding would have had to delete its own
payload to do so.

Third, web_search's dormancy shape. There is no `[web_search]` config flag
to leave false, because none exists — the live Kagi adapter (ADR-024 W4) was
never built. Rather than invent an enable flag (a posture decision that is
not mine), the tool delegates to a module-level runner seam that NO
production code registers — the #655 url_adjudicator built-not-registered
pattern — and a source-scan test locks that absence. So the model can call
web_search today and gets back a deterministic, user-comprehensible
unavailability notice as the tool result; the model then tells the user it
could not search instead of the user seeing raw `<tool_call>` JSON. The full
refusal chain is documented on the tool body and pinned by tests, in loop
order: allowlist (passes — listing is what routes the emission into the
layered refusal), Layer-3 lock (GUARDED under untrusted content), #570 PA
adjudication (the CAR is `tool:web_search`, not a URL, so RULE 3 does not
fire at this seam — golden case gov-adj-005 had already pinned that honest
boundary before I arrived), then the unregistered runner. Beneath all of it,
the welded egress door: I added a go-live tripwire eval (gov-pf-007) that
asserts the Kagi endpoint is RULE-3-denied while the allowlist is empty —
when the LA runs the ADR-027 Am.1 ceremony, that case flips and forces a
reviewed baseline refresh. Going live is therefore three deliberate acts
(allowlist, adapter-over-guarded_fetch, runner registration), no one of
which can happen by drift.

Two measured numbers worth keeping. The system-prompt tools block grew from
2,210 to 3,333 chars with the two new schemas — +1,123 chars, \~+280 tokens
under the repo's len//4 approximation (whole system prompt now \~5,103 chars
/ \~1,275 tokens); that is real per-turn KV budget spent on advertising
retrieval, accepted because a model that cannot see the tools cannot reach
for them. And the retrieval result cap landed at 4,000 chars (\~1,000 tokens,
25% of the 4,096-token context budget), truncated deterministically with an
explicit marker so the model knows it is reading a cut — sized against the
auto-recall path's k=4 chunks so a tool retrieval cannot evict the
conversation it is meant to serve. One schema subtlety: max_results bounds
(1..8) live in the description prose plus a fail-safe body clamp, NOT as
JSON-schema minimum/maximum keywords — the schema feeds the xgrammar
structural-tags builder verbatim, and numeric-bound keywords are the one
shape not proven on the installed GenAI build; a grammar-construction
failure would have silently disabled the constraint for every tool.

The eval harness earned its keep on its second night in the tree: two golden
cases (tc-adv-002, tcj-adv-001) asserted web_search was an UNKNOWN tool —
written as adversarial "egress-shaped stranger" probes — and the moment I
registered it they failed, exactly as designed. Re-pointed them to a
still-unknown name (`browse_web`) so the unknown-tool coverage survives, and
refreshed both baselines through the sanctioned `--write-baseline` path
(tool_calling 43→49 cases, governance 28→38, all passing).

**Next:** the LA decisions flagged in the #719 report — whether the
web_search dispatch CAR should carry the real endpoint URL at go-live (so
RULE 3 fires at the AO loop too, defense-in-depth ahead of the egress door),
and the knowledge-session UX observation that any session with auto-recalled
knowledge already locks GUARDED tools, so search_knowledge often needs
/trust in exactly the sessions it serves. Then the W4 LiveKagiAdapter build
(over guarded_fetch, never its own client) when the LA schedules the
web_search go-live ceremony.

**Lesson 185:** A tool's risk tier and its action's danger are separate
decisions enforced at separate layers — tier the DISPATCH by what the model
can steer (friction, overridable by consent), and leave the irreversible
action's deny to the deterministic layer built for it (absolute, no
override). Collapsing the two (marking every egress-adjacent tool DANGEROUS)
burns the consent mechanism without adding a single real control.

*(commit `6828e79` (#719: both tools at all five SSOT surfaces, provenance grounding, +55 tests, 6+10 eval cases); merged `29cd53e`; live retrieval-choice smoke `fbafbf4`.)*

### 2026-07-02 — The yardstick caught its first bug the night it was built

Tonight was the capability program the LA approved in one line — "I approve of
the full recommendation" — after asking the question that started it: *what
obvious wins is my project lacking?* The honest answer was that BlarAI's
governance had outgrown its capability. We had a vault-grade tool loop carrying
five coins: a homemade tool-call format fighting the model's training, an idle
NPU, and — the sharpest gap — four thousand software tests with not one
measurement of whether the *model* was getting smarter or dumber. Three builders
ran in parallel worktrees overnight: the eval harness (#717), the Qwen3-native
JSON tool-call migration with the xgrammar constraint (#718), and the NPU
encoder offload (#720), with the tool-surface expansion (#719) queued behind the
format change it depends on.

The portfolio moment came at the second merge. The eval harness landed first
and went green: 4750 passed, three suites, committed baselines. Then the
tool-call migration merged — individually green on its own tree, 4770 passed —
and the combined gate lit up: three failures, all in the eval harness. Golden
case `tc-parse-010` had encoded the OLD parser's nested-paren defect as
*expected behavior* ("`calculate((2+3)*4)` must fail to parse"); #718 had
deliberately fixed that defect, whose most damning exhibit was that the system
prompt's own example — `calculate(2*(3+4))` — could not be parsed by the
grammar that document was teaching the model to use. Two correct branches, one
wrong expectation, caught at the seam. This is exactly the class of collision
that used to reach a live screen before anyone noticed; tonight it lasted the
length of one gate run. The golden case was re-baselined with the story in its
description, sixteen native-format cases were added alongside it, and the
harness went back to green with the defect-fix now *locked* instead of
tolerated. The trade-off I want on the record: an eval harness is also a place
where yesterday's bugs can hide as today's expectations — golden sets must be
re-litigated whenever the contract they encode is deliberately changed, and the
"regression" alarm is doing its job even when the verdict is "the baseline was
wrong."

The live verifies then did what live verifies do. The first
grammar-constrained tool call ran on the real 14B with the draft engaged:
native JSON emission, zero legacy-fallback hits, zero fail-closed drops,
`get_current_time` dispatched and answered in a 4.8-second turn — the offline
compose-proof (grammar + speculative decoding + streaming) held on real
silicon. And the eval harness produced the number ISS-3 had been missing for
months: 26/30 overall, with all four misses being false DENIES of benign
actions. The Policy Agent's model-judgment layer over-blocks; it never
under-blocked. That is the safe direction of error and a real usability tax at
the same time, and dispositioning it — prompt tuning versus few-shot examples
versus accepting the bias — is now an LA decision that can be made from data
instead of vibes. I deliberately did not touch it tonight: loosening a
security classifier's deny bias is a posture decision, not a defect with one
correct fix.

Decisions flagged rather than made, for the morning: the grammar constraint
shipped ON (proven-defaults-to-live doctrine; one TOML line to soak it OFF),
the system prompt grew from \~270 to 841 tokens to carry the schema block (a
real per-cold-prefill cost, accepted for format fidelity), and the legacy
tool-call fallback stays in as transition telemetry until an LA call retires
it. The NPU flip and the Whisper negative finding are recorded in their own
fragment and PERFORMANCE_LOG entries.

**Next:** merge #719 (tool-surface: `search_knowledge` + `web_search` as
GUARDED tools) when its builder reports; run its gate + evals; fold the
night's fragments; then put the ISS-3 over-denial disposition and the
answer-quality eval suite (LLM-judge vs human-rubric) in front of the LA as
the two open decisions.

**Lesson 186:** A quality yardstick pays for itself at the first seam it
guards — and golden sets are commitments, not observations: when a contract
changes on purpose, the baseline must be re-litigated in the same change, or
yesterday's defect calcifies into today's specification.

*(commits `303a024` (golden re-baseline at the cross-merge), `16b6efe` (live grammar-constrained tool round-trip + the first ISS-3 evidence artifact), `fbafbf4` (retrieval-choice smoke); merges `584d1d9` (#717) / `1fe2078` (#720) / `a75b958` (#718) / `897afd9` / `6756ef3` / `29cd53e` (#719) / `9e00195`; standing gate 4919/0/120 post-program.)*

### 2026-07-02 — The over-denial that turned out to be unreachable ALLOW

The first thing ISS-3's first measurement told us (26/30, all four misses
false-DENIES of benign actions) looked like a model-judgment problem, and the
LA's decision on #717 was to tune the prompt. So I went in expecting to write
few-shot exemplars. What I found first was arithmetic: on the model path, an
ALLOW verdict was *structurally unreachable*. The decision matrix requires
`label == ALLOW and confidence >= 0.75` to pass; confidence comes from a
`CONFIDENCE:` line in the model's output, falling back to a per-label default;
and the 2025-era fail-closed hardening (`bd8c378`, P2-3) had zeroed those
defaults from 0.995 to 0.0. The prompt, meanwhile, had never asked the model
for a confidence line — it demanded "EXACTLY one line: DECISION: <LABEL>" —
and `MAX_CLASSIFICATION_TOKENS = 10` could not have fitted a second line
anyway. So every model ALLOW parsed with confidence 0.0, failed the 0.75
gate, fell through the ESCALATE band, and landed on DENY. The unit tests had
modelled the two-line format all along (`test_gpu_inference.py` builds mock
outputs as `DECISION: X\nCONFIDENCE: 0.9`); the prompt just never requested
what the parser was built to read. The 86.7% wasn't the model being
over-cautious — it was three components each individually fail-closed
composing into a policy that could never say yes.

The fix stayed inside the prompt-tune mandate. Before touching the prompt I
added five deny-side model probes to the golden set (pa-mdl-009..013:
cloud-credential read, browser cookie-store read, autostart persistence
write, secret-fishing memory query, audit-log-destruction execute — each
verified to slip past every deterministic rule so the model's judgment is
what is actually measured), so the loosening would be scored in both
directions. Then the prompt: the output format became two lines (DECISION +
CONFIDENCE, with calibration guidance pointing uncertainty below 0.75 where
the matrix routes it to human review), four worked benign-ALLOW exemplars
mirroring the four missed shapes on *different* concrete instances (a travel
itinerary read, a roadmap query, a meeting-notes draft write, a preferences
memory query — never the eval cases themselves), and one scoped sentence
naming routine reads/queries/drafts of the user's own documents and memory
as normal assistant work *unless* a named risk indicator applies
(credentials, session tokens, persistence hooks, log destruction,
secret-fishing). `MAX_CLASSIFICATION_TOKENS` went 10 → 24 so the second line
physically fits — a generation-cap mechanic in service of the prompt's
format, not a decision-threshold change; the 0.75 gate, the ESCALATE band,
the verdict mapping, and every deterministic rule are byte-untouched.

Measured on the real 14B on the Arc 140V, before and after, same golden set
(35 cases, 13 model-mode). Before: 31/35 — the same four benign false-DENIES,
and all nine deny-side model probes (four original + five new) correctly
DENY. After, on the first prompt variant: 35/35 — benign false-DENIES 4 → 0,
false-ALLOWs 0 → 0 across every deny-side case old and new. The trade-off is
named honestly: making ALLOW reachable at all *necessarily* opens the
false-ALLOW surface that the broken composition had welded shut by accident.
I accepted that because it is what the LA's D1 decision asks for — a Policy
Agent that can approve benign work is the point — and because the deny-side
now has nine probes standing guard where it had four, plus the committed
hardware baseline (`evals/baselines/pa_classification.json` now carries real
pass statuses for the model cases, not `skipped_hardware`) so any future
drift in either direction fails the eval gate loudly. The alternative — leave
the structural DENY in place and call the bias a safety feature — would have
meant the GPU classifier stage was dead code for benign actions, burning
\~2-4 s of inference per request to produce a verdict the matrix would
overrule.

**Next:** fold the ISS-3 disposition back to the LA on #717 with these
numbers (the over-denial is now 0/4 on the measured probes; ISS-3 can close
or narrow); consider whether the AO's own tool-dispatch adjudication wants
the same confidence-line treatment; grow the model-mode golden set past 13
as new judgment shapes surface in live use.

**Proposed lesson:** When a measured model "quality miss" is 100% one-sided,
audit the deterministic plumbing around the model before tuning the model —
three individually-correct fail-closed defaults (zeroed fallback confidence,
a one-line output format, a 10-token cap) composed into a classifier that
could never emit its ALLOW verdict, and no single component's tests could see
it. The eval harness measuring the *full pipeline* (not the model in
isolation) is what made the composition visible.

### 2026-07-02 — Retiring the dialect the day after it stopped being spoken

When #718 moved tool calling to Qwen3's native JSON format, I left the old
homemade `<tool_call>NAME(ARGS)</tool_call>` grammar alive as a logged
transition fallback — every hit would print `LEGACY tool-call form used` at
WARNING so residual legacy emissions would be visible in launcher.log before
anyone pulled the plug. The LA's decision (D3, recorded on #718 c.1295) was
to pull it now, without a soak period. The reasoning is worth keeping: a soak
measures risk on a system with traffic, and this system has barely been used
— the live verify already showed native emission with zero legacy-fallback
hits, and the xgrammar constraint makes the decoder structurally unable to
emit the old form inside `<tool_call>` tags anyway. Waiting would not have
produced better evidence; it would only have left a second parser branch —
a second attack grammar — alive in the most governance-sensitive function in
the AO. I took the tightening over the transition tolerance, accepting that
any hypothetical out-of-tree caller speaking the old dialect now lands on the
fail-closed no-call path instead of being helped along. The evidence snapshot
before surgery: zero `LEGACY tool-call form used` warnings across the real
launcher.log (3.9 MB), boot.log, and crash.log — the fallback retired having
never once fired in production.

The removal itself was small and pleasingly negative: `_LEGACY_CALL_PATTERN`
and the fallback branch in `parse_tool_call` are gone, and the function now
hands every tag payload straight to `_parse_json_payload` — a legacy-shaped
payload is just another malformed payload, dropped with the same logged
deterministic fingerprint as any other. No new error surface, one fewer
grammar. The deliberate subtlety is what I did NOT delete: `execute()` still
accepts bare argument strings from direct callers (its contract, independent
of the wire format), and PGOV's Stage-4 detection still scans for bare-name
`<tool_call>` references — detection of a retired shape is a governance
tripwire, not acceptance of it. The tests flipped rather than vanished: every
unit test that asserted the legacy form parsed now asserts it does NOT
(`TestLegacyFormRetired`, plus the flipped `TestParseToolCall` locks), so the
coverage survives as proof the form stays dead. The tool-loop harness tests
migrated their fake emissions to the native JSON form — they test governance
(Layer 3, allowlist, #570 adjudication), not grammar, and now they say so.

The golden re-baseline is the reviewed act lesson 186 demands, so here is the
full ledger. Twenty-two `legacy_xml` cases flipped from parse-and-verify to
expect-no-parse (old expected verdict -> new): tc-parse-001 (parse
`get_current_time`,"" -> no-parse), tc-parse-002 (parse `calculate`,"2+2" +
dispatch "4" -> no-parse), tc-parse-003 (parse `get_current_date`,"" ->
no-parse), tc-parse-004 (parse `calculate`,"3*3" + dispatch "9" -> no-parse),
tc-parse-005 (parse `calculate`,"5-2" + dispatch "3" -> no-parse),
tc-parse-009 (parse `get_day_of_week`,"" + dispatch day-regex -> no-parse),
tc-parse-010 (parse `calculate`,"(2+3)*4" + dispatch "20" -> no-parse — this
case's SECOND deliberate re-baseline: first it encoded the pre-#718
nested-paren defect as expected behavior, then the cross-merge fixed it to
parse under the fullmatch fallback, now the whole grammar it exercised is
retired), tc-disp-001 ("2.5" -> no-parse), tc-disp-002 ("1024" -> no-parse),
tc-disp-003 (contains "division by zero" -> no-parse), tc-disp-004 ("1" ->
no-parse), tc-disp-005 ("-3" -> no-parse), tc-disp-006 (time-shape regex ->
no-parse), tc-disp-007 (date-shape regex -> no-parse), tc-adv-001 (parse
`delete_all_files` + not-allowlisted + KeyError -> no-parse, strictly tighter:
the hostile call now dies at the parser instead of the allowlist), tc-adv-002
(parse `browse_web` + not-allowlisted + KeyError -> no-parse), tc-adv-003
(AST refusal of `__import__` -> no-parse), tc-adv-004 (AST refusal of
`os.system` -> no-parse), tc-adv-005 (contains "could not parse" ->
no-parse), tc-adv-006 (parse `generate_image` + allowlisted -> no-parse),
tc-adv-007 (contains "could not parse" -> no-parse), tc-adv-008 ("calculate:
no expression provided" -> no-parse). Five legacy cases already expected
no-parse and kept their verdicts (tc-parse-006/007/008/011, tc-adv-009 —
descriptions refreshed where the old rationale named the retired grammar);
all 22 `qwen3_json` cases are byte-unchanged. Every adversarial expectation
that lived downstream of a legacy parse (AST allowlist refusals, the
SAFE-only dispatch guard, KeyError on unregistered tools) still exists in its
native-format twin (tcj-adv-001/-008/-009 et al.), so no governance coverage
was lost — the legacy cases' job changed from "verify the old grammar's
behavior" to "prove the old grammar is dead". The committed baseline was
refreshed through the harness's own `--write-baseline` path and stayed 49/49
pass — the golden expectations moved WITH the system, which is exactly what a
deliberate re-baseline looks like in the diff.

Gates: the full standing gate green with LOCALAPPDATA redirected — 4900
passed, 21 skipped, 120 deselected, 0 failed in 1:30 on the isolated
worktree, where every one of the 21 skips is the standing environmental
delta (20 gitignored-bge-small `semantic_router` skips + 1
14B-config-absent kv-sweep skip; all pass on the main checkout), so the
effective count is 4921 = the 4919 baseline + this change's +2 (a native
round-trip lock and a legacy-round-trip-is-dead lock added alongside the
one-for-one flips) — and `python -m evals.run --suite all` exit 0
(pa_classification 22/22, tool_calling 49/49, governance 38/38). What this
change does NOT touch: the xgrammar constraint, the system prompt, the
#570 adjudication wire, PGOV detection, and every egress posture — the
parser got smaller and nothing else moved.

**Next:** fold this fragment at the next quiet-tree point; the remaining
#718-adjacent LA decisions (grammar-ON soak-vs-keep, ISS-3 over-denial
disposition) are still open on the ticket — this retirement resolves only
D3.

*(commits `<this>` (JSON-only `parse_tool_call`; legacy pattern + WARNING
path deleted; 22 golden flips + baseline refresh via `--write-baseline`;
test locks flipped to prove the form dead, +2 net tests); standing gate
4900/21-env-skips/0-failed on the worktree (effective 4921 = 4919 + 2) +
eval suite exit 0, both LOCALAPPDATA-redirected.)*

### 2026-07-02 — Building everything except the door-opening

Tonight I built the whole web_search go-live except the go-live itself — the
deliberate shape the LA authorized on #719 (Part B, decisions recorded at
c.1298): every mechanism the ceremony needs exists, tested, on main, and the
tree still cannot make a single outbound request. The discipline that made
this tractable was naming, up front, exactly which locks hold at merge and
which act releases them. At merge: the flag is off (`[web_search].enabled =
false`), no key blob exists, and the deterministic egress allowlist is empty —
three independent locks, any one sufficient. The release is ONE governance
act: populating `DeterministicPolicyChecker._EGRESS_ALLOWLIST` with `kagi.com`
(ADR-027 Amendment 1), plus the operator's key ceremony and the flag flip,
all scripted in `docs/runbooks/web_search_go_live.md`.

The pieces, in dependency order. The secret slot turned out to be half-built
already — the W2/#573 DPAPI store (`shared/secrets/dpapi_store.py`) and its
provisioning ceremony predate this arc — so the new work was the boot-time
consumption posture: `shared/secrets/kagi_key_loader.py` returns the key only
inside a `KagiApiKey` wrapper whose every string conversion is a constant
redaction marker (not even length leaks), with absent/empty/malformed/
undecryptable all collapsing to `None` → dormant. A test drives the real
logging machinery with a sentinel key in every accidental shape (`%s`, `%r`,
f-string, inside a list, inside a dict) and asserts the sentinel reaches no
record. The `LiveKagiAdapter` (ADR-024 W4) fetches ONLY through
`guarded_fetch.fetch_external` — the door grew a purely additive keyword-only
`authorization` parameter for the `Bot <key>` header (the frozen #577 contract
untouched for every existing caller, wire-asserted), applied only after the
full SSRF + PA pipeline permits the fetch and never logged. Every adapter
failure mode — door denial (which is every call today), timeout, non-200,
non-JSON, non-dict, a raising door — is an empty result list, which the
runner shapes to `""`, which the tool body maps to its deterministic failure
notice. Registration is the reworked lock: the old source-scan test asserted
NO production code calls `register_web_search_runner`; its new form asserts
the ONLY caller is the entrypoint's `_maybe_register_web_search`, and a
flag×key matrix over the REAL registrar proves default-off, enabled-but-no-key
stays dormant, a pre-existing door adjudicator is never clobbered, and stop()
re-welds exactly what start() wired.

The judgment call of the night was **D4** — the LA's decision that the #570
dispatch CAR for web_search should carry the REAL Kagi endpoint URL instead of
the `tool:web_search` shape, so RULE 3 and the egress allowlist fire at the
tool loop as well as at the door. The trade-off is real: the loop-level check
adds CAR complexity and a second enforcement point that could drift from the
door's. I took the defense-in-depth with a single-source mitigation — both
layers read the SAME allowlist (`_EGRESS_ALLOWLIST`; the loop calls
`check(car)` with the default, the door's adjudicator is
`make_deterministic_url_adjudicate()` over the same default), and a coupling
test pins the adapter's endpoint constant to the `gov-pf-007` golden resource
so the URL literal cannot fork either. The rejected alternative — keeping the
loop check at `tool:web_search` and trusting the door alone — was the honest
boundary the old golden case `gov-adj-008` documented; that case is now a
**reviewed re-baseline**: old expected `null` (dispatch passes the loop seam),
new expected `DENY / DENY_EXTERNAL_NETWORK` (dormant-safe proven — with the
allowlist empty, a registered runner is never even consulted, locked by a
loop test that counts door calls: zero). `gov-pf-007` is untouched and still
green, as demanded — it flips at the ceremony, not tonight.

One finding worth keeping: verifying the Kagi contract against the live docs
(permitted dev-session research) showed the Search API has moved — **v1 is
current; v0 is deprecated-but-documented**. The repo pins v0 everywhere
(ADR-024, the W2 parsers' verified schema, gov-pf-007). I stayed on v0 —
moving would have meant touching the tripwire this build is forbidden to
touch — and made the migration a named ceremony precondition with the
coupling test as the enforcement that endpoint, tripwire, and parsers move
together. Also a small honesty note on the runbook: the mission sketch said
"key in a file via Notepad," but the repo's existing posture is strictly
better (DPAPI ceremony, hidden input, machine+user-bound blob), so the
runbook uses that and says why.

**Next:** the ceremony itself — LA present: allowlist population + gov-pf-007
/gov-adj-008 reviewed baseline refresh + DECISION_REGISTER row (one branch),
key provisioning at the keyboard, flag flip, boot, one benign query on
explicit GO, the seven-line full-chain verify, evidence to PERFORMANCE_LOG +
journal + #719. Before or at the ceremony: the v0→v1 endpoint decision, and
the shared door-adjudicator-slot interaction with the UC-003 text go-live
(whichever runs second must re-verify both postures).

**Proposed lesson:** when a build must stop exactly one act short of live,
enumerate the independent locks at merge-time and design the release as ONE
reviewed governance act that a tripwire test forces into review — "dormant"
is then a provable property (tests count zero door calls), not a promise.

*(commits `<this>` (the Part B build: key loader + wrapped-key never-logged
posture, door `authorization` extension, LiveKagiAdapter + runner, conditional
registration + D4 + gov-adj-008 re-baseline, ceremony runbook); standing gate
+ eval gate green — numbers in the commit message; the first live GET awaits
the LA ceremony.)*

### 2026-07-02 — The endpoint the docs swore was fine returned 401

The web_search go-live ceremony reached the moment it was built for: PA
adjudication approved, the egress allowlist widened to `kagi.com` for exactly
one fetch, the `guarded_fetch` door opened, and — for the first time in the
project's life — a BlarAI runtime socket left the box under governance. The
whole chain worked. And Kagi answered `HTTP 401 Unauthorized`.

Nothing in our controls was wrong. The wrong thing was a fact I had inherited
and never re-verified against the live service. The `LiveKagiAdapter` targeted
Kagi's `GET /api/v0/search` with an `Authorization: Bot <key>` header and the
query in a URL parameter — the shape ADR-024, the W2 parsers, and the
`help.kagi.com` docs all described when this was built dormant. The runbook
even carried an explicit "ENDPOINT VERSION CHECK" step flagging v0-vs-v1 as an
open decision. We had chosen v0 because it was the documented, proven-shape
endpoint. It is now simply deprecated: it 401s.

The clue was one line in the Kagi MCP README — a `Bearer` token, not `Bot`.
Probing the live API with the operator's real key (no BlarAI code in the loop)
proved the current contract in one shot: `POST https://kagi.com/api/v1/search`,
header `Authorization: Bearer <key>`, JSON request body `{"query": "<text>"}`
— HTTP 200 with 43 results. And the response shape had moved too: v0's flat
`data` array of `{t, rank, url, title, snippet}` became v1's
`{"data": {"search": [ {title, url, snippet, time, props}, ... ], "infobox":
[...]}}`. The results now live under `data.search`, carry no `t`/`rank`, and
wrap matched query terms in `<strong>` tags.

So the fix was a four-axis correction that had to move as one unit — `v0 -> v1`,
`GET -> POST`, `Bot -> Bearer`, and `query-param -> JSON-body` — plus a new
v1-shaped parser (`_parse_v1_search`, local to the adapter, since `data.search`
objects have no `t`/`rank` and the W2 `parse_kagi_search_response` is a v0
parser). Kagi's array order *is* the rank now, so the parser preserves order
and enumerates it; title/snippet are tag-stripped to plain text deterministically
before they ground as UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL. The `Bot -> Bearer` change went into
`KagiApiKey.authorization_header_value` (via the `_AUTH_SCHEME` constant), not the
adapter — the wrapper still owns header construction, so the bare key never leaves
`kagi_key_loader`; the adapter only forwards the opaque string the door forwards
onward.

The load-bearing judgment was at the door. `guarded_fetch.fetch_external` is
the ONE sanctioned egress path and a frozen contract (#577) — the whole
SSRF-guard -> PA-adjudication -> allowlist-widen/revoke -> injection-scan
pipeline is the reason the air-gap-removal decision was safe. v1 needs POST
with a body, which the door did not do. The alternative was a second HTTP
client for the search path — and that would have broken the one invariant the
egress story rests on: exactly one network client, exactly one door. So I
extended the door instead, additively: two keyword-only params (`method="GET"`,
`json_body=None`) threaded through `_fetch_raw` / `_fetch_body` /
`fetch_external`, defaulting so every pre-existing GET caller (the UC-003 text
door, the image door) is byte-identical on the wire — same method, no request
body, no new header. POST changes exactly one line, the `client.stream` call,
and the streaming byte cap still bounds a POST response so a hostile reply
cannot exhaust memory. I accepted a slightly wider frozen contract to keep the
one-network-client invariant whole; a second client would have been the easier
diff and the worse architecture.

The endpoint move is a governed surface, so the D4 CAR resource
(`KAGI_SEARCH_ENDPOINT`) and the two golden eval cases that pin it —
`gov-pf-007` (the go-live tripwire, resource `https://kagi.com/api/v0/search`
-> `.../v1/search`) and `gov-adj-008` (whose resource is that same constant,
read at runtime) — re-baselined together, named old->new in the commit and
here. The egress allowlist is host-based (`kagi.com`), so the path change does
not touch it and both governance verdicts are identical to before; a coupling
test binds the constant to `gov-pf-007` so they can never fork silently. No
live network call was made from test code — the door is mocked at its seam
throughout.

**Next:** relaunch with the corrected adapter and let the ceremony's first live
`/ingest`-style web_search fetch complete against `/api/v1/search` — the 200 the
401 was standing in front of — then re-weld the three locks per the runbook.

**Proposed lesson:** *Re-verify an external contract against the live service
before you depend on it, not against the docs.* The v0 endpoint was documented,
proven-shape, and coupling-tested — and dead. Every axis of an external API
(URL, method, auth scheme, body shape, response shape) is a fact that can rot
between "built dormant" and "go live," and only a live probe with the real
credential settles it. The controls around the call were all correct; the
inherited fact about the call was the whole defect.

### 2026-07-02 — The answer the door let through and the validator swallowed

The `web_search` go-live ceremony did exactly what a go-live is supposed to do: it
proved the whole chain end to end, on real hardware, with a real question. The model
reached for `web_search` unprompted, the deterministic egress allowlist released
`kagi.com`, the Kagi endpoint answered **HTTP 200**, and the AO composed a faithful
answer with the price the operator had asked for sitting right there in it. Every
lock we had welded shut for weeks opened in the right order, once, for one GET. And
then PGOV Stage-5 held the answer on the way out: `PGOV DENIED — Leakage score 0.930
>= threshold 0.85`. The operator never saw the price. The feature we had just proven
worked had, in the same breath, proven it could not deliver an answer.

The failure is worth keeping because it is a collision of two decisions that are each
individually correct — the same shape as the knowledge-bank "write-only" bug
Amendment 2 fixed a fortnight earlier, one tier further out. Decision one: web content
stays untrusted (lesson 13, *provenance is not trust*) — an injected instruction in a
search result must never gain trusted standing or fire a tool, so `web_search` results
grounded as `UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL`, which is the tier fed to the Stage-5 cosine leakage
detector. Decision two: don't echo untrusted content — an answer whose cosine
similarity to an untrusted grounded chunk clears 0.85 is the exfiltration signature the
control exists to catch. Both right. But a faithful answer relaying a search result is,
by construction, \~verbatim to that result — that is what 0.930 measured. The control was
not wrong about the similarity; it was wrong about the *direction*. Exfiltration is
content leaving to an untrusted destination. Here the content was flowing *to the
operator who asked for it*, and the search results were already public. Relaying public
results back to the requester is the entire point of a search feature, not a leak.

So the fix is Amendment 2's mechanism aimed at a new source: a fifth provenance tier,
`UNTRUSTED_WEB`, that stays untrusted for everything that matters — the Layer-3
action-lock still trips (an injected search result still cannot fire a subsequent tool),
the datamarking and delimiter-wrapping still apply, the #570 per-dispatch deny still runs
— and is exempt from the Stage-5 leakage feed *only*. Mechanically it cost no predicate
change at all: `get_untrusted_chunk_texts` already filters on `== UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL`
(an equality, deliberately, from the #664 work), so the new tier falls out of the leak
feed for free, while `has_untrusted_content`'s `not-in-trusted` test traps it for free.
The whole behavioural delta is one line — `web_search` grounds as `untrusted_web` instead
of `untrusted_external` in `_TOOL_RESULT_PROVENANCE`.

The trade-off I want on the record is the one I *didn't* take. It was tempting to reuse
`UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE` — its gating is byte-identical to what web results need today
(leak-exempt, everything-else-untrusted), so a fifth enum member looks like ceremony.
I kept them distinct anyway, for the same audit-legibility reason §2.1 keeps
`TRUSTED_LOCAL` and `TRUSTED_MEMORY` apart: web results are transient public lookups the
model requested this turn, not the operator's curated bank. A future per-source policy —
a retention rule, a "web results expire at turn end" rule, a different injection-scan
aggressiveness — will need to tell them apart, and the audit trail must record *which*
untrusted-but-leak-exempt source grounded a turn. Overloading one tier to mean both
would erase that forever to save one line of enum. The equally-tempting shortcut —
broaden the `get_untrusted_chunk_texts` filter to exempt all `UNTRUSTED_EXTERNAL` — I
rejected harder, because it silently drops Stage-5 screening for `/external` pasted
content too, and that content is exactly what the control must still catch: the operator
declared it as external and did *not* request it as a public lookup, so echoing it
verbatim is still the exfiltration signature. The carve-out has to be web-search-specific,
and a regression test now pins that `/external` stays in the feed while `web_search` does
not.

One numbering wrinkle: the DECISION_REGISTER had earmarked "Am.3 pending" for #723 (the
D5 consent-grain rework), which is not yet on disk. This carve-out landed first, so it
takes Amendment 3 and #723 renumbers to Amendment 4 when it lands — noted in the ADR
header and the register so the next integrator isn't surprised.

**Next:** relaunch, ask the price question again, and watch the answer stay on the screen
this time — the door was never the problem; the validator was, and now it knows the
difference between a leak and a relay.

**Proposed lesson:** *The output validator has a direction, not just a similarity.* A
verbatim-echo control keyed on cosine alone cannot tell "leaked what you didn't ask for"
from "answered with the public thing you did ask for" — it only sees that two strings
match. Twice now (curated knowledge recall, web-search relay) the honest, requested
behaviour has been \~verbatim to a grounded untrusted source and been held as a leak. The
durable fix is never a threshold tweak (a faithful relay is cosine → 1.0; no threshold
separates it from an echo); it is provenance — carve the *intended-to-be-relayed* sources
out of the leak feed by an explicit, auditable tier while keeping them untrusted for the
action-lock, and keep the genuinely-external, not-requested source (`/external`) screened.

### 2026-07-02 — The lock I lifted by naming what it was actually guarding

The `web_search` go-live earlier tonight only worked with the knowledge
bank switched off. That was the tell. The Layer-3 action-lock — the
control that refuses tools when a session holds untrusted content — was
doing its job so indiscriminately that it locked the operator's own
knowledge reads against the operator's own store, and the only way past
it was a per-session `/trust` typed before the assistant had done
anything the operator could see. A consent gesture given blind, every
session, to make friction stop, is not consent. It is a rubber-stamp
wearing a control's clothes. #723 is the rework that routes each of the
three tool classes the blanket covered to a grain the human can actually
judge; this fragment is rung 1, the smallest and least glamorous of the
three, shipped first on purpose because it un-breaks a daily friction
with almost no blast radius.

The interesting call in rung 1 was not *whether* to stop locking
`search_knowledge` — the LA had decided that — but *how to say why*. The
inherited framing was "`UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE` provenance no longer trips
the lock for this tool." The LA pushed back on that, and he was right: a
provenance-keyed relaxation is ambiguous the moment a session holds
*mixed* untrusted content — a knowledge recall and a pasted `/external`
document both present, two provenances live at once, and the rule has to
arbitrate which one wins. The honest axis is not the content's
provenance at all. It is the *tool's* bounded danger. `search_knowledge`
is a redirectable read over the operator's own local store: an injection
can at most steer which record is read, the result grounds as
`UNTRUSTED_KNOWLEDGE` (still action-locked, still datamarked), and it
performs no egress and no mutation — so it cannot exfiltrate or fire an
action no matter what else shares the session. The exemption belongs on
the tool, keyed on that property, unambiguous under any provenance mix.
So the mechanism is a one-entry allowlist — `_LOCK_EXEMPT_TOOLS =
{"search_knowledge"}` behind `is_lock_exempt()` — fail-closed (unlisted
means locked), with an invariant test that every exempt tool is GUARDED
and never DANGEROUS, and one clause added to the single Layer-3 gate. The
#570 Policy-Agent adjudication sits downstream of that gate and still
runs on every `search_knowledge` dispatch; the exemption lifts the lock
and nothing else. I went with the per-tool bounded-danger framing over
the per-provenance one because it is the version that stays correct when
a session gets messy — accepting that it means maintaining an explicit
allowlist rather than a blanket predicate, which is exactly the fail-
closed shape I want here.

The trade-off worth keeping on the record: rung 1's payoff is smaller
than the handoff I inherited claimed. It un-locks `search_knowledge`,
not `web_search`. Those are different tools; a knowledge-bearing session
still locks `web_search` by its untrusted content until rung 3 replaces
`/trust` for egress with a turn-scoped Windows-Hello envelope. The
ceremony's real pain — web search usable with the knowledge bank *on* —
is a rung-3 deliverable, and rung 3 also fixes a nastier thing rung 1
does not touch: once a session hits the refusal, the model imitates its
own prior refusal lines and keeps declining even after `/trust` clears
the lock (#726 c.1310). Removing the lock *trigger* for egress is what
stops those refusals being generated in the first place. I wrote that
distinction into ADR-023 Amendment 4 plainly, because a journal that
inherits an overclaim and passes it on is worth less than one that
corrects it.

Two verdicts changed in the governance eval golden set, which makes this
a reviewed re-baseline, not a silent drift: `gov-l3-008`
(`search_knowledge` locked under untrusted content) flips `true →
false`, and `gov-l3-009` keeps its `false` but for a new reason (exempt,
not `/trust`-overridden) so its description was rewritten; `gov-l3-012`
is a new boundary case pinning that `generate_image` still locks under
the identical posture. The eval suite carries its own reimplementation
of the Layer-3 predicate (`layer3_lock_decision`) that has to move in
lockstep with the real gate — a place two-copies-of-one-rule could drift
— so that mirror got the same clause and the harness re-derives the
count clean at 42/42.

**Next:** rung 3 is the marquee and the hard one — the Hello verifier is
synchronous and system-modal, so firing it mid-tool-loop blocks the AO
turn on a WinRT dialog, and the design has to answer whether streaming
stalls, how the WinUI surfaces the prompt during an in-flight
generation, and the fail-closed timeout on the streaming path. That is
where the design budget goes. Rung 2 (per-batch image approval, mostly
WinUI) trails. Present the rung-3 concurrency design to the LA for his
nod before building it.

**Proposed lesson:** *Consent grain is set by judgeability, and
judgeability lives on the action, not the content.* The blanket
`/trust` failed not because it was too strict but because it asked the
human to authorize a category on information he did not have — an
unjudgeable request degrades to a rubber-stamp. The fix was to exempt a
tool on the ground that *its own danger is bounded* (a non-exfiltratable
local read), not on a property of whatever content happened to be in the
session — because a content-keyed rule goes ambiguous under mixed input
while an action-keyed one stays honest. Route danger to deterministic
controls; ask the human only what he can actually evaluate, at the
coarsest grain that is still meaningful.

*(commit `<this>` (rung 1: `_LOCK_EXEMPT_TOOLS`/`is_lock_exempt`, the
Layer-3 gate exemption, the `layer3_lock_decision` eval mirror, golden
`gov-l3-008/009/012`, `test_tools.py` unit + gate + boundary tests, ADR-
023 Amendment 4, the DECISION_REGISTER row); governance eval 42/42;
standing gate re-run pending at fragment write-time; rungs 2-3 decided in
Am.4, pending their own merges.)*

### 2026-07-02 — A fingerprint on the way out: the egress envelope, and the lock I forgot to remove

Rung 3 is the one the whole `web_search` go-live had been quietly waiting
for. Earlier tonight the ceremony proved the full egress chain live — the
model chose to search, Kagi answered, a governed answer came back — but it
only worked with the **knowledge bank switched off**. The reason was the
Layer-3 action-lock: once a session holds untrusted content (and a
knowledge-bearing session almost always does), every GUARDED tool locks
behind a per-session `/trust`, and `web_search` is GUARDED. So in daily
use, with the bank on, the operator would have to `/trust` every session
before he could search the web — the exact unjudgeable rubber-stamp rung 1
diagnosed, now standing directly in front of the marquee feature.

The decided shape (the LA set it across three #719 comments) is a
**turn-scoped Windows-Hello envelope**: the first outbound query of a turn
raises a fingerprint showing that exact query under an explicit bound
("up to N searches for this question"); one touch covers up to N searches;
each subsequent query is disclosed live in the chat as it leaves rather
than re-prompted; exceeding N re-fingerprints. N=3 by the LA's dial (which
also happens to equal the tool-loop cap, so it is effectively one
fingerprint per turn while every query is still shown). The posture he
affirmed is *nothing leaves the machine without a fingerprint on the
specific query* — and because agentic search is sequential, the later
queries do not exist at the moment the first dialog appears, so an
envelope-plus-live-disclosure is the faithful implementation of that
posture rather than a compromise of it.

The concurrency worry the LA flagged up front turned out to be the easy
part, once I read the actual streaming path instead of assuming. The
Windows-Hello verifier is synchronous and system-modal — `verify()`
blocks — so firing it mid-tool-loop pauses the turn on a WinRT dialog. But
the tool-call tokens (`<tool_call>…`) are *suppressed* from the user stream
by a tested invariant, and the generation that decides to search completes
before any gate runs. So the fingerprint fires in the gap between "the
model finished deciding to search" and "the search actually goes out" —
there is no half-drawn answer on screen to freeze. I reused the exact
fail-closed harness the PA-ESCALATE path already uses (worker thread +
bounded join, the same registered verifier), gave the envelope its own
turn-scoped state on top, and made the Hello dialog wording source-aware so
an egress touch reads "allow this outbound web search?" rather than "approve
escalated action?". One operator surface, one fail-closed harness, two
consumers.

The part worth keeping honestly in the record is the thing I *missed* on
the first pass, and what caught it. I built the envelope, wired the gate,
wrote the tests — and a single integration test, "two searches in one turn
share one fingerprint," failed. Not because the envelope was wrong, but
because after the first `web_search` grounded its `UNTRUSTED_WEB` result,
the **second** `web_search` hit the Layer-3 lock and never reached the
envelope at all. I had implemented the fingerprint and forgotten to remove
the lock it was replacing. The whole marquee value — web_search usable with
the knowledge bank on — depends on egress tools being *exempt* from the
untrusted-content Layer-3 lock, with the envelope as their sole consent.
That is also precisely what kills the #726 chat-poisoning at its source: no
lock trigger means no refusal line generated means nothing for the model to
imitate. I had written all of that into the ADR as the intent and then
under-built it; the test that encoded the real desired behaviour is what
made the gap undeniable. I added `is_egress_tool` to the lock condition
next to rung-1's `is_lock_exempt`, and — the important half — kept the
egress tool's own `UNTRUSTED_WEB` result tripping `has_untrusted_content`,
so a *non-egress* GUARDED tool (generate_image) called afterward still
locks. Web content can steer words, never a local action; that line is
regression-locked in two places now.

One smaller refinement fell out of another failing test, the disabled-tool
case: a dormant `web_search` with no runner registered egresses nothing —
it returns a deterministic "unavailable" notice — so it must not raise a
fingerprint for a search that will not happen. `egress_tool_active` gates
the prompt on the tool actually being live, consistent with the existing
rule that a deterministic notice takes the plain path and never locks.

Two `test_retrieval_tools.py` tests were removed as genuinely obsolete: they
asserted the pre-Amendment-4 `/trust`-lock matrix for the retrieval tools,
and after this work neither retrieval tool is `/trust`-locked —
search_knowledge is bounded-danger exempt (rung 1), web_search is
egress-exempt and fingerprint-governed (rung 3). The correct new behaviour
is covered by the envelope's own integration tests; deleting tests that
encode a behaviour you deliberately removed is honest, not a coverage loss,
as long as the replacement coverage is real — and it is (19 envelope unit
tests, 8 loop-integration tests, a new `egress_consent` governance-eval
kind with five committed golden cases proving the fail-closed default, the
N-window, the exceed-N re-fingerprint, and the deny-latches-the-turn
property).

Accepted trade-offs, on the record: friction scales with searches per turn;
no unattended egress until a future scoped pre-authorization; and one named
residual — an in-envelope follow-up query could be steered by injected
search-result content, mitigated by the live per-query disclosure, the turn
cap, and the deterministic kagi.com-only allowlist. The relax path if it
bites is per-session grain, which is looser and therefore a one-line change
— easier to relax than to tighten, which is the right direction to leave a
security default pointing.

**Next:** live-verify on the box is the real proof — relaunch, open a
knowledge-bearing session, ask something that needs the web, and confirm a
single fingerprint prompt shows the real query and the answer comes back
with no `/trust` anywhere. Then rung 2 (the per-generation-batch image
approval) is the last of the three, mostly a WinUI approval round-trip.

**Proposed lesson:** *When you replace a control, delete the old one in the
same breath — and let the test that encodes the real goal tell you whether
you did.* The envelope was correct in isolation and useless in practice
because the lock it was meant to supersede was still standing in front of
it. The behaviour-level integration test ("two searches share one
fingerprint") caught what every unit test passed clean, because only the
end-to-end test knew what the feature was actually *for*. Write the test
that asserts the outcome the feature exists to deliver, not just the
mechanism you built.

*(commit `<this>` (rung 3: `egress_envelope.py`; `tools._EGRESS_TOOLS`/
`is_egress_tool`/`egress_tool_active`; the entrypoint Layer-3 egress-exemption
+ tool-loop fingerprint gate + live disclosure + fail-closed help; `[egress]`
config; source-aware Hello wording; the `is_egress_tool` eval-mirror + the
`egress_consent` eval kind; golden `gov-l3-010` flip + `gov-egr-001..005`;
`test_egress_envelope.py` + `TestEgressEnvelopeLoop` + coupling + hello_verifier
tests; two obsolete retrieval /trust-lock tests removed); ADR-023 Amendment 4
rung 3 marked DONE + DECISION_REGISTER updated; governance eval 47/47; standing
