The root cause was ugly in an instructive way: the New-Project "web" scaffold profile unconditionally seeded a `node:http` server plus a `public/app.js` that fetched `/api/health`, even when the goal explicitly excluded a server. Opened as `file://` there is no backend, so the box never resolved. The fleet had built a modern-web-app shape for someone who asked for a single file.

The fix had to live in two repos at once, and that is the first lesson. BlarAI's 14B refiner learned to narrow `surface: web → web-static` on explicit static/no-server signals (`_refine_web_static`, a refiner-only surface deliberately excluded from `_MODEL_SURFACE_VALUES` so the model never emits it directly). But that half, merged alone (`438f95e0`), fixes nothing a user can see — it just relabels a surface the agentic-setup fleet doesn't recognize yet. The fleet half (`c5b24be`) is what makes it real: a `web-static` build-profile arm that seeds a single self-contained `index.html` (inline style, inline SVG, inline plain-DOM script, a `class="placeholder"` marker and an "EXTEND THIS FILE" banner so a no-op coder leaves visibly-provisional output) and NOTHING else — no `package.json`, no `server.js`, no fetch. The two halves are each individually safe (the BlarAI half is an inert relabel; the fleet arm is dormant until something emits `web-static`), which is exactly why the danger is subtle: a green gate on either repo says nothing about whether the user-visible bug is actually gone. I held #886 open until both mains carried the fix, and wrote the ticket to say so in plain language, because "merged" on one side would have read as "done" when it was "no-op."

The second lesson came from the reviewer, not the build. #888 (the sibling: the plan card had been false-warning "I couldn't tell this project's language" on New-Project dispatches) leaned on a helper, `repo_will_scaffold`, meant to mirror the fleet's real `$hasProj` gate — "does this repo already have a project marker, so the fleet will skip seeding?" The helper did a shallow glob (repo root plus one level). The actual `$hasProj` in `new-agent-task.ps1:153` is `Get-ChildItem -Recurse` — all depths. Nobody had noticed they diverged, because they only disagree on an odd shape: a marker buried two-plus directories deep with no root manifest (a monorepo `packages/app/package.json`). There, the shallow check said "will scaffold → checks will run" while the fleet actually skips the scaffold — the card over-claiming coverage for work that never happens. One line, `glob` → `rglob`, made the mirror match its original exactly, and the regression test now builds the deep-monorepo shape and asserts the honest answer. The general shape is worth keeping: when one function exists only to mirror another across a language boundary, nothing keeps them in step except a test that pins the divergent case, and the docstring that claims "mirrors X" is the first thing to lie when X changes.

The third lesson was about proving it. It would have been easy to close #886 on the unit seam — the real `Resolve-TaskScaffold` and `Copy-ScaffoldInto` are driven by `verify-scaffold.ps1`, which now asserts a `web-static` surface seeds `index.html` and NOT `server.js`/`app.js`/`package.json`, plus a `[kill]` test proving the profile arm is load-bearing (strip it and the same static ask falls through the keyword heuristic back to `web`). But the LA chose the real ceremony, and it paid off beyond confidence: I drove a live static New-Project dispatch headlessly through the real `DispatchHarness` → the running Assistant Orchestrator on `:5001` → the real fleet. The live 14B classified the static ask as `surface: web-static`; the plan card said "Building this as: a single-page web page (opens straight in a browser)" instead of the old "modern web application" framing; and 42 seconds after approve, the seeded worktree held exactly `.gitignore`, `README.md`, `index.html` — no server, no fetch, no package.json. That end-to-end run is the only thing that proves the surface actually *threads* through swap → per-run queue JSON → run-fleet → new-agent-task → the scaffold picker; every link was individually tested, but "individually tested" is precisely the state that hides a broken seam.

A reviewer nit (N1) turned into a fourth, smaller lesson about how to decide whether a nit blocks. `add-fleet-task.ps1`'s `-Surface` ValidateSet omitted `web-static`, so a human typing `add-fleet-task -Surface web-static` would hit a parameter-binding error. Whether that mattered depended entirely on which code path is live: I traced it and found the automated path writes the per-run queue JSON directly (`swap_ops` `json.dumps`, no ValidateSet) and forwards through `new-agent-task.ps1 -Surface` (also no ValidateSet) — the ValidateSet only ever gates a manual CLI no automated path uses. So N1 was cosmetic, not a blocker — but "cosmetic" was a conclusion I had to earn by reading the live path, not assume. I folded the one-line fix in anyway (mature-not-minimal), with its own W11b assertion.

One operational note for the next session, honestly: stopping the ceremony's dispatch mid-swap left a stranded 14B↔30B swap and orphaned fleet processes; the reap-then-relaunch reconcile path (`shared/fleet/swap_state.py`) converged it cleanly on the next boot ("Swap recovery: run … resumable"), exactly as designed. And bringing the app up with `--winui` popped the desktop window when a harness-driven dispatch needs only the backend — the launcher has no backend-only mode today, so I filed one (#893). The workshop is still rougher than it should be for autonomous operation.

Trade-off named: the rejected path was closing #886 on the unit seam alone and calling it done. It would have been defensible and faster, and it would have missed nothing *this* time — but the whole point of the cross-repo shape is that unit-green on both sides is the failure mode, so the live thread was worth the swap.

**Next:** #697 (the node/jest per-task oracle) lands on the same `acceptance.py` (a clean 3-way merge, no conflict after all), then the Wave-3 cleanups (#704 first). **Lesson 272 and a tally on lesson 149:** (a) a cross-repo fix whose halves are each a safe no-op needs one ticket that stays open until BOTH mains carry it — unit-green per repo is the trap (lesson 272); (b) a function that mirrors another across a language boundary drifts silently — pin the divergent case with a test and treat the "mirrors X" docstring as the first casualty when X changes (tallied on lesson 149).

### 2026-07-16 — The convention that had to describe the mess, not an ideal

*Plain summary: I wrote the doc-lifecycle convention and its frontmatter lint for #267 — and the honest, load-bearing decision was to make the lint advisory-only, because zero documents in the tree actually carry the frontmatter yet.*

The additive half of #267 (doc-sprawl consolidation) was mine: a lifecycle convention plus a frontmatter lint, the two parallel-safe slices the research decomposition (c.1989) carved out ahead of the serialized archive-move batches. The temptation with a "lifecycle convention" is to write the aspirational version — the clean four-field frontmatter every doc *should* carry — and wire a lint into the gate to enforce it. I grounded first instead, and the grounding killed that plan: a grep for `status:` frontmatter across all of `docs/` returned zero. The `status:` hits that exist are ADR body-fields (`**Status:** ACCEPTED`) and a template's example block, not YAML frontmatter. So the true current convention is *there is no frontmatter convention* — the lifecycle is signalled by ADR Status lines, `DRAFT_` filename prefixes, the `docs/archive/` directory, and frozen-record prose. The convention I wrote had to name those existing signals and generalize them, not pretend a greenfield.

That reality forced the lint's posture: it exits non-zero on violations so it *can* be gated later, but it is deliberately not in the standing gate, because gating today would fail all 88 top-level docs. I verified that honestly rather than hiding it — the live run reports `88 scanned, 0 compliant`. The convention doc itself is the one compliant file (it dogfoods its own frontmatter). Wiring the gate waits for the living-set back-fill, which is a later serial pass.

Two smaller judgments. First, placement: the team-lead brief said `docs/doc-lifecycle.md`, but the ticket's own acceptance criteria and the research decomposition both say `docs/governance/doc-lifecycle.md` — and a convention whose whole purpose is to keep the top level small should not itself land at the top level. I put it under `docs/governance/` and linked it from the governance README; the literal top-level path was the rejected option. Second, the two ticket vocabularies (`living/reference/archived` from the brief, `active/superseded/archived/draft` from the ticket) had to be reconciled, not picked between — I merged them into one five-state set (`living | reference | draft | superseded | archived`) with `active` accepted as an alias for `living`, so neither prior framing breaks.

The lint defaults to a non-recursive top-level scan so it never wanders into the ~19,200 gitignored `node_modules` files under `docs/security/**/_validate/` that balloon any recursive `docs/` walk ~20x; the recursive mode is opt-in and skips those dirs (unit-tested with a synthetic `node_modules`, since a fresh worktree checks out only tracked files and so has none to trip over live).

**Field note (recorded in `FIELD_NOTES.md`):** `git worktree add` populates only tracked files — a fresh worktree has none of the gitignored `node_modules`/model trees the primary checkout carries, so "it didn't crash on node_modules in the worktree" is not evidence the skip logic works; prove that class of guard with a synthetic fixture in a unit test, not a live worktree run.

**Next:** the serialized archive-move batches (#267 #3–#7) remain — SERIAL, quiet-tree, one commit each — and a later living-set frontmatter back-fill graduates the lint from advisory to gate-eligible.

### 2026-07-16 — A child fixing its own exit code doesn't protect the parent reading it

*Plain summary: the battery's nightly preflight calls a separate script to check whether the scheduled task's settings have drifted, and branches on that child's exit code — but on a differently-configured Windows host the child's failure would throw before the parent could read it, mislabeling a real drift as "the check errored"; the fix scopes one PowerShell preference around just that call.*

The #833 conformance check (built earlier this arc) made the battery's task-settings drift *detectable* — it exits non-zero and names each drift. What #903 caught is a seam one level up: the nightly launcher's preflight *reads* that exit code, and it read it under whatever `$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference` the host happens to set. On this box that preference is `$false` (PowerShell 7.6.3), so a native command's non-zero exit is just an exit code the parent inspects — everything works. But on a host where that preference is `$true`, the child's non-zero exit becomes a *terminating error* that unwinds into the launcher's generic catch, and a genuine drift (the exact thing #833 exists to surface) gets relabeled "the check errored" — a real alarm degraded into a shrug. The transferable lesson is the one the builder named: a child fixing its exit-code contract *internally* does nothing for a parent that reads that contract under its own host assumptions. #833 hardened the child; the parent's reading of the child was still host-dependent, and only fixing it at the parent closes the gap. Robustness at a boundary is a property of both sides of it, separately.

The fix is deliberately narrow: scope `$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false` around *just* the `& pwsh -File verify-battery-task-settings.ps1` call, save-and-restore in a `finally`, capture the exit explicitly, branch on that. The launcher's other native calls (git, python, the runner) keep the box default — this isn't a blanket preference flip, which would have been the tempting broad-brush move and would have silently changed error semantics for every native call in the script. The rejected alternative worth recording is "map the caught throw to a drift verdict" instead: that sounds equivalent but is worse, because it would raise a *false* drift alarm whenever the child is merely missing or uninvokable, converting one honest failure mode ("errored") into a misleading one ("drifted"). Keeping "errored" on the genuinely-errored path and "drifted" on the genuinely-drifted path is the honest taxonomy. The change ships behavior-preserving on this box and corrects only the label on a hostile host; its regression lock (`verify-battery-native-error-scoping.ps1`, 27/27) drives the live preflight block both ways plus a vacuity control that proves the hostile-host simulation genuinely throws — so the lock can't pass by failing to reach the condition.

**Next:** wire `verify-battery-native-error-scoping.ps1` into whatever offline-locks sweep runs its `verify-battery-*.ps1` siblings, so the parent-side contract is walked alongside the pass-banking-freeze and unregister-scoping locks.

### 2026-07-16 — A fix on one path is not a fix on the parallel path

*Plain summary: the certificate-cache fix that kept the coder connected (#906) was written on the host-to-host connection path and quietly left the guest-VM connection path a step behind; #907 gives the guest path the same one-shot recovery, so neither topology can silently keep the weaker behavior.*

#906 closed the stale-cert-cache stall on the host-loopback connect, and it did two things: keyed the cached client context to the on-disk cert generation, and added a bounded one-shot retry when a connect fails verification right after a re-mint. The first half — the generation fingerprint — lives in the shared `_client_ssl_context()`, so the guest-mode AF_HYPERV path (the topology BlarAI uses when the Assistant Orchestrator runs inside the isolation VM, #615) inherited it for free. The second half — the retry — lived only in `_connect_host_loopback_mtls`, because that's the path the battery exercises and where the failure was observed. The review of #906 flagged this as a nit and I filed it as #907 rather than folding it, which is exactly the "stop early and leave residual" habit worth not repeating: a security-relevant recovery that exists on one of two parallel connect paths is a latent inconsistency, not a closed issue. If host-mode is ever swapped for guest-mode as the default (it's a config choice, read at boot), the weaker path silently becomes the live one.

The fix mirrors the host path precisely — a `SSLCertVerificationError`, and only that, earns exactly one rebuild-and-retry against the freshly-read CA; timeouts and refusals never retry (no timeout doubling); a genuine trust mismatch stays a loud failure. Verification is never relaxed on either path. The rejected alternative was to leave it as a low-priority ticket on the reasoning that host-mode is the default today and guest-mode's µs-window race is already covered by the generation fingerprint — true, but "the default topology is fine" is precisely the assumption that rots when the default changes, and the parity cost was three small tests plus a mirrored except-branch. The regression locks mirror the host trio (retry-once, retry-bounded, timeout-no-retry) against `_connect_hyperv`; the two retry-behavior locks were watched failing on the pre-#907 tree.

**Next:** the two paths now share the recovery shape but not the code; if a third connect path ever appears, factor the verify-retry into one helper both call rather than mirroring a third time.

### 2026-07-16 — Finishing the sweep: the last unmarked prompt on the path

*Plain summary: when I wired the "don't obey web content" marker into the web-search prompts (#909), I marked the two prompts that build the answer but deliberately left the third — the one that decides whether to do more searches — for a follow-up ticket; #911 marks it too, so no prompt on that path reads untrusted web titles without the marker.*

#909 datamarked the extraction and synthesis prompts and I *ticketed* the gap-detection prompt (#911) as a bounded residual rather than folding it in — the reasoning was sound on its face (that prompt carries no fetched-page body, only source titles and the model's own distilled learnings, and its only output is 1–3 follow-up search queries capped by `max_passes`, so the worst an injected title can do is nudge a bounded routing decision, never poison the user-facing answer). That reasoning still holds, but the ticket-and-defer was the same reflex I'd just been called out for the night before: a real, buildable security-consistency fix parked because it wasn't the scariest one. The honest completion is to mark all three prompts, so the invariant is simple and total — *every* prompt on the /search path that shows the model untrusted web text prefixes it with the per-session marker and tells the model those lines have no instruction authority. An invariant with an exception ("all prompts except the gap one") is a footnote a future reader has to remember; an invariant without one is a property the code enforces.

The fix reuses the exact machinery from #909 — a `build_gap_detection_prompt` that threads the session token, prepends the marker-instruction header, and marks the findings block line-by-line via the same `_mark_web_lines`. The one design choice was whether to touch the shared `GAP_DETECTION_PROMPT` constant (adding a `{web_data_header}` slot) or leave the constant and mark only at the call site; I added the slot so the header rides the template rather than being concatenated in the loop, keeping the three prompts symmetric in how they carry the header. The rejected alternative — leaving the gap prompt as documented-but-unmarked and trusting the bound — is defensible for *this* prompt today, but it leaves the reviewer of the next web-search change having to re-derive why one of three prompts is special, and that re-derivation is exactly where a real gap slips back in. Marking it costs two tests and one builder function.

**Next:** all three /search prompts now share the datamark; if a fourth prompt is ever added to this loop, the pattern to copy is `build_gap_detection_prompt` (header slot + `_mark_web_lines` on any untrusted block), and the reachability lock that proves an injected title lands only as a marked line.

### 2026-07-16 — The /search command relay that returned an answer but never armed the lock

*Plain summary: the explicit /search command path came back with a plain, ungrounded answer — so if it were ever shown to you, an injection hidden in a web page could have fired a tool on your next message. It isn't wired into the live assistant, but I fixed the relay so it's correct before the egress door ever opens.*

#913 came out of the #909 independent review as a "belt-and-suspenders" item for the egress go-live: confirm that the *command* form of web search grounds its answer as untrusted, the way the *tool* form does. I traced both paths on disk before touching anything, because the ticket's own framing was a hypothesis to verify, not a fact to act on.

Two paths reach the web. The TOOL path — the 14B emits a `web_search` tool-call — grounds its results `UNTRUSTED_WEB` through `add_grounded_context` in `entrypoint.py` (~6170). That single call is what flips `has_untrusted_content(session_id)` True, which is the exact signal the tool loop reads at `entrypoint.py:5980` to hold tools back on the *next* turn. So a prompt-injection that survives the datamark (#909) and the injection scan (#896) still can't fire a tool — the lock is armed by the grounded result sitting in the session. That path is fine, and its MUST-NOT-WEAKEN lock (`test_post_ceremony_success_grounds_untrusted_web`) proves it.

The COMMAND path is a different animal. `WebSearchSkill.handle` / `handle_search_command` run `run_web_search`, which is a *self-contained* agentic loop over a standalone `SearchState`. It fetches, scans, datamarks, and synthesises — and never once touches the operator's `ContextManager`. It hands back `state.final_answer` as a plain string. Nothing from that invocation is grounded into the operator session. So the ground truth: the command relay is **ungrounded**. If that string were relayed into your session as the assistant's reply, `has_untrusted_content` would stay False, and an injection that lived through both defenses could steer a tool on your next message — the precise hole the tool path was built to close.

The saving grace today is that the command path is wired into *nothing*. I grepped the whole tree: `handle_search_command` / `WebSearchSkill` have zero production importers — only the tests and the package export. The `register()` seam is documented "intentionally NOT called from any production code," and it wired the raw `handle` anyway. So the risk is dormant by structural absence, the strongest kind. But the brief was explicit — and the security-by-design posture agrees — that you don't get to defer a fail-open just because it's dormant. The next integrator who copies that seam inherits a foot-gun.

So I made the relay correct by construction rather than correct by future diligence. I added `handle_search_command_grounded` / `WebSearchSkill.handle_grounded`, which run the same loop and then ground the answer `UNTRUSTED_WEB` into the operator session *before* returning it — mirroring the tool path, and fail-closed: if `add_grounded_context` returns False or raises, the answer is **withheld** (a notice replaces it), never relayed ungrounded. The decision of *whether* to ground is structural, not string-sniffing: ground iff the loop actually extracted web learnings (`all_learnings` non-empty), which is exactly how the tool path skips its content-free `is_retrieval_notice` strings — so an empty question or a decomposition-produced-nothing notice never arms the lock spuriously. Then I rewired `register()` to *require* the `ContextManager` and wire the grounded handler, so copying the seam can no longer produce an ungrounded relay — there is no seam that relays without a grounding sink.

I deliberately did **not** wire `/search` into the live turn pipeline. That would be a new egress-adjacent capability — a posture flip that belongs to an LA-present ceremony, not a defect fix. This change keeps the path dormant while making the surface it would eventually use safe. I rejected two thinner fixes: a "no production import of the raw handler" gate test (it makes the future wiring safe by *breaking* when someone wires it, not by construction — fragile), and simply documenting the hazard and moving on (that's the defer-because-dormant anti-pattern the brief named).

The regression lock is `test_websearch_command_grounding.py`, driven through a REAL `ContextManager` (the seam, not a mock of it): the grounded relay arms `has_untrusted_content` + lands `UNTRUSTED_WEB` (principle-12 ON); the raw relay leaves the lock disarmed on the identical search (principle-12 OFF toggle — proving the grounding is the load-bearing control, and demonstrating the exact gap); grounding-fails-closed withholds the answer with no leak of the raw text; content-free paths never ground; and the `register()` seam wires the grounded handler and refuses to be called without a context. 9 new tests; the change ran 346 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped across the websearch package, the tool-path golive-wiring suite, `test_context_manager`, and `test_retrieval_tools`.

**Lesson 273:** *a plain string returned from a self-contained untrusted-content loop is a latent fail-open until its relay grounds it — make the relay correct-by-construction (require the grounding sink at the seam), never leave the grounding to the integrator who wires it later.* This is the mirror image of the "built-but-wired-into-nothing" family: here the control was built, the relay was structurally absent, and the danger was that the *eventual* wiring would be the ungrounded one.

**Next:** fold this into the egress go-live ceremony checklist alongside the live-Qwen3-14B marked-prompt quality check; if `/search` is ever promoted into the live turn pipeline, the integration MUST go through the grounded seam (`register(..., context=ao.context_manager)`), and the marked-prompt quality check should re-confirm the answer text still reads faithfully after grounding.

### 2026-07-16 — Extending an already-enforced signature gate to a lower-priority surface needs its own dormant twin, not the same switch

*Plain summary: signing the spec-decode draft models (#107) could not reuse the 14B's `require_signed_manifest` flag — that flag is already `true`, and the drafts aren't signed yet, so flipping the drafts onto it would have bricked boot. The fix was a separate, independently-flippable `require_signed_draft_manifest` that ships dormant.*

Vikunja #107 was the genuine remainder of FUT-05: the authoritative Qwen3-14B manifest has been TPM-signed and boot-verified since Sprint 17 (`require_signed_manifest = true` in both service configs), but the speculative-decoding draft manifests were still unsigned — a `manifest.json` with digests but no `.sig`/`.pub`. The LA had already made the one real judgment call the ticket needed: reuse the existing `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` key rather than mint a draft key, because the drafts are non-authoritative — in speculative decoding the draft only proposes tokens the signed 14B target re-verifies, so a tampered draft degrades speed, not output integrity. Integrity-not-authenticity closure; one key is enough.

The trap was in how the enforcement flag threads through. My first instinct was the tidy one: the 14B already has `require_signed_manifest`, so just route the draft verification through the same flag. That would have been a live boot-brick the moment it merged. `require_signed_manifest` is already `true` on this machine, and the drafts have no signatures, so a draft verification gated on that flag would fail closed at the next boot — before any ceremony could sign them. The 14B's flag being *already true* is exactly what makes it unusable as the draft's switch: there's no dormant window left in it. So I built a separate `[security].require_signed_draft_manifest`, default `false`, threaded through the launcher into `build_shared_pipeline(require_signed_draft=...)` and down to the draft's `verify_weight_integrity`. It ships dormant: the draft stays digest-verified exactly as today, and only after the (LA-present) ceremony writes the draft `.sig` files does flipping this second flag begin enforcing them. This also matches a pattern already in the tree — `[image_generation].require_signed_manifest` and `[coordinator].require_signed_policy` are each their own per-domain flag, not the security section's — so the drafts getting their own is the house style, not a special case.

I had to correct myself on the exact dormant-state guarantee, which is the part worth keeping honest. I first wrote the config comment claiming that even while dormant, a *present-but-invalid* draft signature would still fail closed (the "no silent downgrade" property the 14B has). That's true for the 14B because its per-service boot gate calls `load_manifest_verified`, which verifies any present `.sig` regardless of the flag. But the draft's only verification is `verify_weight_integrity`, and with `require_signed=False` that function takes the plain `load_manifest` branch and never consults the `.sig` at all. I considered making `verify_weight_integrity` always route through the signature-checked loader to give the draft parity — but that primitive is on the Policy Agent's per-request re-hash hot path (`adjudicator.py`), so changing its global semantics would add a TPM verify to every adjudication. Not worth it for a non-authoritative model. So I left the primitive alone and made the draft's posture the clean, defensible one: dormant = digest-only (a tampered draft *weight* is still caught by the digest), enforced = missing-or-invalid `.sig` fails closed. Then I rewrote the comments to say what the code actually does. The lesson: when you copy a security control's flag onto a new surface, verify the new surface actually flows through the same enforcement function — the guarantee lives in the call path, not in the flag name.

One grounding find changed the scope, and an independent review narrowed it further — the more important of the two. The ticket text names `qwen2.5-1.5b` as a "legacy fallback" to sign, but on disk it is referenced only by `phase2_gates/` research and dev `scripts/` — no runtime service or the launcher ever loads it. Signing it would have wired a verification path to nothing (the built-but-wired-into-nothing anti-pattern in reverse). I excluded it with a documented reachability rationale. My first pass then covered the *two* genuinely-served drafts — `qwen3-0.6b-pruned-6l/openvino-int8-gpu` (the shared-pipeline draft, verified by `build_shared_pipeline`) and `qwen3-0.6b/openvino-int4-gpu` (the Policy-Agent standalone/fallback draft). The reviewer caught the real mistake in that: the standalone draft's load path (`gpu_inference` standalone) has NO integrity check, and nothing reads a PA-side flag — so my ceremony would have *signed* it and my runbook + PA config comment would have *claimed* it protected, while no code ever verified the signature. A signature nothing checks isn't defense-in-depth; it's pure attack surface plus a false coverage claim in an LA-facing ceremony doc. The scope-down (the team lead's call) is to sign only what is enforced — the one shared-pipeline draft — and track the fallback draft's real enforcement as its own ticket (#917). The lesson underneath: "is it served" was the wrong reachability question; the right one is "does a code path actually *verify* what I'm about to sign." Reachable-as-loaded and reachable-as-verified are different sets, and only the second earns a signature.

The build (post-scope-down): `provision_manifest_signing_key.py` signs the enforced set in one run — the 14B (required) plus the one shared-pipeline draft (optional/skip-if-absent), one key; the stager gained a `--drafts` convenience; `ceremony_preflight` reports the enforced draft (absent → advisory, present+mismatch → a real FAIL); the runbook documents the coverage, names the fallback draft as out-of-scope (#917), and shows the flag flip in the AO config only. The dormant flag lives only in the Assistant-Orchestrator config — the one the launcher actually reads — with no dead twin left in the PA config. Regression locks in `test_draft_manifest_signing.py` (11 tests): the draft verify posture across both flag states (valid sig passes, missing/invalid sig fails closed when enforced, unsigned passes when dormant, digest mismatch fails either way), the multi-manifest ceremony mechanism (signs the set, skips absent optionals, fail-closed on a missing required or no TPM), the served-set scope lock (14B + the enforced draft only — the fallback and the 1.5b are asserted excluded), and the preflight coverage. Full shared/ + launcher/ green; the config-sensitive service + security suites green. A second correction from the same review: my dormant-posture comments overclaimed — they said a present-but-invalid draft `.sig` would still fail closed while dormant. False, and worth keeping as a note: the 14B gets that "no silent downgrade" property because its boot gate calls `load_manifest_verified`, which checks any present `.sig`; the draft's only check is `verify_weight_integrity`, and with `require_signed=False` that takes the bare `load_manifest` path and never consults the `.sig` at all. Dormant = digest-only, full stop. The comments now say what the code does. What is NOT done and cannot be by a builder: the on-chip ceremony that writes the real draft `.sig`, and the later flag flip — both LA-present. The change ships dormant and boot-safe until then.

**Lessons 274–275:** (1) *Sign only what a code path verifies. A manifest signature that no loader checks is not defense-in-depth — it is added attack surface plus, once it reaches a ceremony doc or config comment, a false coverage claim. Before extending signing to a surface, confirm reachable-as-verified, not merely reachable-as-loaded; they are different sets.* (2) *Reusing a security control's config flag on a new surface is safe only if the new surface flows through the same enforcement function AND the flag still has a dormant state to give — an already-`true` flag has no dormant window, so extending it to an unsigned surface bricks the gate. Give the new surface its own dormant twin, and put the twin only where something reads it.*

**Next:** LA-present ceremony to sign the 14B + the one shared-pipeline draft manifest with `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` (the extended `provision_manifest_signing_key` run), verify on-chip via `ceremony_preflight`, then flip `require_signed_draft_manifest = true` in the Assistant-Orchestrator config — #107 stays open until that signature-generation step lands. Enforcing the Policy-Agent standalone/fallback draft is tracked in #917; deciding whether `qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct` is dead weight is a separate cleanup call.

### 2026-07-16 — The model named in a signing ticket turned out to be served by nothing

*Plain summary: #916 asked whether qwen2.5-1.5b is dead weight; verifying the served set on disk — not the ticket's story — showed it is served by no runtime path, so "dead weight" is a disk question, and every code reference to it is load-bearing history, not code to scrub.*

#916 (identifier #301) fell out of #107, the model-manifest signing work. #107 had signed the Qwen3-14B and the one enforced speculative-decoding draft (qwen3-0.6b-pruned-6l) but deliberately *excluded* qwen2.5-1.5b, on the reasoning that signing it would wire an integrity-verify path to a model nothing loads. #916 was the hygiene follow-up: is that model dead weight, and should its references be scrubbed?

The temptation was to trust the ticket ("only phase2_gates/ + dev scripts reference it") and go straight to a removal. I didn't. I read the served set on disk instead. `services/` and `launcher/` have zero references to the model. The served-model constants name only `Qwen3-14B` (TARGET_MODEL_OV_PATH `models/qwen3-14b/openvino-int4-gpu`) and the draft `models/qwen3-0.6b-pruned-6l/openvino-int8-gpu`. Both signing paths — `stage_production_manifest.py` and `provision_manifest_signing_key.py` — exclude qwen2.5-1.5b by name, and `test_draft_manifest_signing.py` is a regression lock asserting it must never be signed "since no code verifies them." So the #107 exclusion was correct, and the claim was verified rather than assumed.

The history explains the orphan cleanly: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct (INT4-MIXED, NPU) was the *original Orchestrator model on the NPU* under ADR-010 (2026-02-24). ADR-011 retired the NPU for all LLM inference; ADR-012 put the single served model (Qwen3-14B) plus its 0.6b draft on the GPU. The Feb evidence even records the model's NPU latency baseline as literally "invalidated" (`p5_task4_1_adr_addendum.json`). So constants.py's "demoted to legacy reference" is exactly right.

The real decision was RETAIN, not remove. I considered the scrub-it path — delete the dev scripts and the comments so the tree stops mentioning a non-served model — and rejected it. Every reference is load-bearing: the `shared/` comments are the #107 exclusion *rationale* (delete them and the "why isn't this signed?" ambiguity comes right back), the test is the *structural control* enforcing the exclusion, and the scripts + phase2_gates evidence + ADRs are the documented journey of the NPU-vs-GPU feasibility study that produced the whole GPU-only architecture. Nothing is dead *code* — no broken import, no stale served-config pointing at a phantom. "Dead weight" is true only for the ~1.05 GB of gitignored weights still on disk, and reclaiming that is a destructive operator step I left to the LA, not an autonomous delete. Docs-only change; the standing gate is untouched because pytest does not collect `docs/`.

**Folded into lesson 274:** *When a model is named in an integrity/signing ticket, verify the served set (the served-model config + the launcher's load path, on disk) before either wiring integrity to it or scrubbing it. A model can be referenced widely yet served by nothing; when that happens, "dead weight" is a physical-disk question, and the surviving code references — exclusion comments and the regression lock that enforces the exclusion — are load-bearing history, not scrub targets. The strongest dormancy here is structural: the model path is simply never in the served/draft tuple.*

**Next:** none required — disposition is RETAIN with an optional LA-only disk-reclaim follow-up recorded on #916; the micro-nit (constants.py:143 "Draft model under evaluation" is stale) is flagged for a future in-passing tidy, not this change.

### 2026-07-16 — Reachability, not production-path membership, decides whether a control belongs

*Plain summary: The Policy Agent's standalone/fallback pipeline was loading its speculative-decoding draft with zero integrity checking. I wired a fail-closed digest/signature check into it (dormant), after first confirming the path is genuinely reached by the guest-VM smoke ceremony and tests — the thing that made "wire it" the right call over "delete it as dead code."*

This is the completion of the gap #107's independent review filed as Finding B and deferred to #917. #107 signed and verified the *shared-pipeline* draft (`qwen3-0.6b-pruned-6l`) but found the Policy Agent's *standalone/fallback* path (`gpu_inference.py`, the `else` branch when no shared pipeline is injected) building its own draft — `qwen3-0.6b/openvino-int4-gpu` — through `ov_genai.draft_model(...)` with no digest check, no signature check, nothing. The 14B target on that same path already gets the full manifest sweep; only the draft was naked. #107 correctly refused to *sign* that draft at the time, because signing a manifest no code verifies is pure attack surface plus a false coverage claim in a go-live runbook. The honest fix was never "don't sign it" — it was "add the verifier, then sign it." That is this ticket.

The load-bearing question the brief posed was whether to wire the check at all or report the path as dead. I chased reachability on disk before deciding. The launcher is the only host-mode production path, and it *always* injects the shared, already-verified pipeline into the PA and **aborts** (returns 1) if that shared build fails — it does not fall back to a standalone PA. So the standalone branch is never taken in host-mode production, and the code comment claiming it is "fallback when the launcher's shared-pipeline build fails" was aspirational; I corrected it. But `scripts/guest/guest_startup_smoke.py` constructs `PolicyAgentService.from_runtime_mode("guest")` with no shared pipeline, and its guest config's `draft_model_dir` is exactly `qwen3-0.6b/openvino-int4-gpu`. That ceremony, plus the PA test suite, genuinely drive the branch and build that draft. A path a real ceremony exercises is not dead code because production happens to route around it. So: wire it, don't delete it — and that is also what the brief's step 3 (re-add the int4 draft to the signing set) presupposed.

The design mirrors the 14B/pruned-6L two-flag pattern. A new `_verify_draft_integrity()` runs `verify_weight_integrity(draft_bin, draft_dir/manifest.json, require_signed=self._require_signed_draft_manifest)` before the `draft_model(...)` call and returns False (refusing the whole standalone build) on failure. A new dormant `[security].require_signed_draft_manifest = false` in the PA config threads through `PolicyAgentEntrypointConfig` into the constructor — the PA now reads *its own* flag, which is the concrete correction of Finding B (the flag #107 removed from the PA config was read by nothing). Dormant = digest-only: the `.sig` is not consulted but the digest is enforced, so a tampered draft weight is still caught. Enforced = a missing or invalid `.sig` fails closed, at 14B parity.

The one real trade-off was the absent-manifest-while-dormant branch. The strict-fail-closed instinct says "no manifest, no load." But the shipped default is dormant, the production/guest draft manifest is present anyway, and making absent-manifest fail closed while dormant would both break the no-brick guarantee and force churn on every existing standalone test that reuses a bare tmp dir as its draft dir. I chose the codebase's own dormancy idiom instead — the exact shape `load_manifest_verified` already uses for a missing `.sig`: dormant → fail-*loud* WARNING and proceed; enforced → fail closed. Rejected alternative: gate the draft check on the target's `manifest_path`; I kept it independent because the draft is an independent defense-in-depth layer with its own flag, and coupling them would have muddied which control was actually engaged.

Ships DORMANT. Both PA configs (default + guest_runtime) carry `require_signed_draft_manifest = false`; the int4 draft is re-added to `DRAFT_MANIFEST_PATHS`, `ceremony_preflight`, and the stager now that a code path verifies it; the runbook now describes both drafts and the two-flag flip honestly. Regression locks: 7 new tests drive the real `PolicyGPUInference.load_model()` standalone path (only `ov_genai` mocked; the integrity check runs for real against on-disk draft files) — block-when-engaged and the toggle proving it's off, across both flag states plus the boot-safe absent-manifest case; the served-set scope lock in `test_draft_manifest_signing.py` now asserts *both* drafts are covered and only `qwen2.5-1.5b` stays excluded. Affected suites green: the new lock file + draft-signing + preflight = 42 passed; PA `gpu_inference` + PA/AO `entrypoint` = 159 passed, all 0-skip with LOCALAPPDATA redirected.

**Lesson 276:** *A control belongs on any code path that genuinely loads/handles the asset it protects, even a secondary one production routes around — reachability by a real ceremony or smoke path, not membership in the default production path, is the test for "dead code vs. real gap." The inverse of built-but-wired-into-nothing: don't skip wiring a control into a live-but-secondary path just because the happy path avoids it.* Cross-reference the existing "sign only what a code path verifies" lesson (#107) — this is its complement: add the verifier first, then the manifest belongs in the signed set.

**Next:** the LA-present on-chip signing ceremony now signs three manifests (14B + both drafts) with the one `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` key and, after `ceremony_preflight` is green, flips `require_signed_draft_manifest = true` in *both* the AO config (shared draft) and the PA config (standalone draft). Until then #917 stays open; commit `49fa8ec5` on `feat/917-pa-draft-integrity` (merged `bfe71660`), dormant and boot-safe.

### 2026-07-16 — Version pins answer "which"; hashes answer "genuine" — and pip's any-match rule almost hid the difference

*Plain summary: added a hash-pinned companion lock so a poisoned build of an allowed dependency version fails the install closed; the live tamper test only passed once I learned pip accepts a wheel matching ANY listed hash, not all of them.*

I picked up `#560` bullet 1 — supply-chain hash-pinning — expecting to write a
lockfile from scratch, because the ticket's 2026-06-04 description says BlarAI
has "no lockfile/pinning." Grounding on disk corrected that in the first ten
minutes: a version-pinned reproduction lock already exists
(`requirements.2026.2.1.lock.txt`, 256 entries), gate-locked by `#810`'s
`test_dependency_truth.py`, and a read-only `pip freeze` of the live `.venv`
came back byte-identical to it. So the versions were already pinned and true.
What was missing was the integrity half: the lock had zero `--hash=sha256:`
lines. That is the exact gap Sprint 16's own security review named — "C's pins
are version-containment, not supply-chain integrity" — the class where a
compromised build of an *allowed* version installs silently and the zero-egress
test can't see it, because it arrives through an allowed channel.

The build was deliberately conservative: the runtime `.venv` is live and
load-bearing, and the brief was explicit that touching it was out of bounds. So
I never ran `pip install` against it. I wrote a stdlib-only generator
(`scripts/generate_hashed_lock.py`) that reads the digests PyPI *already
publishes* over its JSON API — no downloads, no venv contact — and emits the
`--require-hashes` companion. 251 of the 256 lock lines are hashable; the other
five are structurally un-hashable (one co-resident non-BlarAI editable, three
`git+` optimum build-toolchain installs) and pip rejects an entire hashes file
that contains an editable or VCS line, so they go into a documented, commented
exclusion block rather than being silently dropped. None of the five is a
declared runtime dependency, so the runtime's real import surface is fully
covered.

The part that earned its keep was the live proof. I cut a three-package slice
verbatim from the generated lock and drove real `pip download --require-hashes`.
Correct hashes: success. Then I flipped one character of colorama's first hash
expecting a fail-closed — and it *passed anyway*, exit 0. The reason is that
`--generate-hashes`-style locks record every published file's digest for a
version (colorama had two), and pip accepts a download that matches **any** one
of them. My mutation broke the hash colorama's wheel didn't happen to match. I
had to break *both* of colorama's hashes before pip aborted with "someone may
have tampered with them," exit 1. That is a real trap: a tamper test that breaks
a single hash proves nothing and would have shipped a green-but-hollow check. I
considered narrowing the lock to only the one installed wheel's hash per package
(which would make single-hash mutation fail), but rejected it — the multi-hash
form is what makes the lock portable across platforms and is the pip-tools
convention; the right fix is to *know* the semantics, not to cripple the lock.
So the gate test documents it (`test_tamper_requires_breaking_all_hashes_note`)
and the verify doc records the exact three-step proof.

I stopped at dormant on purpose. The lock is committed and gate-checked, but I
did not wire `--require-hashes` into the venv build path or flip any flag —
that enable is a posture change and belongs to the LA's next egress-door
ceremony, per the `#560` gate-framing. Groundwork lands complete and reviewable;
the switch waits for the ceremony.

**Lesson 277:** *A hash-pinned lock that lists multiple digests per version is
satisfied by pip if the artifact matches ANY of them — so a "tampered artifact
fails closed" proof must invalidate every recorded hash of the chosen
distribution, and a single-hash mutation that still installs is a false
negative, not evidence of enforcement.*

**Next:** b2 (broader weight-integrity signing across the unsigned models —
draft, bge, Whisper, Kokoro, VLM) remains the other open `#560` residual and the
substantive second half of the next-egress-door gate; the enable of
`--require-hashes` at venv-build time is the LA-gated flip these two conditions
feed.

### 2026-07-16 — The error-sanitizer ticket that was already a closed door (scout the boundary before you build the control)

*Plain summary: #560's "sanitize raw exception strings to RPC clients" turned out to be substantially already done — the client boundary shipped earlier, and the remaining chat-path leak was closed by structural absence — so the right deliverable was a durable guard test, not a redundant wrapper. Getting there also caught me mislabeling the umbrella ticket.*

I picked up #560 sub-item 4 expecting to write an error-output sanitizer: the ticket (authored 2026-06-03) named three raw-exception leak sites — `dispatcher.py:104`, `server.py:203`, `entrypoint.py:826` — returning internal strings to the RPC client. The mature-not-minimal instinct is to go wrap them. I scouted first instead, and the picture inverted twice.

First inversion: grepping the leak pattern surfaced `services/ui_backend/tests/test_error_sanitize.py` — a teeth-backed suite already locking the client-facing `ui_backend` dispatcher, which mints a correlation id, logs the full exception server-side, and returns only a generic `internal error [<cid>]`. That boundary — the untrusted WinUI-facing one — was already shipped and live, in the standing gate. My first #560 board comment had called sub-item 4 "not started"; that was wrong, and I corrected it on the ticket. The lesson I keep relearning: read the ticket and the code on disk before you believe the ticket's framing of itself. I'd also mislabeled the whole of #560 as "the hash-pinning ticket" from the earlier b1 slice, when it is actually the umbrella internet-facing GO/NO-GO gate (four sub-items). Closing it would have signalled a false green on the LA's go-live gate. I kept it open with an honest CURRENT-STATE instead.

Second inversion: the genuinely open question wasn't the client boundary but whether the *backend* (AO/PA) `message=str(exc)` frames leak *through* the gateway to the client. builder-560d4 traced it and found the answer is no — and it's closed by structural absence, not a wrapper: `TransportGateway.stream_tokens` drops a backend `MessageType.ERROR` frame (logs it server-side, breaks the loop, `transport.py:2316-2320`), so the raw text never becomes a token; with no PGOV cached, `get_pgov_result` returns the generic Fail-Closed denial; `_m_prompt` never reads a backend error message. So the correct deliverable was a durable guard — `test_backend_error_passthrough.py`, 5 tests driving the real gateway + real dispatcher with a genuinely leaky framed payload, with a mutation check proving that regressing the drop to surface the payload fails exactly the two guard tests. No source change. Merged `c60bbe36` (test-only). The rejected path was the obvious one: sanitize every `message=str(exc)` I found. That would have been actively wrong — several of those frames are deliberately-labeled internal fail-closed diagnostics that *should* carry detail within the trust domain, and stripping them loses diagnostics for no security gain. Two frames that genuinely can reach a client (the LIVE `/ingest` generic-Exception fallback, and the DORMANT `/dispatch` legs) were scoped out as their own tickets (#919, #920) rather than folded in.

The transferable lesson: when a ticket says "add a control," first prove whether the boundary is already closed — by an existing control, or by structural absence. A guard test that locks an already-closed door is worth more than a redundant wrapper that adds attack surface and diverges from the real leak sites, and it costs a fraction of the effort. Structural absence (principle 4) beats a vigilance wrapper every time you can establish it.

A process note worth keeping: this landed during an extended outage of the auto-mode safety classifier that gates my shell tool. Verification is read-only, so I finished the independent review on disk regardless; the merge itself I handed to the LA to run via the `!` in-session prefix (guarded, test-only, conflict-free) rather than stall or improvise a git operation he can't rescue. Read-only work continues through a classifier outage; state-changing work waits or routes through the human.

**Next:** the remaining #560 gate sub-items — enable `--require-hashes` live (b1 residual), integrity-verify the non-LLM weights (bge/Whisper/Kokoro/VL), and the automated zero-egress test — plus the two ticketed leak residuals (#919 live-narrow, #920 dormant). None gates today; all live under the LA's internet-facing go-live ceremony.

### 2026-07-16 — The style authority the index vouched for, but that never landed

*Plain summary: the governance README named `STYLE.md` as its style authority in four
places — prose, an audience-matrix row, and a whole Style Authority section — and the
EA-5 ledger recorded that STYLE.md "landed in docs/governance/". It never did. An index
and its mechanically-built matrix vouched for a phantom peer for three months.*

Vikunja #915 read like a one-line link fix: `docs/governance/README.md` links
`[STYLE.md](STYLE.md)` as the directory's "style authority", but `docs/governance/STYLE.md`
does not exist on disk. The obvious question was whether the real authority lived
elsewhere under another name — in which case the fix is to repoint the link and delete the
dead reference cleanly. It did not. A grep of `docs/` for STYLE / style-guide / writing
conventions turned up nothing but the README's own dangling references and node_modules
noise; there was no style guide hiding under a different filename. So this was fix (a):
author the authority the README already promised, not repoint to something that was never
there.

What made the ticket more than cosmetic was reading the EA-5 landing-page ledger
(`docs/ledger/20260424_050528_...`). It states, in prose, that the README "catalogues the
13 domain docs + the `STYLE.md` style authority that landed in `docs/governance/`" — and
its ORACLE gate note proudly records "No edits to STYLE.md" among the untouched files. The
gate confirmed the README didn't *edit* STYLE.md; nothing ever confirmed STYLE.md
*existed*. The Audience Taxonomy Matrix even carries a STYLE.md row (Future Agent ✓),
justified by a rule that reads STYLE.md's own §Audience Taxonomy — a section in a file that
was never on disk. For roughly three months the index and its matrix vouched for a peer
that had never landed, and every reader who trusted the matrix inherited the phantom. The
irony is that the same README has a whole "Phantom and Forthcoming References" section
enforcing exactly this discipline (L-17) for `boot-sequence.md` — it just never applied the
discipline to its own style authority, because a resolving-link check was never run against
the inventory it asserted.

Authoring the file was the easy half, and I deliberately kept it descriptive, not
aspirational: the rules are the ones `weight-integrity.md` and `credential-lifecycle.md`
already keep — the six-part template, the ≥ 150-substantive-line floor (STYLE.md itself
lands at 258), path-plus-symbol anchoring with line ranges quarantined to a
`## Verified against` footer, the five reader personas, the doc-lifecycle frontmatter (it
passes `tools/doc_lint --strict` clean), LIVE/DESIGN-INTENT/DECLINED labeling, no emoji,
no "prototype"/"nearly done" framing. The one subtle constraint was self-referential: the
already-merged README's matrix is built *mechanically* from each doc's `## Audience`
section, and its construction note explicitly claims STYLE.md "does not have its own
`## Audience` section" but "identifies 'future agent' as STYLE.md's primary consumer." So
the new file had to honor that claim exactly — no `## Audience` section, with the
persona-self-identification folded into §Audience Taxonomy — or I would have made the
README's mechanical matrix retroactively wrong. Writing the target to fit the index that
already described it, rather than writing the index to fit the target, is the inversion
this ticket turned on. The rejected alternative — give STYLE.md a normal `## Audience`
section like every domain doc — would have been more "consistent" on its face and silently
falsified a merged doc's matrix.

The README needed no edit at all: its link was always `STYLE.md`, pointing at the right
place; only the target was missing. Supplying it turned four dead links live without
touching the index.

**Lesson 278:** an inventory or index doc that asserts a sibling *exists* — a matrix row,
a "catalogues N docs" count, an authority reference — must run a resolving-link check
against the actual directory before it merges, the same way L-17 forbids linking a phantom
forward-reference. The failure mode is quieter than a broken link: a broken link is
visibly red, but an index that confidently describes a file's *contents* (a matrix row
built from its Audience section) reads as authoritative and propagates the phantom to every
downstream reader.

**Next:** the #267 living-set frontmatter back-fill will sweep the older governance docs
(e.g. `weight-integrity.md`) that still carry the acronyms header without frontmatter;
STYLE.md and `credential-lifecycle.md` are already compliant and can serve as the pattern.

### 2026-07-16 — The reclaim probe that must not move the thing it measures (#900 instrumentation slice)

*Plain summary: I built the OFF-by-default instrumentation that will let us prove whether evicting a model on this laptop actually returns its memory to Windows — and the whole design constraint was that turning the measurement off has to leave the eviction byte-for-byte unchanged.*

#897's RAM-headroom study surfaced a suspicion I couldn't confirm from the desk: this exact chip generation (Lunar Lake) is reported upstream (openvino #33896) to *retain* GPU memory after a model is unloaded, where the next chip generation hands it back. That pattern-matches BlarAI's old "insufficient memory with 22 GB actually free" image-gen incident. #900's job is to stop guessing and measure it. This slice is the GPU-free half: the instrumentation that captures the measurement. The actual answer — does the driver hold the ~9.7 GB of the 14B or not — is the @hardware run, which needs BlarAI up with real models on the Arc 140V, and the main session owns that live.

The first thing I checked was whether #897 had already left a memory probe I should reuse. It hadn't — `shared/diagnostics.py` had `memory_snapshot()` and `log_memory()`, but they report *Available* and a used-percent, and nothing computes In-Use = Total − Available or a before/after delta. So step one was a real helper: `in_use_mb()` (Total − Available, never a working set — on the unified pool a working-set sum misses the GPU allocation entirely), a `ReclaimSample` dataclass whose `reclaimed_mb = in_use_before − in_use_after` is positive when Windows genuinely got memory back and ~0/negative when the driver held it, a `to_record()` that serialises to the community-dataset JSON shape, and two gated front doors: `record_reclaim()` for paths that already snapshot, and a `reclaim_probe()` context manager for paths that don't.

The design tension worth recording is that instrumentation for *this* question cannot perturb the thing it measures. Two consequences fell out of that. First, the probe is gated OFF by default (an env flag plus a process override for the harness), and the OFF path takes no snapshot at all — a single boolean check — so an instrumented eviction is exactly the work it was before until a measurement run arms it. Second, and less obvious: I wanted a single tidy `with reclaim_probe(...)` around the 14B evict, but the 14B's `unload()` deliberately runs `gc.collect()` *outside* its lock (so a concurrent generate can start the lazy reload while gc runs). Wrapping it in the context manager would have either dragged gc inside the lock or forced the before-snapshot to happen after the reference was already dropped — either a behaviour change or an undercount. So for the two 14B methods I used the plainer gated-manual pattern (snapshot under the lock only when armed, record after), and saved the context manager for the embedding-cache idle-unload, where the clear is a simple in-place zero under one lock and the CM fits cleanly. Right tool per path beat one uniform wrapping.

One binding-time bug I caught in my own code before it shipped: the context manager's `snapshot_fn` first defaulted to `memory_snapshot` as a default argument, which captures the original at definition time and would have made the whole thing un-mockable (and un-substitutable by the measurement harness). Switched it to late binding — resolve the module global at call time — and the wiring tests that monkeypatch `memory_snapshot` then actually exercised the real code path instead of a frozen reference.

I instrumented five evict paths, not three. The ticket names SDXL post-generate, the 14B evict/reload, and the embedding-cache idle-unload; I also wired the VLM unload (it's one of the "other resident caches" the orchestrator evicts before a generate, same shape) and `release_gpu_for_exit()` (its own docstring already promised "the instrumented GPU-free before/after" — it was asking for exactly this). All GPU-free tested by driving the real entry points with a fake resident object and a scripted memory source: 37 new tests, and each path gets both proofs — it fires with the right delta when armed, and it is provably silent (zero snapshots taken) when off, including on a no-op or refused eviction so we never log a bogus zero-delta record. Full `shared/` came back 2970 passed / 0 failed; the touched-module files 158 passed.

The trade-off I'm consciously accepting: this slice measures nothing real yet — every number in a unit test is mocked. That's correct for a worktree with no GPU, but it means the ticket stays open on the honest part (the live deltas), and the "if the driver holds memory, isolate the job in a short-lived process + evaluate the blob-cache" follow-ons are deliberately NOT built here — they're decisions that only make sense after the measurement says the driver actually holds it.

**Lesson 279:** *instrumentation for a question of the form "does X actually happen" must be built so that turning it off leaves X byte-for-byte unchanged — gate the probe so the off path takes no measurement, and never relocate a `gc.collect()` (or any load-bearing step) across a lock just to fit a context manager. Pick the plain gated-manual pattern where the tidy wrapper would move the machinery.*

**Next:** the main session runs the @hardware measurement — arm the probe (`BLARAI_MEM_RECLAIM_PROBE=1`), exercise each evict path on the live Arc 140V with the models loaded, capture the In-Use deltas, and record them community-grade in PERFORMANCE_LOG.md + docs/performance/. If a path's `reclaimed_mb` comes back ~0, that's the #33896 confirmation and the short-lived-process / blob-cache follow-ons get their tickets.

### 2026-07-16 — The probe that found nothing wrong, and the trigger that wasn't there

*Plain summary: the #900 live memory-reclaim measurement answered its question cleanly — the GPU driver returns every byte on model eviction, the upstream bug does not exist on our silicon — but the runbook's primary trigger turned out to be impossible in the shipped config, and the brief I inherited was wrong on two load-bearing disk facts within a day of being written.*

The question #900 existed to answer was simple and slightly ominous: when BlarAI evicts the resident 14B mid-life, does Windows actually get the ~10 GB back, or does the Lunar Lake driver quietly keep it (openvino #33896)? If the driver kept it, every image-generation evict/reload cycle would be a slow leak, and two mitigation work-items were pre-staged behind the answer.

The answer is unambiguous: **the driver gives it back, every time, on every path.** Three real `unload()` cycles through the launcher's own `build_shared_pipeline` returned a median of 11,830 MB each; the process-exit release returned 11,920 MB; the in-app SDXL evictions returned ~1,347 MB, the vision-model evictions ~1,598 MB, and the embedding cache's ~180 vectors were sub-megabyte, exactly as designed. The discriminator I trusted most wasn't any single number — it was that **process RSS and system In-Use moved in lockstep on all twelve samples**. Retention has a signature: RSS falls while In-Use doesn't. It never appeared. The pre-staged mitigation tickets die unopened, which is the good kind of dead work.

Two things earned their place in the journal beyond the headline.

First, the runbook's route A was impossible before I started. The designed production trigger for the 14B evict — a hires-fix image generate — has been `hires_enabled=false` in the shipped config since a 1536² refine once drove the box to 8 MB of available RAM. The runbook (written 2026-07-16, same day!) and the handoff brief both offered it as the primary route. Nobody had checked the flag. The measurement went ahead on the sanctioned route-B harness with zero config changes, but the lesson generalizes: **a measurement plan that names a trigger must verify the trigger's config is live, at plan time** — otherwise the plan quietly describes a system that doesn't exist. Related instance, same day: the brief asserted the llama.cpp working set was on disk (deleted ~2026-07-12) and that a ~70 GB source download + CPU conversion was pending (the Intel-official artifact had already been fully downloaded on 2026-07-11 by a predecessor). Two load-bearing facts, both stale, both caught only by grounding on disk before gating. The twist, found hours later: the deletion was neither rogue nor unrecorded — the 2026-07-10 overnight handoff's disk plan explicitly sanctioned reclaiming the benched GGUFs after their numbers were banked, and its "check daily for an official Intel IR" alternative is exactly what the 2026-07-11 session executed. The record existed; it lived in a GITIGNORED handoff file that no successor grounding via git and tickets would ever see, while the ticket carried nothing. Durability-without-distribution, textbook case: a sanctioned-cleanup decision that changes what is on disk must land on the ticket (the durable queue the next actor actually reads), not only in a handoff brief with a lifespan of one session. This is a recurrence of both the verify-handoff-facts-on-disk and durability-needs-distribution lesson classes — the integrator should tally both rather than mint new numbers.

Second, a bonus finding with teeth — and a corrected one: during ordinary `/imagine` turns the box peaked at ≈**29.1 of the 31.3 GiB ceiling with ≈2.1–2.6 GiB available** while the 14B stayed co-resident. (My first draft of this entry claimed "available in the low hundreds of MB" — a unit conflation the independent pre-merge review caught: the probe records decimal MB, and I had subtracted decimal-GB In-Use from the binary-GiB ceiling. The mistake is preserved here deliberately: the same review pass that validated the headline also had to fix the author's arithmetic, which is the whole argument for author ≠ verifier.) The corrected magnitude still carries the design point: base SDXL generation leaves ~2–3 GiB of margin, the hires refine's extra buffers demonstrably did not fit inside it (the recorded spiral), and any future capability adding resident state to an image turn spends from that same thin margin.

Trade-off worth naming: I measured route B (harness-built launcher-shape pipeline) instead of flipping `hires_enabled` on at the config's own prescribed reduced ceiling to get the literal in-app path. Flipping a welded-off flag to manufacture a measurement trigger felt like the instrumentation moving the thing it measures — the rejected path — and the harness drives the identical `unload()` entry point the app would. The gap is named in the record's not-measured list rather than papered over.

The reclaimed magnitudes also taught something small and reusable: they reflect each pipeline's private+GPU footprint, not the on-disk model size — OpenVINO memory-maps weights, and file-backed pages never count in In-Use. An SDXL "3.3 GB model" evicting 1.35 GB is not a partial free; it's accounting.

**Recurrence of lesson 14 (tallied):** a measurement runbook's named trigger is a config-dependent claim — verify the flag/path is live at plan time, same standing as verifying a handoff brief's disk facts; the durability half (a sanctioned-cleanup decision that changes what is on disk must land on the ticket, never only in a gitignored handoff brief) rides the same tally. The smaller methodology point — carry process RSS alongside system In-Use in every sample; the lockstep/divergence of the pair is the retention verdict, and it rides free — is recorded in `FIELD_NOTES.md`.

**Next:** the #769 official-35B bench (the de-caveat leg) runs tonight on the now-proven-lean box; #900's harnesses + records ship on a feature branch after it; the #36270-adjacent upstream note stays LA-held.

### 2026-07-16 — The Neural graph was a queue, not a meter — and the handoff's "confirmed viable" was neither

*Plain summary: Finished the vision trailer end-to-end — locally-generated score on the Arc GPU (large model), six rounds of live operator direction, diverse recast via BlarAI's own image pipeline — and along the way disproved three of my own working theories with instruments.*

The trailer arc closed today: the empty `MUSIC_DATA_URI` slot the 2026-07-11 session left behind now carries a 90-second sectioned orchestral score composed by MusicGen-large on this box's Arc 140V, mixed under six rounds of narration the operator tuned live. But the day's real value was how many confident claims died on contact with measurement.

First casualty: the handoff brief's music-backend plan. It said `optimum-cli export openvino --model facebook/musicgen-<tier> --weight-format int8` was "confirmed viable, unmeasured." The command does not work at all — optimum-intel 2.0.0 rejects musicgen as an unsupported architecture. "Confirmed viable" had meant "a predecessor found a plausible-looking command," and I planned an hour of work on top of it before running it once. The recovery was better than the plan: PyTorch's native XPU backend ran the existing generator unchanged (`--device xpu`), 3.9× the CPU probe rate, zero custom plumbing. Lesson-shaped: a handoff's technical claims are hypotheses exactly like the operator's — the cheapest possible probe (running the one command) should precede any planning on top of it.

Second casualty: my own thermal story. Medium's full-track render decayed 4.10 → ~1.5 s-audio/min across 43 minutes and I wrote "sustained-load throttling on a passively-constrained iGPU" into the perf log — mechanism plausible, box thin, story clean. Then large ran its full track detached on a quiet box and got FASTER as it went (1.27 → 2.57). The decay had been contention from my own interactive session sharing the chip. I corrected the log entry the same day rather than appending a quiet contradiction. The general form: when a rate curve bends, the first suspect is what else you were doing, not the laws of thermodynamics.

Third casualty: the "Neural = AI hardware" reading of Task Manager. The operator noticed today's music job lit the GPU's "Compute" graph where every past BlarAI job had lit "Neural," and asked why and whether it mattered. Instrumented answer (`\GPU Engine(*engtype_*)` counters): the Neural graph tracks a driver submission queue, not the XMX matrix units — OpenVINO submits through it, PyTorch-XPU never does at any precision. The performance half of his question produced a clean negative: bf16 weights (verified genuinely bfloat16) ran 16% SLOWER than fp32 on this path with no memory savings. fp32-on-compute is the measured best config for the dev-side music tool; real XMX music means the manual OpenVINO port, now priced honestly in #858's design basis.

Also banked: an objective arc-gate (`verify_track_arc.py`) that measures per-section RMS against the intended dramatic shape before any track embeds — it caught the first render peaking on the Trial scene the operator had explicitly demoted, and a mastering pass (`master_track.py`) moved the summit to the finale with numbers to prove it. I cannot hear; the gate can. Same class as the WinUI screenshot lesson: for quality dimensions outside my senses, build the instrument before shipping the artifact. Notably the large model needed almost no correction (its finale was natively the loudest section; medium's needed +2.8×) — parameter count bought musical *structure*, not just texture.

Operational scar tissue, recorded because it will recur: the tool harness's background-task watchdog killed the large fp32 render twice at the identical post-load moment (14.8 GB RSS), with no Windows memory events and no GPU driver resets — and the same pressure made unrelated process spawns fail with bare exit-127. The workaround that held: launch via `Start-Process` detached from the harness, watch it with a coverage-complete monitor (progress lines, error signatures, AND silent-death detection — silence must never read as success). Six operator-direction rounds also landed same-day (montage recasting for actual diversity after I verified the generator's ethnicity-unspecified prompts default white; copy de-escalated from "make anything" to software-honest; a segment-spliced finale with hand-timed pauses because Microsoft's TTS blocks pause markup). Every performance number, both negative results, and the queue-class finding are in PERFORMANCE_LOG.md 2026-07-16 + docs/performance/musicgen_xpu_arc140v_2026-07-16.json.

**Next:** operator's medium-vs-large ear verdict picks the shipping score (one-command swap); #858 productization stays gated on his explicit go, its design basis now measurement-grounded (torch-xpu fp32 for dev-side scoring, manual OpenVINO 3-IR port budgeted for the runtime capability, local voice already proven by the shipped Kokoro).

### 2026-07-16 — The headline that died of a download

*Plain summary: the July-10 benchmark headline — "llama.cpp beats OpenVINO on MoE models" — was destroyed tonight by a 6-minute re-run: the OpenVINO number had been measured on a broken community conversion, and Intel's official artifact runs the same model 22× faster on decode and 131× faster on prefill. The evidence that would have caught it on July 10 was already on our own disk.*

Six days ago the #769 matrix banked what looked like the season's most interesting result: a symmetric architecture-dependent inversion, OpenVINO dominant on the dense 27B, llama.cpp dominant (4.6–5.1× decode) on the 35B-A3B mixture-of-experts. The OV-MoE cell — 4.0 prefill, 1.59 decode — was measured on the only artifact then on disk, an unofficial community INT4 conversion, and to the July-10 session's credit the cell was explicitly caveated ("conversion pessimality cannot be excluded") and the upstream post held until an Intel-blessed artifact could be re-tested. That discipline is the only reason tonight is a correction instead of a public retraction.

Tonight, Intel's official IR — which a predecessor had quietly staged on 2026-07-11, following the overnight handoff's "watch for the official publication" alternative — ran the identical VLMPipeline text-path methodology: **525 prefill / 35.0 decode, TTFT 372 ms**. The unofficial conversion wasn't a data point about OpenVINO's MoE kernels; it was a data point about that conversion. With official artifacts on both rows, OpenVINO leads llama.cpp on BOTH architectures on this box — 2.4–4.3× on the dense-hybrid, 2.1–4.7× on the MoE. The `MOE_USE_MICRO_GEMM_PREFILL` accuracy knob, the one lever we thought might rescue the OV number in place, turned out to cost nothing measurable at this scale — there was nothing to rescue.

The lesson is not "community conversions are bad." It's sharper: **the falsifying evidence was already ours.** Since 2026-06-29 this box has served Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B — the same MoE class, INT4, same GPU — at 38.6 tok/s median through OVMS. A 35B-A3B measuring 1.59 tok/s should have collided with that number the night it was recorded; instead the anomaly was filed as an architecture insight with a caveat. The July-10 session did the right procedural thing (caveat + hold the post) but never ran the cheapest validity check there is: *does this magnitude even cohere with what we already measure in production?* Tonight's independent reviewer prescribed exactly that check ("sanity-check headline magnitudes"), and it took one file-read to run.

Trade-off honestly named: the correction compares a 2026-07-16 OV row against 2026-07-10 llama.cpp rows (same box, same driver, six days apart) rather than re-running the whole matrix tonight — the cross-day caveat is carried in the record, and the llama.cpp working set is staged for tomorrow's production-model legs if a same-night matrix is ever wanted. Also named: the official IR emits untagged visible thinking under greedy defaults, so the genai #3937 chat-ergonomics blocker applies to the official artifact too — speed did not fix the model-swap watch's hardest active blocker.

Strategic consequence, teed for the Lead Architect with data rather than adjectives: the 35B-A3B consolidation branch now has throughput (35 tok/s without speculative decoding), multimodality (the official export ships vision components), and residency (~19 GB against the 31.3 ceiling) all green; what remains are the quality-parity eval gate and #3937. And the serve-the-coder-via-llama-server question that item 1 was framed around has lost most of its motivation — the right IR beat the alternative substrate before the trial began.

**Lesson 280:** every recorded benchmark headline gets a same-session magnitude cross-check against the nearest in-house production baseline before it is written down — an outlier that contradicts a number we serve daily is a validity alarm first and an insight second. (Candidate structural control if this class recurs: the perf-record template gains a mandatory "nearest in-house comparable + delta" line.)

**Next:** ship the #900 harnesses + tonight's records (branch → gate → merge); production-model llama.cpp legs + the llama-server thinking probe tomorrow; the corrected #36270 draft (v2, IR-provenance finding + a question back to the thread) stays LA-held.

### 2026-07-16 — Four hours was enough, because the protocol already existed

*Plain summary: the Lead Architect asked at 7 pm whether the two remaining benchmark legs could still run "correctly" before the 11 pm battery window; they could — both landed with full validity evidence by 8 pm — but only because every hard decision had been made days earlier and tonight was pure execution. The night also produced two genuinely new findings: the serve-the-coder-via-llama-server idea died on its own data, and the thinking-toggle probe isolated the genai #3937 regression as model-intrinsic.*

When the operator asked for legs 3 and 4 tonight instead of tomorrow, the honest time budget worked only because nothing needed inventing: the July-10 matrix had frozen the llama-bench protocol, the working set was already re-downloaded, and the probe script was a port of an existing one. Leg 3's four cells (both production models × Vulkan/SYCL) ran in eleven minutes — my estimates, calibrated on the pathologically-slow dense-hybrid 27B, were off by 5× in the happy direction because the production models are classic architectures with mature kernels. The lesson cuts the other way too: the same calibration error in the pessimistic direction would have made me decline work that was actually cheap. Estimates transfer badly across architecture classes; protocols transfer perfectly.

The results settled a standing strategic question with almost anticlimactic clarity. The coder — the one model where llama.cpp's MoE advantage was supposed to motivate a serving-substrate trial — decodes at 27.1 tok/s under Vulkan against 38.6 in the production OpenVINO Model Server. The assistant 14B: 7.5 against 13.6 (spec-decode asymmetry named — llama.cpp ran draftless). With tonight's earlier official-35B result, OpenVINO now leads decode on every model class measured on this box when fed proper artifacts. A second nuance died quietly: SYCL's best-decode-on-MoE crown from the 35B does not generalize — on the coder-30B-A3B, Vulkan wins decode 4.1×.
