Backend rankings are model-specific even inside one architecture family; every "X beats Y on class Z" claim we record should carry the model it was measured on.

Two tooling potholes are worth their ink. First, llama.cpp b9957's CLI is chat-first and hangs a scripted session even with the non-conversation flag — it sat at 22.7 GB RSS holding the GPU until killed; the server binary is the only automation-safe entry point, and its load-time layer-offload evidence (the Lesson-151 check) only prints at `-v` verbosity, to stdout. Second, my first probe design piped the server's stderr without draining it — a classic pipe-buffer deadlock in waiting. Both were caught before any measurement depended on them, but both are the kind of thing a protocol note saves the next session from rediscovering.

The probe itself delivered the cleanest isolation of the night — after two rounds of honesty repair. On the 35B, `/no_think` — the mechanism the Policy Agent's ADR-012 §2.4 design leans on — is ignored under llama.cpp exactly as under OpenVINO GenAI (coherent thinking, full token budget): the regression is the model's, not the runtime's. The template-level `enable_thinking=false`, which GenAI has no API path to reach, works cleanly there. The 27B told a different story in two stages of my own misreading. First I nearly recorded its condition C as "toggle works" from the summary flags alone; reading the output text caught 220 tokens of slashes. I congratulated myself, characterized the 27B as "broken only in C" — and the independent reviewer then read what I had not: the A and B conditions were the SAME slash-garbage, parsed into the server's reasoning field where my flags called it "thinking generated." The 27B degenerates in every condition on this build's chat endpoint (while coherent on the raw path in the 07-10 matrix — a template/server interaction, itself a datum) and contributes no toggle signal at all. Every conclusion now rests on the 35B alone, and the verdict rule carries a second clause: a field being PRESENT is not the same as a field being MEANINGFUL — classify the content, not the container. Instruments inherited across backends measure the old backend's shape until proven otherwise, and summary flags measure nothing at all.

Trade-off named: single greedy sample per condition (deterministic, and byte-identical prompt to the OV probe for comparability) rather than a sampled sweep — the rejected path was breadth we didn't need for a works/doesn't-work isolation, at the cost of saying nothing about sampled behavior.

**Lesson 281:** when porting a measurement instrument to a new backend, audit the DETECTION path against the new backend's output shape before trusting any verdict — tonight's probe would have silently under-reported thinking on one backend and over-reported toggle success on one model without that audit. The companion observation — wall-clock estimates calibrated on one architecture class mislead in both directions across classes — stays in this entry's narrative; no numbered estimates lesson exists to tally.

**Next:** ship legs 3+4 (branch → gate → merge, tonight); #769 reduces to item 4 (the two LA-held upstream posts); the model-consolidation decision brief for the LA now has every measured input it was waiting on.

### 2026-07-16 — We tested their fix so they wouldn't have to

*Plain summary: the evening's last arc built a stranger's in-review pull request from source, found it doesn't compile and — once patched — doesn't work on the one model that matters, and turned both findings into gifts instead of gotchas: a one-click fix suggestion and a test-bench offer, posted under the operator's name after his line-by-line direction on tone and voice. A first vision-quality probe of the 35B closed the night by showing exactly why that PR matters.*

The operator asked two questions in quick succession that reshaped the plan: "should we even bother testing PR #4139?" and then, when the answer was yes, "why can't we do the fifteen minutes right now?" He was right both times. The test was worth it because the PR's author had verified on three models but never on the Qwen3.6-35B-A3B — the model the linked issue is actually about — and because our own consolidation decision quietly depends on the control that PR adds. And "right now" was right because the only thing between us and the answer was a build I had throttled out of politeness to an idle machine.

The findings escalated in value as they got worse. The branch does not compile: its own new assert line lost its closing parenthesis, probably in a rebase, and MSVC fails on every file that touches the header — meaning no Windows user has ever built this PR. Patched locally (one character), the wheel builds, all four new API fields appear, the model loads — and then nothing works: with the switch off, with explicit think-token ids, with a reasoning budget, the outputs are byte-identical to baseline. Not similar. Identical, sha256-proven — though only after the independent reviewer caught that my first "byte-identical" claim rested on a 300-character prefix and an assumption. The mechanism of failure is the finding: the PR watches for `<think>` tokens in the stream, and this model's thinking arrives untagged, so the watcher never fires. A state machine keyed on tokens that never come.

Twice tonight the same lesson wore new clothes: my probe's summary flags called untagged thinking "no thinking," then called parsed-out garbage "thinking" — and now a prefix nearly stood in for the whole. Flags and prefixes are containers. Verdicts belong to content, verified end to end.

The operator's fingerprints are on this arc in a way worth recording. He redirected the deliverable from critique to collaboration — "fix it for them... rather than showing them up" — which turned the compile bug into a GitHub suggestion block the author can accept as their own commit. He struck a line from the draft that dressed my mechanism-surmise in his voice — "this makes it sound like I know what I am talking about" — and the posted text now says, honestly, "I don't know the internals well enough to say which." And he endorsed influence-first sequencing with a concrete trigger: if the thread stalls two weeks, we propose and build the template-level fix ourselves. The non-technical operator made no technical decision tonight; he made three judgment calls a technical reviewer would have missed, and each one made the work better.

The permission classifier made one too: it blocked the vision probe's first launch because I had casually included one of his personal photographs in the test set. Nothing would have left the machine — but consent to be a test subject is his to give, not mine to assume. The probe ran on neutral images, and the block earned its keep.

The vision probe itself closed the loop on why any of this matters. First-ever image turns for this model on this box: scene understanding strong (it decomposed a lighthouse render and correctly suspected it was AI-generated), artifact detection strong (it caught the stiff, unnatural fingers in a render when asked), and one result I graded wrong in both directions — it read the project's own logo as "BIAIr" and spiraled ("Wait, let me look closer... No, wait..."), which I recorded as a text-reading failure until the operator overruled me: the logo genuinely reads that way, he had noticed it himself, and had been meaning to fix it. The model's most-criticized answer was its sharpest — it detected a real design defect only the designer could confirm — and the session's grader became the evening's fourth instance of a verdict computed from the wrong reference. (The legibility fix is now #922; the operator's private to-do became board state the moment it was spoken.) Beyond that reversal, every single output was the model's visible thinking transcript, none reaching a clean answer within budget — because on our substrate, thinking cannot be turned off. The PR we spent the evening helping is not someone else's problem. It is the missing piece of our own consolidation decision, and now both sides of that dependency are recorded.

**Lesson 281 (shared with the benchmark-legs entry):** a verdict must be computed from verified content, never from a summary flag, a parsed field's presence, or a prefix — this failed three distinct ways in one evening on one instrument, and only independent review caught the third. The companion judgment — when a finding damages someone's work, the deliverable is the repair path made easy for them (the suggestion block, the test-bench offer), with the finding as its supporting evidence, not the headline — is standing external-contribution doctrine rather than a new number.

**Next:** the PR #4139 thread has our review + follow-up; two-week check-back ~2026-07-30 (queued on #859 delta 3). The consolidation brief's remaining measurements: the eval-suite quality gate and the 35B-vs-VL-8B head-to-head on identical images. The 27B chat-endpoint degeneration deserves its own upstream report once reduced to a minimal repro.

### 2026-07-17 — Ground the ticket's hypothesis before shipping its fix — and put identity before every kill

*Plain summary: The gate-kill ticket (#902) blamed an unmocked boot-reconcile in the launcher unit tests; on-disk grounding showed the reconcile was already double-guarded in-process and cannot kill a pytest tree at all, while the two dying tests were the only ones compiling the real 14B onto the GPU mid-gate. I shipped the ticket's both halves anyway (defense-in-depth) plus the guard for the real trigger: a fail-loud reconcile/kill tripwire and a benign model-build stub for the whole launcher suite, and a PID-reuse-safe reconcile that refuses every destructive arm when the recorded identity no longer matches.*

Ticket #902 read as settled: the standing gate dies at ~74% inside `launcher/tests/test_launcher.py` whenever stranded fleet-swap state exists, therefore `main()` must be running the live boot-reconcile and tree-killing the gate. The prescription was precise — mock the reconcile in the launcher suite, and make the reconcile PID-reuse-safe. Before writing either, I walked the actual call graph, and the hypothesis did not fully survive. First, the reconcile is only reachable from `main()` through `AssistantOrchestratorService.start()`, and the named dying tests mock that class outright; the root conftest additionally no-ops `reconcile_at_boot_for_roots` for every unmarked test (#758's guard). Second, the reconcile's only kill arm is `real_stop_ovms` — a name-keyed `Stop-Process -Force -Name ovms` — which cannot terminate a pytest process under any PID confusion whatsoever. What the grounding DID find: `test_policy_entrypoint_failure_returns_1` and `test_orchestrator_entrypoint_failure_returns_1` are the only `main()`-driving launcher tests that never patch `build_shared_pipeline`, so on the models-present primary they genuinely compile the Qwen3-14B onto the Arc 140V inside the gate — and the recorded die-point (about six `test_launcher.py` tests in, three reproductions) is exactly the first such unmocked build. A GPU-runtime abort or RAM-pressure kill terminates the process cleanly with no traceback, which matches the observed signature better than any reconcile path. On a model-less worktree the build fails fast and those tests pass for the wrong reason, which is also why the worktree gate never reproduced it.

I shipped both ticket halves regardless — the hazard class is real even where this instance's mechanism was misattributed — and added the guard for the likelier trigger in the same motion. Half one: a new autouse tripwire in `launcher/tests/conftest.py` patches all three reconcile entry points AND `real_stop_ovms` with `pytest.fail` sentinels (the #817 pattern — BaseException-derived, so the AO's `except Exception` cannot swallow it), making any future unmocked path a named failure instead of a silent kill or a silent no-op; plus a benign SUCCEEDING `build_shared_pipeline` stub in the existing side-effect fixture, so no launcher test can ever compile the real model — and the two failure-path tests now actually reach the step they claim to test on every checkout. Half two: `driver_alive`'s boolean collapsed two situations with opposite safe responses, so the reconcile now reads a four-valued `driver_liveness` — alive, dead, reused, unstamped — with a second identity axis (`driver_image`, stamped by both driver stamp sites alongside pid and create-time). A REUSED verdict (the pid exists but its identity mismatches or cannot be proven) converges the stale record only: no sentinel disarm, no OVMS stop, nothing external touched. And the destructive arms are now phase-allowlisted to LOAD-30B-and-later — a record stranded at HANDOFF (the #902 stranded shape) provably armed no sentinel and loaded no OVMS, so the old unconditional disarm-and-stop could only ever have hit someone else's fleet run through the recovery door.

Two trade-offs, named. A genuinely-crashed swap whose driver pid happens to get recycled before the next boot now loses the automatic OVMS stop — I accepted that because killing an unattributable OVMS is the strictly worse failure (2026-07-07 proved it on a live dispatch), and the expired record ends the haunting either way. And the phase allowlist narrows the "converge a stuck 30B" backstop to phases where a stuck 30B of ours can exist; the rejected alternative — keeping the unconditional stop as a general backstop — was rejected because the backstop only ever fires when an in-flight record exists, and a pre-load record is proof there is nothing of ours to stop. Targeted verification (this isolated worktree, LOCALAPPDATA redirected, live battery untouched): `pytest shared/fleet launcher/tests tests/integration/test_swap_state.py tests/integration/test_swap_ops.py -q` went 472 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped before the change to 487/0/0 after (15 new regression locks, including the reused-PID-kills-nothing gate-shape driven through the REAL identity probe against this live process). The adjacent swap-state consumers — exactly `pytest shared/tests/test_work_state.py shared/tests/test_heartbeat_cycle.py shared/tests/test_coord_render.py shared/tests/test_driver_ticket_leg.py tests/integration/test_dispatch_coordinator.py tests/integration/test_dispatch_harness.py -q` — ran 298 passed / 0 failed (re-derived and confirmed 2026-07-17; a wider guard-adjacent selection adds more tests, so any count must travel with its exact selection to be verifiable).

**Lesson 282:** a unit test that mocks the services can still run the machine — when a suite drives a real entry point, enumerate the entry point's side effects, not the test's mocks; every unpatched step of `main()` runs for real, and the dangerous one may not be the one the ticket names. **Lesson 283:** dead and reused both read as "not alive", but they demand opposite responses — recovery wants presumed-dead, kills demand proven-identity; any control that can destroy something needs the multi-valued answer, never the boolean.

**Next:** merge in the coordinator's safe window; then reproduce the ticket's scenario on the primary (stranded swap-state planted, full standing gate) to confirm the die-point is gone — and if the kill still reproduces with these guards in place, instrument Step 2.5's real model build directly, because it is the remaining suspect. *(Landed: merge `123bb8c3`; the counts-travel-with-their-selection note followed as `08426894`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — Golden expectations are authored from contracts, or the baseline launders bugs

*Plain summary: Built the fixture-board coordinator eval suite (ADR-039 §2.12.7): 76 golden cases feed synthetic Vikunja boards through the coordinator's real deterministic judgment code — classification, Definition of Ready, board transitions, stalls, tri-state reads, pagination, the quiet-queue tripwire, redispatch staging — with a committed baseline and regression exit codes, merged dormant-equivalent alongside the six existing eval suites.*

The coordinator program's item 7 has been promising this suite since the plan was approved: golden boards in, expected classifications and pull decisions out, re-run at every model swap as the capability-contract enforcement for the coordinator role. Tonight it exists as `evals/suites/coordinator.py` + `evals/golden/coordinator.jsonl` (76 cases, 14 judgment kinds) + `evals/baselines/coordinator.json`, registered seventh in `SUITE_NAMES` so the standing gate's existing eval locks (`tests/integration/test_eval_harness.py`) pick it up with zero new gate plumbing, plus a dedicated lock file (`tests/integration/test_eval_coordinator.py`, 15 tests) for what is coordinator-specific: the tri-state coverage shape, the pagination-past-the-clamp case, the forged-Done pair, the born-dev stamp, and source-fragment pins proving the suite still drives the real modules.

The method point worth keeping: I wrote all 76 expected outcomes before the first run — from ADR-039's stated contracts and hand arithmetic (the stall-outlier cases needed actual mean + 1.5σ computations; a 30-day item among 1-day peers clears the 27.09-day threshold, a 2-day item among 0.1-day peers misses its 2.077-day one, so I rescaled to 3 days against 2.709). The suite then passed 76/76 on its first execution. That order matters more than it looks: the harness's `--write-baseline` flow makes it mechanically trivial to run first, eyeball nothing, and commit whatever the code currently does as "golden" — which is exactly how an eval baseline once encoded a parser bug as expected behavior in this repo. An expectation authored from the contract can catch the code lying; an expectation copied from the code can only ever catch the code changing. First-run green is the only trustworthy kind of green a new eval can have.

Two design decisions, each with the rejected path named. First, the fixture seam: the synthetic boards are served by an in-process fake of the Vikunja HTTP API injected at the bridge's first-class `Transport` parameter — so the real client, the real pagination loop (against the same `maxitemsperpage=50` clamp the live server applies, drift-locked to `DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE` by test), the real tri-state classification, and the real snapshot composer all execute. The rejected alternatives were reimplementing the judgment logic inside the eval (mocks lie; grading a mirror grades nothing) and driving a live throwaway Vikunja instance (non-deterministic, port-bound, and the nightly battery owns the live substrate — an eval that can collide with production dispatch is a gate hazard, not a gate). Second, the redispatch cases construct the real born-encrypted `ProposalStore` via `build_proposal_store(":memory:")` rather than a stub — the eligible/refused/deduped decisions ride the actual cipher-wired store, including the cross-cycle dedup case and a TOCTOU case where the workspace repo becomes a governed-core-identity tree after staging and the execution-time re-validation refuses it.

Honest scope, per the brief: everything model-dependent is out. The `mode:"model"` field exists as a clearly-marked seam — such a case hardware-skips by default and a hardware run that would need to grade one is refused with a loud error until #855's shadow-grading adapter lands (fail-closed beats silently passing a case nobody can evaluate). Zero model cases ship tonight, and a gate test pins that. Also named rather than papered over: ADR-039 §2.12.8's global class-then-age pull queue with the starvation guard is designed but not yet built (#846 proper), so there is no pull-order composition to grade end to end — the suite pins its primitives instead (`pull_rank` ordering inside stall detection, DoR gating, redispatch eligibility, resource-gated tripwire eligibility), which is what C4's builder will compose over. All 76 cases are stamped `card_class:"dev"` — born-dev per ADR-038's ratified growth policy, explicit rather than default because the absent-field default is frozen and an unstamped card would silently change class.

Verification: `python -m evals.run --suite coordinator` → 76/76, exit 0 against the committed baseline; a doctored baseline (ghost passing case) exits 1; a missing baseline exits 2; targeted pytest `tests/integration/test_eval_coordinator.py tests/integration/test_eval_harness.py` → 66 passed, and the three neighbor eval-suite lock files → 87 passed, all under a redirected LOCALAPPDATA. Nothing in this change touches live coordinator behavior or any config default; the suite is pure read-and-judge code the gate and model-swap ceremonies invoke. Merged `7bed8a6c`.

**Next:** #846 builds the global pull-order composition (class-then-age, starvation guard, WIP refusal) on these locked primitives, and its pull decisions join this suite as new born-dev cases; #855's shadow-grading adapter replaces the fail-closed model-mode refusal with real graded cases.

### 2026-07-17 — The gate spoke a stricter dialect than Python and parked a green tree

*Plain summary: B4's overnight park was not the duplicate scaffold the forensic blamed — the layout gate's import probe used a check stricter than Python's own import rules and failed a tree whose oracle imports actually work; fixed the probe to mirror CPython's semantics, and collapsed the duplicate-scaffold root cause in the same arc so the seeder and the oracle stop pinning two different layouts.*

I was dispatched against #790 sub-task 5: collapse the duplicate scaffold layout that parked B4 (flashcards CLI) on night-20260715-230002 — the as-built tree carried BOTH a generic `app/` skeleton and the real `flashcard_app/` package. The forensic (c.2087) said the eager `flashcard_app/__init__.py` made one broken submodule fail the whole package. The disk said otherwise. Every committed version of that `__init__.py` in the archived repo is LAZY — a docstring and `__version__`, no re-exports — and the run's own `import-probe-verdict.json` reads "module 'flashcard_app' resolves but export 'card_manager' is absent" four times over. That reason string can only come from a lazy init. The narrative and the evidence disagreed, and the evidence won.

The real killer sits in `shared/fleet/import_probe.py`: the H3 check was `hasattr(mod, name)` and nothing else. Real Python's `from pkg import name` does more — when the attribute is absent and `pkg` is a package, CPython's `_handle_fromlist` imports `pkg.name` as a submodule and binds it. So a lazy `__init__.py` over real submodule files satisfies the oracle's import statement but failed the probe. I proved it with the production artifacts: copied the archived B4 tree to scratch, ran the run's exact `import-probe-targets.json` through the old probe (exit 1, 4 unresolved, byte-identical reasons to the run log), then ran the oracle's actual import line in a fresh interpreter — it succeeds. The layout gate parked a tree the oracle could import, and then the fix cycle re-ran a coder with instructions to "provide these EXACT importable names" — names that were already importable. Of course it resolved nothing: the harness was demanding a dialect stricter than the language, and no amount of re-running the coder can satisfy a gate that is wrong. This rhymes with the idle-abort arc (a healthy coder indistinguishable from a wedged one) and the scratch-test gate artifact: the chronic failure mode of this program is the harness manufacturing coder-attributed failures.

The fix is semantics parity, not leniency: when `hasattr` misses and the module is a package (`__path__` present), the probe now imports `{module}.{name}` exactly as `_handle_fromlist` would, with the same H3b in-repo containment check on the imported submodule. A plain module's absent attribute keeps its byte-identical stub-module miss; a package name that is neither attribute nor submodule now reports both failures in one reason; an eager `__init__` over a broken submodule still fails honestly, because it fails in real Python too. The rejected alternative was prompt-side: tell coders to always eagerly re-export contract names from `__init__.py`. That would have enforced a style Python does not require, left the false-red probe armed for the next lazy package, and treated a harness defect as a coder education problem.

The assigned defect — the duplicate scaffold — was real too, just not tonight's killer. Root cause on disk: the seed commit `bbae2e7` in the archived repo ("seed: python skeleton") comes from `new-agent-task.ps1` → `Copy-ScaffoldInto`, which copies `build-infra/python/reference` — package name hardcoded `app`, placeholder `core.py`, `tests/test_core.py` importing `app.core` — into every fresh python target. Plan-graph oracles seed FIRST and name their own package (`flashcard_app`), so two mechanisms pinned two layouts into one repo, and the coder dutifully built both. The "stale text-stats template" of the forensic is really the generic placeholder that every python job starts from; B2 text-stats just happens to be the job whose oracle (via the #752 F3 template pin) matches it. Fix at the root: BlarAI's `context_pack.canonical_package_from_contract()` derives the ONE top-level root the oracle contract names (conservative: quoted node lines skipped, anything but exactly one root yields nothing), `swap_driver` stamps it onto every plan-graph task dict, and `Copy-ScaffoldInto -PackageName` re-roots the seeded package and rewrites the package token in the seeded files — deny-by-default (valid identifier, not `app`, python scaffold only; any miss is the byte-identical legacy seed). The seeded `__init__.py` stays lazy, which is now safe because the probe speaks Python.

Numbers: BlarAI side `4016d5a8` on `fix/790-scaffold-collapse` — targeted suite 219 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped (test_import_probe_gate, test_failure_policy, test_context_pack, test_oracle_app_import_contract, test_integration_gate; project venv, LOCALAPPDATA redirected); the B4 exact-signature lock (both trees, lazy init, 4-name contract) watched FAILING on the pre-fix probe and passing on the fix, plus the production-artifact replay (old exit 1 → new exit 0 on the same tree and targets). agentic-setup side `7939a8e` on `feat/790-scaffold-collapse` (isolated worktree; the live battery's primary checkout untouched) — verify-scaffold 101/101 with 15 new locks (exactly-one-canonical-package, no `app/` twin, no stale `app` token, invalid-name kills, wiring), the C12 lock failing against the old lib ("A parameter cannot be found... PackageName"), and the renamed seed live-verified: its own pytest 2/2 and a clean `from flashcard_app.core import summarize`. Both branches are committed, NOT merged: the probe fix changes live swap-path gate behavior and wants the standing one-fix-per-battery-night A/B discipline; the scaffold limb is dormant until the agentic-setup branch merges. (Both have since landed: BlarAI merge `90da3967`; agentic-setup merge `5f2f649`.)

One boundary deliberately not crossed: the layout fix-cycle's owner-selection and verdict semantics (#790 sub-task 2 territory, and the still-open recovered-parked→GREEN LA decision) are untouched — this arc fixes what the gate SEES, not what the cycle DOES.

**Lesson 284:** a harness gate that re-implements a language rule must implement the language's ACTUAL rule — an approximation that is "close enough" manufactures unfixable failures downstream, and the fix cycle will happily spend a full coder re-run trying to satisfy the approximation. Corollary reaffirmed from this same ticket's history: forensic narratives are hypotheses; the verdict JSON and the committed bytes are the evidence.

**Next:** merge both branches on a quiet slot (BlarAI first, then agentic-setup post-battery), make the next battery night the isolated A/B for the probe-parity fix — B4 is the clean demonstrator (baseline: PARKED-HONEST[BUILD] on an oracle-importable tree; target: layout gate green, oracle runs) — and verify a fresh B4-class night seeds exactly one `flashcard_app/` tree with no `app/` twin in the as-built.

### 2026-07-17 — Retiring the "map is not the territory" debt with a verifier, not more vigilance

*Plain summary: built a script that re-derives every fact in a handoff brief against the live repo and fails loud on drift, so the lesson that briefs go stale is now enforced by a tool instead of a rule people have to remember.*

Lesson 14 — "a handoff brief is a map, not the territory" — reached its third tallied instance at the 2026-07-16 fold, and the third instance is exactly the one the curation rule says must ship a structural control in the same change. It hadn't, and the overnight integrator correctly refused to invent doctrine autonomously and escalated it as #929. The LA approved both halves the next morning; this entry is that control landing.

The failure class is always the same shape: a brief asserts a fact — a commit SHA, a file path, a count like "11 fragments await fold" — and the tree moves on underneath it. The most recent data point was a brief that said "11 fragments" while the CLAUDE.md snapshot said 9 and disk actually held 60. Grounding-by-eye caught it that time, but "caught it by being careful" is precisely the vigilance the third-instance rule exists to retire. Vigilance doesn't compound; a gate does.

So `scripts/verify_handoff_brief.py` re-derives instead of trusting. Each brief now carries a required, machine-checkable ```anchors block: a pipe-delimited table where every anchor declares its type and value, and — the load-bearing part — every *count* anchor carries the shell command that re-derives it on disk. The verifier runs `git merge-base --is-ancestor` on every SHA (a non-ancestor means the brief predates a history change or names the wrong repo), stats every path, runs each count's derivation command and compares, and structurally validates ticket refs. A brief with no valid block FAILS — deny-by-default, because an absent block is the ungrounded state itself. The template's step-2 grounding instruction now says "run the verifier," replacing "spot-check by eye."

The sharp design decision was the board check. The obvious version queries Vikunja so `#929` can be confirmed live — but that welds a network dependency and a running-server assumption onto a grounding tool that must work on a cold, offline checkout. I made board-checking optional, off by default, and structurally incapable of failing the run: with no built-in network client the `--check-board` flag degrades every ticket to a WARN, and even an injected resolver that returns "missing" or throws only warns. The hard gate is the three checks a plain script can always make from disk and git alone. Rejected alternative: a YAML anchors schema parsed with PyYAML — heavier, and a stray dependency for a script whose whole point is running anywhere; the line-oriented pipe table parses with the standard library and reads cleanly in the brief.

One real bug the screen caught that the first green run didn't: the summary line printed a Unicode em-dash, which rendered as a mojibake box on the Windows console — and worse, would have raised `UnicodeEncodeError` outright if the output were redirected to a cp1252 file. A fail-loud control that crashes while reporting drift is a defect, so all script output is now ASCII. Drove the real CLI end to end against this worktree: a brief built from real ancestor SHAs and live counts exits 0; the same brief with a fabricated SHA, a stale path, a wrong count, and a malformed ticket exits 1 and names each; a brief with no anchors block exits 1 fatal. The importlib/dataclass loading trap this build surfaced is banked in FIELD_NOTES.md.

Tests live in `scripts/tests/test_verify_handoff_brief.py` (36 cases, all green) — outside the standing gate's paths like the rest of `scripts/tests`, run explicitly. They drive a real throwaway git repo built in `tmp_path` rather than mocking git, so the ancestor logic is exercised for real: a genuine non-ancestor commit returns "not an ancestor," a fabricated SHA returns "unresolvable," both fail. A separate test asserts the shipped template still carries a schema-valid anchors block, so the template can't silently lose it.

**Lesson 14, third instance discharged (control shipped):** the map-is-not-the-territory rule is now enforced by `scripts/verify_handoff_brief.py` + the required ANCHORS block in the handoff-brief template, not by successor vigilance.

**Next:** retrofit filled ANCHORS blocks to live briefs as they are authored. *(Landed: merge `c7bfd65e`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — A blessed helper welds nothing shut until a gate forces its use

*Plain summary: The process-spawn helper had existed since 2026-07-09, but nothing stopped a new raw `subprocess` call from bypassing it — so I built the gate that makes bypassing it fail loud.*

Back on 2026-07-09 we consolidated five paid Windows-spawn scars (the venv `python.exe` shim defeating `DETACHED_PROCESS`, the cp1252 stderr that killed a banner print, Textual's "Driver must be in application mode" from a hidden console, `msedge --screenshot` no-op'ing, the undrained-PIPE deadlock) into one blessed surface, `shared/procspawn.py`, each rule carrying its incident citation. That was #774's first limb. But merging the helper *welded nothing shut*. A green conformance suite proved the helper worked; nothing proved that fleet or service code actually *used* it. Any new `subprocess.Popen` could re-learn the whole class one hop at a time — the "built-but-wired-into-nothing" failure the project keeps naming. A helper you have to *remember* to reach for is the weakest form of dormancy (security-by-design principle 4: structural absence over vigilance).

So sub-task 4 is the weld: `tests/integration/test_no_new_raw_spawn_sites.py`, a deny-by-default ratchet in the standing gate. It AST-parses every non-test production file under `shared/`/`services/`/`launcher/` (excluding `procspawn.py`, which legitimately wraps the low-level API) and finds every real *call* to a raw spawn API — `subprocess.*` plus the `os` spawn/exec/system family — resolving import aliases (`import subprocess as sp`, `from subprocess import Popen`, `import os as _os`). I chose AST over a regex deliberately: the plain grep that seeded this counted a spawn *mentioned* in a docstring and a `popen=subprocess.Popen` *default-argument assignment* as sites; the AST walk counts only `Call` nodes with the spawn as the callee, so `swap_ops.py`'s two default-arg assignments and every docstring reference drop out. Ground truth: 20 real call sites across 14 `(file, api)` keys. Those 20 are baked into a documented allowlist; the 12 `shared/fleet/*` sites are tagged as #774 sub-task 2's migration debt.

The identity question was the real design fork. Keying a site by `(path, line)` is brittle — any edit above a spawn shifts the line and reddens the gate on an unrelated change. Keying by exact `(path, qualname, line)` reddens on a pure function rename. I keyed by `(path, api)`-with-a-count instead: it survives line drift and renames, a new spawn anywhere increments the count and trips the gate naming the file+api+lines, and a migration decrements it. The named trade-off: a same-file, same-api remove-one-add-one nets zero and slips the count ratchet — an unrealistic shape (migrations remove spawns, they don't swap them), and one a reviewer's diff still catches. I paired the ratchet with `test_allowlist_not_stale`, which fails if a site is migrated away without the allowlist shrinking in the same change — the same coupling the WinUI passthrough gate uses to force its C# mirror to move with the constant, so the known-debt count can only go down.

I control-tested the lock both ways (principle 12): seven parametrized cases prove every raw-spawn form is detected (aliased, from-imported, both families), a synthetic-injection case proves the gate *logic* reddens on an un-allowlisted key, and I dropped a real `subprocess.Popen` into a throwaway `services/` file and watched the surface scan fail loud — `services/_gate_probe_delete_me.py: 1 un-allowlisted subprocess.Popen call site ... at ['L6 do_thing']` — then deleted it. A no-false-fire case locks that references, default-args, and docstring mentions stay green. 12 passed / 0 failed, 1.6s, in the standing selection.

**Recurrence of lesson 217:** the scar-class registry pattern (here applied to spawning) only becomes a control when a structural gate *forces* callers through it — the merged helper was necessary but inert; the ratchet is the discovery-lock half of the registry discipline.

**Next:** #774 sub-task 2 migrates the 12 `shared/fleet/*` raw sites onto `shared.procspawn` one at a time, decrementing the allowlist in the same change each time (the gate enforces it); sub-task 1 (PowerShell `fleet-lib.ps1`) and sub-task 3 (tree-kill consolidation) remain. *(Landed: merge `79248f55`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — A decoder with no bounds needs the bounds put in FRONT of it, not inside it (#727)

*Plain summary: the live web-search adapter handed Kagi's raw HTTP reply straight to Python's json.loads, which has no size limit and no nesting-depth limit; a hostile or tampered response could have exhausted the machine's memory while parsing. I added a fail-closed guard that checks the response's size and nesting depth BEFORE parsing, and falls back to "no results" (never an error) if either is too big.*

Ticket #727 is a security hardening — strengthen-only, no change to what BlarAI can do or to any egress/door/allowlist posture. The `LiveKagiAdapter` (`services/assistant_orchestrator/src/websearch/live_adapter.py`) decodes Kagi's response with a bare `raw = json.loads(result.content_text)`. Every OTHER failure mode in that method already fails closed to an empty result list and never raises into the tool loop — but the decode itself had no pre-check. stdlib `json.loads` has no `max_size` and no recursion-depth parameter, so a compromised or man-in-the-middle'd Kagi endpoint returning a pathologically large or pathologically deep body could resource-exhaust the host DURING parsing, before any of the adapter's careful per-entry defenses ran.

The interesting decision was HOW to bound nesting depth, since `json.loads` gives you no knob for it. The tempting path is an `object_hook` / `object_pairs_hook` that tracks depth as objects are built. I rejected it for two concrete reasons: a hook fires only as each container COMPLETES — bottom-up, AFTER the C-level scanner has already recursed all the way down — so it cannot PREVENT the recursion-driven exhaustion, it can only observe it too late; and a hook over objects never sees ARRAY nesting at all, so a `[[[[…]]]]` array bomb sails straight past it. So I put the bound in FRONT of the decoder instead: a non-recursive, string- and escape-aware pre-scan (`_within_parse_depth`) that walks the raw text once, counts `{`/`[` depth (ignoring braces inside string literals), and early-exits the instant depth exceeds the cap — so a depth-bomb is rejected in O(cap) work and `json.loads` is never even called. That is validate-before-trust in the literal sense: the untrusted bytes are measured before the trusted machinery touches them. The size check is the same shape: measure the decoded body's UTF-8 byte length and reject over the cap before parsing.

Both caps are a SECOND, independent lock DOWNSTREAM of the egress door's existing on-the-wire streaming cap (`shared/security/guarded_fetch.py` `_MAX_BODY_BYTES`, 8 MiB). They do not duplicate it and must not conflict with it: the door bounds what crosses the network; these bound what the adapter will parse. I set the parse cap to 2 MiB — deliberately TIGHTER than the door's 8 MiB (a real Kagi v1 search reply is a few KB and nests ~5 deep) — and locked that ordering with a test (`DEFAULT_MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES < guarded_fetch._MAX_BODY_BYTES`) so nobody can silently widen the parse cap past the door cap without a reviewed reconciliation. Depth cap defaults to 12 (Kagi v1 nests ~5, so 12 is generous but bounded).

The two bounds are additive `[web_search]` config keys (`max_response_bytes = 2097152`, `max_parse_depth = 12`), validated at boot as positive integers (`AO_CFG_WEB_SEARCH_PARSE_GUARD_INVALID`) and threaded through the entrypoint into the adapter. But the module also carries them as hardcoded `Final` defaults and the constructor CLAMPS a bad value (0, negative, or a `bool` — an int subclass that would otherwise slip through as a 1-byte cap) back to the safe default rather than honoring it: a `0` byte-cap or `<=0` depth would REJECT EVERYTHING, which is a different failure than intended, so the guard must never be silently disarmed OR silently inverted by a bad number. Fail-closed here means "toward the tighter posture," and a config typo surfaces loudly at boot instead of quietly turning the lock off.

Every lock ships tested ON and toggled OFF on the SAME body (security-by-design principle 12): an oversized reply under a tight cap returns `[]` and logs a secret-free WARNING naming the cap, and the identical body parses under a generous cap — proving the block was CAUSED by the cap, not an unrelated skip. Same pattern for depth (a 40-deep valid JSON body rejected at the shipped default of 12, and parsing under a generous 200). The sentinel key is asserted absent from every log line. AO web-search surface: 374 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped (LOCALAPPDATA redirected).

The independent security review passed the fix (229/0/0, the depth scanner withstood adversarial probing) and flagged two non-blocking hardening nits I then folded into the same branch — for a SECURITY control, "one lock is a design smell," so both were worth doing before merge. First: the `json.loads(body)` call was wrapped only in `except (JSONDecodeError, ValueError)`, but a deep body that ever reached `json.loads` (a hypothetical pre-scan miscount) raises `RecursionError`, which is neither — it would have escaped `search_sync` and broken the module's "never raises" contract. Not a live vuln (the pre-scan rejects deep bodies first), but the guarantee then rested on a single lock. I broadened it to a second `except Exception` backstop mirroring the egress-door-call handler's existing fail-closed idiom just above, so a miscount degrades to `[]` (confirmed with a test that monkeypatches the pre-scan too-lenient and feeds a 20,000-deep body that makes real `json.loads` raise `RecursionError` — search_sync still returns `[]`). Second: boot validation checked positive-int but not a CEILING, so a configured `max_response_bytes` above the door's 8 MiB cap would have silently made the "tighter lock" a no-op (the door truncates first). I added a fail-LOUD boot rejection (`max_response_bytes > guarded_fetch._MAX_BODY_BYTES` → `AO_CFG_WEB_SEARCH_PARSE_GUARD_INVALID`) — surface the misconfig, never silently clamp, matching the guard's own stance. Final AO web-search surface after both: 295 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped.

Out of scope but noted for a reviewer: `services/assistant_orchestrator/src/websearch/probe.py:218` has the SAME bare `json.loads(result.content_text)` shape. The probe is a standalone go-live rehearsal diagnostic, not the shipping runtime path, so I left it untouched under #727's scope — but it is the obvious second instance of this pattern.

**Lesson 285:** when you must bound an untrusted input that feeds a parser with no built-in size/depth limit, bound it BEFORE the parser, not with the parser's own hooks. A completion hook (object_hook) fires after the recursion has already descended and never sees array nesting — it observes the attack, it doesn't prevent it. A non-recursive pre-scan of the raw text is the control. And a defensive cap downstream of an existing wire cap should be a tighter, independent lock with the ordering test-locked, never a duplicate.

**Next:** consider a sibling ticket for the `probe.py:218` decode (same guard, or route the probe through the adapter) so the pattern doesn't drift back in. *(Landed: merge `60b79687`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — Positive controls don't prove a checker catches defects

*Plain summary: I was sent to build the offline (no-GPU) half of the #765 oracle-quality eval suite, found it already built and merged five days earlier, verified it green, and added the one thing it was missing — a proof that a BAD oracle actually gets caught, not just that a good one passes.*

I was dispatched to build the offline half of #765 Layer 1: the part of the oracle-quality eval suite that measures a generated grading oracle for soundness (does it wrongly fail correct code?) and sensitivity (does it wrongly pass broken code?), plus the deterministic static checks — everything that needs no model on the GPU. I grounded on disk first instead of trusting the brief's "the suite partly exists" framing, and it was a good thing I did: the offline half was already built and merged to main. Commit `0ae27f17` landed the suite (`evals/suites/oracle_quality.py`), the check engine (`evals/oracle_checks.py`), the frozen known-good B2 fixture (`evals/fixtures/oracle_quality/b2_reference/` + `b2_job_oracle.py` harvested from a GREEN 2026-07-07 battery run), and the first static-check bullets; `0e91081a` completed the remaining two static bullets (the #752-F3 import/contract check — reused verbatim as the SAME function object from the real #821 `scan_invented_contracts`, not a copy — and the criteria-coverage lexical heuristic). The committed baseline `evals/baselines/oracle_quality.json` tracks all 28 golden cases (27 offline pass + 1 `@hardware` model case parked as `skipped_hardware`), and `python -m evals.run --suite oracle_quality` compares clean at exit 0. The model-in-the-loop generation case (`oq-model-b2-job-001`, the live 14B authoring an oracle) is correctly deferred: skipped without `--include-hardware`, and honest-ERROR (naming the #765 live slot) when hardware is requested but no generator is injected. So the build was done. The temptation was to manufacture scope — author B1/B3/B6 references, pad the fixture set — to make the dispatch feel like a build. I rejected that; the single b2 reference is sufficient to exercise the offline engine, and the other-goal references belong to the deferred hardware cases anyway.

What I did add is the one thing genuinely absent. The existing tests were all positive controls: the known-good oracle passes the good reference (soundness), an append-override mutation flips it to failed (sensitivity), the engine classifier maps every pytest exit shape to the failure-mode vocabulary. Every one of those proves the machinery works on GOOD input. None of them proves the suite CATCHES a bad oracle — and a suite that rubber-stamps every oracle would also pass every one of those positive controls. That is precisely the invisibility #765 exists to end (a wrong oracle fails correct code, files under BUILD attribution, and is indistinguishable from a real coder failure). So I added two negative controls in `tests/integration/test_eval_oracle_quality.py` (section F), each driving a deliberately DEFECTIVE oracle through the real `run_suite` path over a one-case temp golden file: `test_soundness_defect_is_caught_by_suite` (an oracle asserting `tokenize('a b') == ['WRONG']` against correct code → `run_status=failed` under a soundness case expecting `passed` → the case comes back FAIL) and `test_sensitivity_defect_is_caught_by_suite` (a weak oracle testing only `tokenize` while `word_frequencies` is mutated to return `{}` → it passes the mutant → `run_status=passed` under a sensitivity case expecting `failed` → FAIL). Both green; the file is 23 tests (was 21); the committed baseline is untouched (test-only change) and still exits 0. Full file 23 passed in ~25s with `LOCALAPPDATA` redirected.

**Recurrence of lesson 222:** grading instruments need negative controls exactly as much as positive ones — a suite verified only with known-good input has proven it doesn't false-alarm, never that it has teeth. This is the `EVERY CONTROL IS TESTED OFF` principle (security_by_design #12) applied to a MEASUREMENT suite, not a lock.

**Next:** the `@hardware` generation cases (`oq-model-b2-job-001`, and any future per-goal model cases) still await the first live 14B slot to wire the real `generate_acceptance_oracle` / W4 job-oracle generator into `oracle_generator` — offline half is complete; #765 Layer 1 remains open on the hardware half. *(Landed: merge `30c7b646`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — A long-context memory guard has to know the model's attention shape, or it lies (#932/#930)

*Plain summary: I built a rigorous measurement tool that will chart how the 35B model slows down as you feed it longer and longer prompts (2K up to 32K words of context), including a fail-safe that refuses to run a context size that would exhaust the laptop's memory. The interesting catch: this 35B model only keeps a growing memory cache on 10 of its 40 internal layers — the other 30 use a fixed-size cache — so a naive memory estimate would have been four times too pessimistic and would have falsely refused to measure context sizes that actually fit comfortably.*

The consolidation research (#932) listed the "35B long-context decode curve" as an unmeasured unknown, and the status snapshot flagged that no protocol-clean long-context instrument existed for the `VLMPipeline` model class the Qwen3.6-35B-A3B loads under — the short-context bench (`benchmark_vlm_text_inference.py`) uses a fixed tiny prompt set, and `kv_cache_sweep.py` sweeps context but on the different `LLMPipeline` + KV-precision path. So this was a genuine gap, and the bar was explicit: minting methodology unreviewed fails the protocol bar, build it reviewable and community-grade. I did NOT run it (GPU-serial; the coordinator runs it after review) — the deliverable is the instrument + its methodology note.

The design decision that mattered was comparability. Rather than re-derive the timing math, the new harness `scripts/benchmark_vlm_longcontext.py` *imports and reuses* the short bench's per-generation primitive (`_measure_one`), its greedy config, and its median/mean/p95 statistics — so the decode-throughput definition is byte-identical (`generated_tokens / (total − ttft)`, greedy, `max_new_tokens=256`) and a long-context row can be laid directly beside a short-context 35B row. The only axis that changes band-to-band is the prompt length. The rejected alternative — a fresh standalone measurement loop — would have produced numbers that looked comparable but weren't, the exact trap the "mocks lie / test the seam" discipline warns about, one level up: a *methodology* seam rather than a code seam.

The real surprise came from reading the model's `config.json` before writing the memory guard rather than after. The 35B is a hybrid-attention Mixture-of-Experts model: its `text_config.layer_types` lists 40 layers of which only 10 are `full_attention`; the other 30 are `linear_attention` (recurrent/SSM-style, a fixed-size state that does NOT grow with context). The key-value cache — the thing that grows with context and threatens the 31.323 GiB ceiling — therefore comes from 10 layers, not 40. Combined with just 2 key-value heads and a 256 head-dim, the analytical KV cache is `10 × 2 × 256 × 2 × 2` = 20 480 bytes/token, which is only 0.625 GiB even at 32K context (and ~5 GiB at the full 256K trained window). A memory guard that assumed all 40 layers — the obvious default, and what I would have written from the dense-model habit — would have over-projected KV by 4× and could have falsely stamped a feasible 32K band as `skipped_memory_ceiling`. The guard now reads the full-attention layer count from `layer_types` at runtime (falling back to the total count — a conservative over-estimate — only when the field is absent), and I locked that 4× contrast into a unit test so the hybrid-vs-dense distinction can't silently regress. This also settled the caps: `max_position_embeddings` is 262 144, so on this box the binding constraint on the context ladder is memory, not the trained window.

The instrument carries the project's standing guards: AC-power fail-closed, refuse-to-start if the app/OVMS holds the GPU, a pre-load headroom floor for the ~19 GiB load, the #816 box-state stamp (I registered the new harness in the `test_perf_harness_env_capture.py` AST gate's `_HARNESSES` tuple, so it must self-capture or the gate fails loud), both memory disciplines (system In-Use = Total − Available AND process RSS), and a coherence spot-check whose full output text is captured for the reviewer to judge — the automated needle-recall flag is stamped `needle_recall_is_authoritative: false` so it can never masquerade as a quality score. Pure helpers (prompt construction, geometry, projection, band planning) are guarded-import-clean and covered by 22 unit tests (34 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped including the new AST-gate cases, LOCALAPPDATA redirected); the real-config read confirmed the 10-full-attention-layer geometry off disk.

**Lesson 286:** a memory/KV projection for a long-context guard must be driven by the model's actual attention topology read from its config, never the dense-model default. Hybrid-attention and Mixture-of-Experts models keep a growing KV cache on only a subset of layers (here 10 of 40); assuming all layers over-projects memory, and an over-projecting fail-closed guard silently refuses feasible work — a fail-closed control that is wrong in the safe direction is still a defect, because it deletes capability. Read `layer_types` before you size the cache.

**Next:** the coordinator runs the instrument on a daytime GPU window after review (exact command in `docs/performance/README_vlm_longcontext.md` §9), then records the curve in `PERFORMANCE_LOG.md` + the JSON. *(Landed: merge `fa6f2d61`.)*

### 2026-07-17 — The gate that parked good code, and the timeout that starved the job beneath it

*Plain summary: two #790 harness fixes — scope the per-candidate test gate to the spec so a coder's buggy self-test can't park working code, and lock the (window,budget) coherence that raising the idle watchdog quietly broke.*

The frontier coder-fleet defect stopped being the coder a while ago; it's the harness sinking the coder's working output. B4 on night-20260715 was the proof: `implement-card-management` produced working code (five product tests green) but the coder also left a throwaway `test_final_verification_fixed.py` with an `UnboundLocalError` in the test body itself, and the per-candidate gate — a flat `pytest -x -q` at the worktree root — collected it, failed, and parked a ~90%-complete app. The same shape as the idle-abort park: a gate artifact, not incapability.

The fix had to survive one non-obvious fact I only found by reading `acceptance.py`: a plan-graph *node* seeds no oracle into its worktree (the #690 per-task oracle fires only for single-task plans; the job oracle runs at integration). So at a node every `test_*.py` is coder-authored — there's no `OracleActive` flag to key off. What separates spec from scratch is git: tests present at the coder's baseline (`$CodeBase`) are the immutable spec; tests the coder added this task are scratch. So `Invoke-CandidateBuild` now restores every baseline test from the base (a candidate can't delete or weaken the spec — the protect property #690 gave the oracle, generalised), runs the coder-added tests as an advisory signal, drops the red ones before the commit (so a red self-test neither parks working code nor poisons the next wave's baseline gate) and keeps the green ones as coverage. I rejected the simpler "quarantine all coder tests" because it throws away legitimate coverage and, for a no-oracle single-task job, would delete the only behavioural gate. The regression lock drives a real git repo through real pytest: the B4 shape parks on the old gate and merges on the new one, and a wrong-code seeded oracle — restored even after a neuter or a delete — still hard-blocks (34/34).

Sub-task 3 was the other half of the idle-600 landing's bill. Raising `acp.idle_sec` 120→600 let a candidate run much closer to its wall-clock ceiling, and nobody re-checked the budget *beneath* the one we moved: Blair's Lab reached "candidate 1 of 2" and then "the overall run budget elapsed." That per-job budget is BlarAI's `swap_run_budget_s` (10800s), not an agentic-setup constant — so the retune belongs there, not here. What belongs here is the coherence lock I was missing: `verify-battery-budget-coherence.ps1` encodes the three dominance pairs (per-candidate ceiling ≥ idle; per-job budget ≥ waves×best-of-N×ceiling; and — the guard that matters — a fix to the middle pair must still fit under the PT16H Task-Scheduler ceiling). It proves the live starvation (10800 < 14400 for a 2-wave job) and, more usefully, proves the fix can't be a global bump: raising all six campaign jobs to 14400 derives a 25h floor against a 16h ceiling. The lever is per-card `run_budget_s` on the multi-wave jobs, or the operator's ceiling — never a blanket constant. Lesson 221 again: every window must dominate the budget beneath it, and when you move one timeout you own the whole stack under it.

**Next:** land sub-task 1 on its own battery night (B4 is the demonstrator — watch its BUILD-park clear); pair the budget lock with a BlarAI-side per-card `run_budget_s` on the multi-wave cards and confirm both `verify-battery-budget-coherence.ps1` and #833's T4 go green; open the node/Pester scratch-test-scoping follow-up ticket. *(Held for its own battery night, then landed on LA direction 2026-07-19 — agentic-setup merge `aa73d51`, sub-tasks 1+3, one day after its assigned night; the slip itself became evidence for lesson 290 — see the 2026-07-19 morning-read entry.)*

### 2026-07-17 — The per-card budget that fed the multi-wave job without feeding the ceiling
*Plain summary: the coder fleet's "overall run budget elapsed" starvation was a too-small per-job timeout; the fix is a per-card budget on the multi-wave battery jobs only, sized to clear the coherence floor while the operator's nightly wall-clock ceiling stays exactly where it was.*

The #790 idle-600 landing let a candidate run much closer to its wall-clock ceiling, and nobody re-checked the budget beneath the one that moved: Blair's Lab reached "candidate 1 of 2" and then "the overall run budget elapsed." That per-job budget is BlarAI's `swap_run_budget_s` (10800 s / 3 h), and the coherence lock `verify-battery-budget-coherence.ps1` RED-confirmed it live — C2 FAIL, 10800 < the 14400 s a 2-wave/best-of-2 job needs (2 waves x 2 candidates x the 3600 s per-candidate ceiling). The same lock proved what the fix must NOT be: a global `swap_run_budget_s` bump to 14400 derives a ~25 h floor at the six-job campaign against the PT16H (16 h) Task-Scheduler ceiling — it would trade a starvation for a preemption.

So the lever is per-card, not global. I set `run_budget_s` on the two active lean-campaign multi-wave cards: B4 = 16200 s (the 5-unit mixed job whose plan grain decomposes to six dependent waves — the heaviest wave count, so the top of the LA-approved 14400–16200 band buys it the most headroom over the C2 floor) and B2 = 15000 s (the 4-unit diamond, `expects_waves_gte` 3 — a multi-wave card whose 10800 s default also fails C2, though it is a fast ~1 h canary so its budget is an abort ceiling it never legitimately reaches). The pair sums to 31200 s and the derived RUN-phase floor lands at 33600 s (9.3 h) — back under PT16H, so #833's T4 stays green with the operator's ceiling untouched. I kept the change per-card precisely so the default stays tight for the single-wave job (B7, one wave) and so a future campaign re-widening can't silently re-cross the ceiling: `verify-battery-task-settings.ps1` T4 fails loud below the derived floor, and per-card budgets provisioned across a full job set re-derive it.

The LA emphasised the registry half: the (window, budget) pair is the long-term ledger, the verifier its teeth. The card `run_budget_s` values are JSON data, not importable constants, so they can't be `TimeoutEntry` rows — but the EXTERNAL Task-Scheduler ceiling row (un-importable, BACKLOG-named) OWNS this pair, so I recorded the #833/#927 disposition there (ceiling stays PT16H; per-card lever; the three C1/C2/C3 relations named) and taught the per-card clamp row (`CARD_RUN_BUDGET_MAX_S`) that it is now a coherence lever. The regression locks freeze the BlarAI-side half the ps1 verifier reads: `test_battery_execution_limit.py` asserts each multi-wave card clears the 14400 s C2 floor and rides below the 28800 s clamp, and that the lean-campaign derived floor stays under PT16H (C3); `test_timeout_registry.py` asserts the registered clamp dominates the floor and the EXTERNAL row keeps the pair documented. I rejected registering the raw card budgets as timeout rows (the resolver has nothing to import for a JSON field) and rejected bumping the global default (the rejected 25 h-floor path). I could not run `verify-battery-budget-coherence.ps1` itself: it is not present in the agentic-setup checkout (it was described in the #790 sub-task 3 build) and it reads live cross-repo/Task-Scheduler state — so C1/C2/C3 green is confirmed at merge prep against the real script; the BlarAI-side arithmetic (T4 floor 33600 s ≤ 57600 s) is reproduced green here.

**Next:** at the #927 merge night (own A/B, earliest 2026-07-19) re-run `verify-battery-budget-coherence.ps1` (→ green on all three) and `verify-battery-task-settings.ps1` (T4 green at the active campaign size); if B5/B6 (retired-to-on-demand multi-wave heavies) ever rejoin an active campaign they need the same per-card budget under the same C3 re-derivation — a campaign-resize decision, not this ticket.

### 2026-07-18 — A "green" preflight that never checked the signature it blessed

*Plain summary: signed and enforced the spec-decode draft manifests (#107 go-live) with the LA present; the ceremony passed, but the independent reviewer caught that ceremony_preflight green-lights on `.sig` existence, not validity — a control inspecting the wrong signal — and my own boot-verify first tripped on a stale log line.*

The #107 draft-manifest signing work had been merged-dormant since 2026-07-16 (`c3c6fccd`); this morning, GPU free after the overnight battery and the LA at the keyboard, we ran the ceremony. He ran the on-chip signing (`python -m shared.security.provision_manifest_signing_key`) — three manifests signed with the existing `BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` TPM key (the 14B plus both drafts), trust anchor SPKI SHA-256 `508defe5c27c0f0f7e5477cb033180f6dad1de6c076b32ff8b015923137b5ae4`. I flipped `require_signed_draft_manifest` false→true in the AO and PA configs (`6fbfc67f`); branch standing gate 8518 passed / 0 failed / 0 skipped / 125 deselected.

Two things earned their keep. First, my handoff brief carried a caveat inherited from the ticket's own review history — "the PA config flag is unread; flip only the AO config." Grounding on the live code instead of the note showed the caveat was **stale**: #917 had since landed the PA standalone-draft verifier (`gpu_inference._verify_draft_integrity`, read via `entrypoint.py:664`), so the PA flag *is* enforced. Flipping both was correct; obeying the brief would have left half the intended coverage on the table. Verify on disk, not the narrative about it — again.

Second, and more valuable: the independent reviewer (author≠verifier, held separate on purpose) went past the diff and re-ran an ECDSA-P256 verification of both on-disk signatures against their public keys — and in doing so noticed that `ceremony_preflight` only checks that a `.sig` *exists* (plus the weight digest), never that it is cryptographically *valid*. So the "signed (.sig present); digest verified" line the operator reads as a go-light does not actually prove the boot-time signature gate will pass: a present-but-invalid `.sig` would read green in preflight and then fail-closed (brick) at boot — the exact failure preflight exists to prevent. Harmless this once only because the reviewer had independently proven the signatures valid. A control that inspects the wrong signal is worth a structural fix, filed as **#943** (route the draft check through `load_manifest_verified(require_signed=True)` + a present-but-invalid-sig block/toggle lock).

I also fumbled the boot-verify the first time: my wait-loop matched a `Minimal prompt-flow preflight passed ✓` line that belonged to *last night's* boot in the append-only `launcher.log`, and exited after five seconds with the current boot still cold-loading (`:5001` not yet up). Re-polling by the current boot's timestamps gave the real proof: `Manifest signature verified: …qwen3-0.6b-pruned-6l…manifest.json key=BlarAI-Manifest-Signing` (a line that did not exist pre-flip) and `draft weight integrity verified (…, require_signed=True)` (was `False` last night), clean preflight, `:5001` up. Banked: a boot-verify against an append-only log must key on the *current* boot's timestamps, never a tail match a prior boot's success line satisfies.

I deliberately boot-verified on the branch *before* merging — the runbook's own order is flip→merge→boot-verify with a one-line revert as the undo, but verifying first means a boot-bricking config never reaches main at all. From this boot on, a missing or invalid draft `.sig` fails closed at 14B parity; the authoritative 14B path is untouched. One honest limit noted: the PA-config flag is **inert on real host-mode boots** (host-mode attaches the shared pipeline and never takes the PA standalone verify branch) — it bites only the standalone/test path, so it's defense-in-depth + posture consistency, not host-mode enforcement.

**Next:** land #943 so a green preflight actually proves the signature verifies; the eventual Qwen3.6 production model signs at *provisioning* time (last step, sign-only-what's-enforced), not early on the bench copy. *(Landed: merge `78b17598`.)*

### 2026-07-18 — The gate's verdict depends on the shell that runs it

*Plain summary: shipping the LA-decided voice pace (1.5 → 0.90 ≈ 189 WPM) took ten minutes; proving the gate green took three runs, because the test harness's own shell was quietly poisoning two environment-sensitive tests and a terminal-sensitive snapshot.*

The change itself was the smallest kind BlarAI ships: `DEFAULT_SPEED` in `services/voice/src/engine.py` from 1.5 to 0.90, the value the LA picked by ear at the 2026-07-17 audition (#853 c.2189 — measured sweep 0.80→162, 0.90→189, 1.00→208 words per minute against a ~150–180 comfort band; he chose a hair fast deliberately). The regression lock flipped with it: the old `test_synthesize_default_speed_is_faster_than_one` asserted `> 1.0` — a test that would have PASSED silently on any wrong-but-fast value and FAILED on the LA's correct slow one; it now pins `== DEFAULT_SPEED == 0.90` exactly. One stale docstring ("a touch faster than Kokoro's draggy 1.0") updated in the same motion. Commit `f2ae6451` on `fix/853-default-speed-090`.

The judgment-worthy part was the gate. First full run: 3 failed / 8515 passed — none in the voice area. Instead of shrugging ("unrelated, probably flaky"), each got a discriminating re-run, and all three turned out to be the RUNNER's environment, not the tree: the PowerShell tool shell exports `PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8:surrogateescape`, which the two `test_swap_ops` std-handle tests captured from `os.environ` and failed against their expected `utf-8` (production wires the value explicitly; the test asserts on an env dict the shell contaminated) — and the same shell carries NO `TERM`, which shifted the Textual baseline snapshot enough to mismatch its golden SVG. The bash tool shell has neither problem; the same 3 tests pass there individually, and the full gate from bash came back **8518 / 0 failed / 0 skipped / 125 deselected** — byte-for-byte the predecessor's morning baseline. The trade-off I declined: patching the two tests to tolerate `utf-8:*` variants would have made the gate portable to my shell at the cost of weakening an assert that exists precisely because a pythonw child with the wrong stdio encoding once died on its first non-ASCII print. The tests are right; the shell was wrong; the fix is running the gate from a clean shell.

**Recurrence of lesson 77:** a red gate is a claim about the runner's environment as much as about the tree — before believing either the green or the red, know which shell ran it, and prefer the shell whose environment matches how the operator actually runs things. The mechanical specifics (which shell, which three tests) are banked in FIELD_NOTES.md.

**Next:** merge on the independent review's verdict, post-merge gate re-run, #853 closed citing the merge SHA; the voice-direction residue lives parked on #940 (LA: PARK, 2026-07-18). *(Landed: merge `566b9cba`, #853 closed.)*

### 2026-07-18 — First grading of a shadowed agent: the acting layer earned trust, the speaking layer disproved it

*Plain summary: the coordinator's sealed shadow journal was opened and graded for the first time — 100% precision on decisions across 3.5 days, but the model-drafted prose claimed a parked run "passed all acceptance tests," which is exactly the failure shadow mode exists to catch before it reaches the operator.*

Ticket #855's item (3) — the measured shadow-precision check — had never been run. Today it ran. The journal held 117 entries across 74 heartbeat cycles (2026-07-15 → this morning): 43 would-be board moves, 74 digests, zero stall comments, zero tripwire alarms. Grading ground truth was the fleet runs' own scorecards, which is the honest referee: the coordinator and the scorecards observe the same world through different code.

The deterministic layer graded clean in a way that impressed me precisely where a flattering failure was cheapest: 7 of 7 distinct move decisions correct (43/43 journaled effects), including two conservative withholds — a STALLED run and a scorecard-missing run both kept at "In Progress" rather than promoted — and the parked B4 run correctly routed to "Ready". The forged-Done lock never bent. Same-day, the coordinator eval suite ran 76/76 with its sabotage cases refusing forged repo ids and governed-core writes fail-closed. And the journal delivered one structural elegance I want remembered: it re-records the same intended move cycle after cycle (fifteen "→ Done" entries for one run) BECAUSE the real board never moved — the suppressed effect is visible in the data, which is the shadow seal proving itself from the inside.

The model layer is where trust died, correctly — and the grading itself taught a sourcing lesson: one run (20260715-081634-bd) had no scorecard, and I initially filed its prose as "unverifiable" — until the dispatch-quality ledger supplied the missing ground truth (ABORTED: a Windows auto-restart killed it mid-CODE; no scorecard exists BY DESIGN for a pre-REPORT abort). With that, all five distinct drafted statements graded: one fully accurate; two put a "completed successfully" headline on runs that actually STALLED and ABORTED (true details underneath); and one — in two wordings, about 20260716-001549-bd — said "passed all acceptance tests… fully functional and ready for use" for a run whose verdict is PARKED-HONEST. The fingerprint is a measured pattern, not a suspicion: whatever happened, the drafted headline says success. Live, those sentences would have told the operator builds succeeded when they hadn't. The rejected path: calling this "within tolerance" in 3.5 quiet days and recommending graduation anyway — rejected because the error direction (optimistic) is the trust-corroding one, and because a deterministic verdict-consistency guard (prose may not contradict the verdict it summarizes — the headline is exactly what it constrains) is small, testable, and in the house style of the forged-Done lock. Recommendation delivered to the LA: hold graduation, build the guard, short re-shadow, then his ceremony. His grade is pending; the flip stays his.

Also named, not hidden: last night's B4 run was NOT harvested in the six morning cycles before shutdown — undiagnosed, watch on next boot; and the window was quiet-board, so zero-false-alarms is proven while stall recall rests on the fixture suite alone.

**Lesson 287:** when an autonomous system has a deterministic layer and a model layer, grade them SEPARATELY against the same ground truth — an aggregate precision number would have averaged a graduation-grade actor with a disproven speaker into a meaningless "mostly fine."

**Next:** LA grades the report (docs/governance/coordinator_shadow_precision_report_2026-07-18.html); on "hold and build the guard," the verdict-consistency guard becomes a ticket under #855/#841. *(The grade came back next morning: hold and build the guard — the 2026-07-19 #946 entry below.)*

### 2026-07-18 — The archives never got an archive, and every frozen file was owned by a ghost

*Plain summary: the LA felt sessions being tugged toward retired worlds and forgetting things; a 12-agent audit measured the cause (stale force-read doctrine + unrotated append-logs), mined all 465 journal entries for the 62 portfolio gems, and delivered a ten-decision restructure sheet — archive everything, delete nothing — now awaiting LA triage on #945.*

The LA opened with a symptom report, not a task: sessions "tugged in the wrong direction, worrying about the old sprints and the old devplatform fleet," forgetting things, regressing — and a friend's tiny CLAUDE.md that made him look at ours in horror. The horror was misplaced by one address. CLAUDE.md peaked at 399 lines and was rewritten to 273 on 2026-07-13; the audit graded it ~95% live. What he was feeling was the constellation around it: an effective ~24,000 tokens of injected-plus-mandated reading at every session start, a large fraction of it stale, and per-ship archive consultations that can pass 100,000 tokens. I mined 771 stored session transcripts to measure rather than guess: the journal (2.26 MB, ~579K tokens, 465 entries in 58 days) has never once been read in full — 103 reads, all slices; LESSONS.md (~81K tokens) was read 87 times in fourteen days, the hottest file in the system; and ACTIVE_SPRINT.md was read full-file fifteen out of fifteen times — a file whose active block is roughly 80% false, frozen since June 7.

The systemic finding is the one worth keeping: every frozen doctrine surface names a maintenance owner that no longer exists. ACTIVE_SPRINT.md literally says "Auto-maintained by Co-Lead Architect Phase 3 — do not hand-edit," so when that role was retired the file froze mid-sentence with an anti-edit order standing guard over its own rot. TEST_GOVERNANCE is "maintained by the EA that changes test counts" — another ghost. Meanwhile every surface with a living owner (DECISION_REGISTER, FIELD_NOTES, the handoff template) was current to within a day. Ownership, not diligence, is what keeps a document true; the restructure therefore leads with re-owning every always-loaded file and backs it with a freshness gate (three doctrine surfaces currently pin three different gate counts — 8518, 8490, and copilot-instructions' 2212, six thousand tests stale — under a sync rule nothing enforces).

The mining half repaid the LA's two-month "write everything down" bet. Seven era-scoped agents read all 465 entries: roughly half the corpus is genuine narrative — the near-quit ultimatum, the first packet off the box, the best-of-N reversal, the instrument-trust ladder — and 62 entries qualify as portfolio gems, organized by five acts and six threads, the premiere thread being novice-as-instrument: the non-technical LA's plain questions repeatedly caught what green gates structurally could not. The lessons corpus supplied the closing argument for its own restructure: L196 ("an append-only list dilutes its own signal") and L217 ("an invisible taxonomy earns a registered, gate-locked table") prescribe, verbatim, the tiering we are proposing for the file that contains them.

Trade-offs I want on the record. I did not restructure anything: the journal and lessons are portfolio product and April's #267 already put archive-moves under LA gating, so the arc ends at a decision sheet (D1–D10 on #945), not a fait accompli — the fast path was rejected on purpose. I also rejected deletion everywhere, including for the ~50% of journal tokens that are changelog/status/duplicate: waste classes inform future entry style, not retroactive surgery. And two of my own briefing claims died on disk — the dispatch brief does not load per-dispatch (it is a build spec; the real per-dispatch surface is opencode's AGENTS.md), and the snapshot-drift direction had inverted by audit time — both corrections are in the evidence pack, which is the audit practicing the honesty discipline it was auditing.

**Lesson 288 (first half):** a document whose stated maintenance owner is a retired role is stale by definition — re-own it or retire it the day the role dies; a freshness/size gate, not vigilance, keeps always-loaded surfaces true. **Recurrence of lesson 48 (third instance):** an audit's briefing is itself an inherited fact — hand every specialist the verify-on-disk mandate and expect corrections back; two of this audit's own briefing claims died on disk.

Evidence: docs/research/doc-infrastructure-audit-2026-07/ (16 files; measured read patterns, all seven mining chunks, the 284-lesson disposition, the 19-entry tug-vector register, the archive architecture). LA report: https://claude.ai/code/artifact/55cd953d-9d84-494b-8099-b6166328ddde

**Next:** LA triage of D1–D10 on #945; on go, the restructure ships in stages (one-time #267 moves → the three log rotations + tiering → the freshness controls), each stage an atomic merge + ticket + journal motion. Claude-side hygiene (memory prune + index reconcile, stale worktree cleanup) proceeds under standing doctrine today. *(The LA approved all ten decisions the same evening — "all as recommended"; the execution entry follows.)*

### 2026-07-19 — A cleanup without a gate is a loan, not a payment

*Plain summary: the LA approved all ten doc-restructure decisions ("all as recommended") and the archive-first rebuild shipped overnight in three gate-green merges — docs top level 157→16 files, the journal rotated into monthly volumes with a 64-gem anthology, LESSONS three-tiered (pre-mint search −91%), the stale sprint pointer and Copilot instructions rewritten, the three /sprint-* commands stripped of the retired world — and, the piece that makes it permanent, a doctrine-freshness gate test that fails the standing gate if the cleanup ever starts rotting again.*

The execution followed the audit by a few hours, and the audit's root-cause finding shaped the build order: every frozen doctrine surface had named a retired role as its maintainer, so the restructure led with re-owning, not tidying. Stage 1 ran #267's four-month-old archive half in one motion (git mv, history preserved): sprints 8–17, the executed scheduler queue, ~140 loose prompt-era files (each .md stamped `status: archived`), the retired fleet manuals, the frozen monolith ledger behind a pointer stub, the repo-root studies onto a reference shelf. Stage 2 rotated BUILD_JOURNAL.md through the new `tools/doc_hygiene/rotate_log.py` — 465 entries split byte-exact (2,316,373 = 1,087,216 hot + 1,229,157 archived; the tool prints its own proof) — published `docs/BUILD_JOURNAL_ANTHOLOGY.md` (64 entries verbatim, five acts, three reading paths; two near-misses honestly promoted over the audit's 62), and tiered LESSONS.md to 110 KB with the full corpus append-only in the archive.
