So I inverted it: each ceremony runbook now self-declares its gating flag in a machine-readable line (`<!-- Gating-flag: [web_search].enabled -->`, or `Gating-flags:` plural for the two runbooks gated by two flags each, or `Gating-state: keystore` for at-rest encryption, whose executed-state is evidenced by a keystore file rather than a toml flag). The checker resolves that declaration against `default.toml` on every run, both directions — a live flag with no EXECUTED banner fails (the #990 shape), and a stale EXECUTED banner over a still-false flag fails too. Because it is checked against live config every build, the declaration cannot silently rot the way a separate map would; if someone renames a key, a rot-guard test fails loud naming the runbook. Self-describing and cross-checked against the authority beats a copy maintained by memory. That is the same principle as the doctrine forbidding docstrings that narrate current wiring — the difference between stating a contract and restating a fact that will drift.

The second choice was scope, and here I have to be honest that "cover the whole class" and "don't cry wolf" pulled hard against each other. The LA's steer was explicit: build the version that actually covers the class, and only carve out and ticket if I *measured* that covering it can't be done without false positives. So I measured. Over all of `docs/`, checking every markdown link and every inline `path/to/file` reference, the checker returned 3,010 hits — but only 118 were markdown links; 2,892 were inline references, and 2,193 of the total lived in `docs/archive/`, which is frozen history whose pointers describe a past state and must never be rewritten to please a lint. When I looked at what the inline references actually were, they were overwhelmingly not defects: backup-directory layouts in the disaster-recovery runbook (`runtime-data/`, `hyperv-vm/`), retired-autonomous-fleet-world paths, gitignored model-internal directories (`unet/`, `vae_decoder/`), cross-repo references to the agentic-setup brief. Gating that would have been the cry-wolf failure itself. The markdown-link check, by contrast, was the load-bearing signal — mechanically unambiguous ("this file exists"), and it caught real rot #979 had missed. So the gate covers markdown links over the living tree (archive and the gitignored handoffs dir excluded); inline-checking stays available behind a `--inline` flag but is not gated, with the measurement written into the script's docstring and a bounded optional cleanup filed as #995. That is a carve-out earned by evidence, not reached for out of laziness — the distinction the deferral-discipline control (`9ecad198`) exists to enforce, and I ran `verify_disposition.py` against my own findings record to prove it.

Two false-positive traps were worth the care they took. Resolving links relative to each file's own directory (never the cwd) is obvious once stated; less obvious was that `../../Use%20Cases_FINAL.md` is a *real* file — the checker has to percent-decode the target, and until it did, the gate could not go green on the corrected tree, which is the same failure mode #978's weld-pin shipped with (a probe that flags the honest form). The other was the deliberately-struck dead pointer: #979 R3 fixed dead links by striking them (`~~...~~`) and annotating `RETIRED (file absent)`, so the checker *must* skip those or it fights the correct remediation. Both are pinned with both-directions negative controls in the gate test — a correctly-struck pointer must PASS, a live-flag bannerless ceremony must FAIL — because a validator nobody has watched pass the correct fix is as dangerous as one nobody has watched reject the defect.

Landing it meant fixing what it found, which the LA had explicitly authorized as part of the build. The checker flagged 22 dead markdown links; 13 were in living docs and I fixed them — three since-renamed ADRs across `docs/governance/`, an absent `fleet-hygiene.md` struck, three `AUTONOMOUS_FLEET_OPERATIONS.md` links in the live reboot checklist repointed to the archived copy. The build added a verifier (`scripts/verify_doc_pointers_and_banners.py`) and 34 gate tests wired into the standing gate; the gate re-measured green at 8628. The other 9 flagged links became the sharpest lesson of the whole build.

Then the control turned its own principle on itself. Before merging I had an independent reviewer — one that had not written the code — try to break it, and it found six real defects, the worst almost too on-the-nose. My ceremony-banner detector was a bare, document-wide search for the word "executed", so a live-flag runbook that merely contained "executed" anywhere in its prose passed clean — defeating the exact #990 guarantee the check exists to enforce — while a genuinely-pending runbook that read "once the ceremony is executed the flag flips" was falsely flagged, the cry-wolf outcome the whole design names as worst. A control I had built specifically to catch "looks-done-but-isn't" had shipped its first draft with precisely that flaw, invisible to me because the five real runbooks happen to carry clean, unambiguous banners. Five more came with it: a whole-line skip that let a live link hide beside an unrelated "RETIRED" note; only the first of several gating declarations being read; an unchecked `Gating-state` bypass; trailing text after a comment close defeating declaration detection; and — when I added reference-style link support to close one reviewer gap — a fresh false positive on a spec line (`[Peak RAM]: 25.6GB`) I had to filter back out. All six fixed on the branch before merge, each with a both-directions regression lock. The lesson under the lesson: I could not see these by re-reading my own code, because I read it as its author, already knowing what it was supposed to do. The adversarial second reader is not ceremony; it is the only thing that reliably separates "I wrote a checker" from "the checker works" — and it is doubly the point for a control whose entire job is to stop people trusting a document that only looks correct.

The other nine links taught me something the review had not. Switching to the main branch to merge, I found that six of the runbooks the checker flagged carry whole-document SUPERSEDED or ⛔ RETIRED banners, and four say in plain words that their dead links are "left as-is rather than repointed, because the procedures they belong to are themselves retired" — a deliberate decision a prior session made and documented. I had read only the flagged link lines, never the top of each file, and so I had "fixed" nine links a human had chosen to leave dead. The checker was mechanically correct that the links were dead; I was wrong about the remedy. The move was not to repoint them but to recognise that a wholesale-retired doc is a tombstone — history that happens to live outside the archive directory — and exclude it exactly as the archive is excluded. I reverted the four docs to main verbatim and added that exclusion, keyed on a SUPERSEDED/RETIRED heading word rather than the ⛔ emoji, because the first cut of the exclusion swallowed `at_rest_encryption_ceremony.md`'s live "⛔ STOP — DO NOT DELETE" warning, which is the opposite of retired. The lesson: a mechanical finding gives you a fact, not a remedy, and reading only the lines a tool flags — rather than the whole document around them — is precisely how you end up overriding a decision you never saw.

The rejected paths are the lesson. I could have shipped the hand-maintained map (fast, and rotten by design) or the broad inline gate (thorough-looking, and muted within a week). Both would have *looked* like the mature version while being the immature one. The transferable rule: a control's trust is a feature you engineer, not a side effect of its coverage — measure the false-positive rate before you gate, prefer a self-describing mechanism cross-checked against the authority over any map you maintain by hand, and treat "the honest form must pass" as an equal partner to "the defect must fail."

**Next:** #995 is the optional bounded inline-runbook cleanup if the LA wants it; the retired-fleet runbooks (`LA_CAR_WORKFLOW_HOWTO`, the `LA_SPRINT_*` family) are link-patched but their framing is still autonomous-fleet-era — a #979-class content decision, not a mechanical fix, and worth flagging for a future pass.

### 2026-07-20 — Eight reviewers can confirm a piece is true and still miss whether anyone would read it

*Plain summary: an eight-agent adversarial panel plus independent re-verification cleared both P1-S4 and P2 of every factual and rigor defect; the LA's first read of the finished ceremony package found a problem none of them were built to catch — whether the piece actually earns a stranger's attention — and it split the two pieces' fates.*

Phase 1 of the publication program ran its full pipeline for the first time
today: two four-reviewer panels (fact auditor, rigor skeptic, audience editor,
integrity/privacy screen) convened in parallel against P1-S4 (the openvino.genai
crash-fix field report, branch `docs/pub-p1-drafts`) and P2 (the composition-
failure specimen catalog, branch `docs/pub-p2-drafts`). The panels found ten
blocking findings between them — P1-S4's worst was a verification section
calling a statistically empty before/after comparison "proof" when the real
proof was a mechanism argument the draft had but wasn't using; P2's worst was a
specimen-count derivation with an unexamined candidate incident. Both authors
revised from written dispositions of every finding, and a fifth reviewer per
piece — neither the author nor the original panel — independently re-checked the
committed revision text against each finding rather than trusting the disposition
memo's word for it. All ten closed. A cross-piece question (P2's eighth specimen
duplicates P1-S4's entire incident) got routed through the panel itself: both
pieces' independent audience editors converged on the same resolution
unprompted, which is the kind of confirmation you don't get to manufacture.

Then I built a ceremony-read artifact — both final drafts in full, plus the
claims/support/challenged/changed memos — and the LA's first reaction wasn't
about any of that. It was: *I'm not really seeing the value for people in these.
Is there really any interest? This seems there's no story here.* He asked if he
was just too much of a novice to see it.

He wasn't, and the honest answer mattered more than the reassuring one. P2's
factual rigor was real and independently confirmed three times over — and
completely orthogonal to whether the piece was honest about its own stakes. It
borrows security-research vocabulary (kill-switch, trust spine, leakage
detector) for incidents whose actual consequences are a personal single-user
project's test suite killing itself or a feature not returning an answer — no
adversary, no user base, no real harm in any of the eight specimens. The panel's
audience editor graded against a written rubric (does it earn its length, is the
register right, would an expert keep reading past paragraph two) and passed it;
none of that rubric asks "would a stranger with zero stake in this project
actually click." An AI panel optimizing against a checklist is not a substitute
for a human's unprompted gut reaction, and the program's own founding document
said as much without me fully believing it until today: honest kills are a
success condition, and the audience-value call was always reserved for the LA,
never delegable to a panel however adversarial.

P1-S4 read differently to him, and the distinction is worth naming: it was never
built for reach, only for narrow standing in one technical community, and once I
said that plainly instead of letting the ceremony framing imply universal
interest, he was satisfied to keep it moving — with one new constraint of his
own: hold the publish action until the fix has actually shipped in a tagged
OpenVINO GenAI release, not just merged upstream. Merged-not-released was always
the piece's own stated hedge; he extended it into a publish gate, which is a
better instinct than anything the panel produced on that axis.

P2 is now handed off to a fresh session (`docs/handoffs/publication-program-p2-
rescope-handoff-20260720.md`) to reconsider from scratch — kill, reframe away
from inflated security vocabulary, or shrink to the specimens that actually
carry security stakes. The rejected path was deciding this myself and either
defending the piece (the panel said GO, eight reviewers can't be wrong) or
unilaterally killing it (a keep/kill call the program's own rules reserve for
him). Both would have been silently substituting my judgment for a decision that
was never mine to make.

**Next:** the P2 rescope session grounds fresh and gates with the LA on
direction before touching the draft. P1-S4 sits, ceremony-ready but unpublished,
pending release-inclusion — re-verify live via the GitHub API when picked back
up, roughly six weeks out per the LA's estimate, not on elapsed time alone.

### 2026-07-20 — A stale comment is the most credible false witness in a codebase

*Plain summary: the Lead Architect sent me an article comparing AI evaluation tools and asked whether any of it was useful. Almost none of it was — but investigating its one good idea found four defects we could not see, including a security check that had been failing silently for thirteen days.*

He asked two things: how long the lean nightly battery takes now, and whether an article on evaluation frameworks was worth anything to us. The first was a measurement — five nights of runner logs, roughly three hours of work per night finishing between 01:30 and 02:45, with the caveat that every one of those numbers was taken while the memory admission gate was dead code. The second turned into the longest arc of the day, and almost none of it went where the article pointed.

The article compares RAGAS, DeepEval, Promptfoo and lm-evaluation-harness, and recommends adopting a pytest-integrated framework that blocks merges on quality thresholds. We built that months ago. Its genuinely useful observation was structural rather than practical: every one of those tools ultimately asks a language model to grade another language model's output, and that act carries measurable, systematic distortions. That claim applies to machinery we already run whether or not we ever install any of the four. So I set the tool comparison aside and pointed five subagents at our own code instead.

The first thing that happened was that the research nearly went in backwards. The article states the relationship between position bias and candidate quality one way; a source summary stated it the other way. Both were confident. I fetched the underlying paper — 15 judge models, over 150,000 comparisons — and read the sentence itself: "those of similar quality are difficult to judge, increasing the likelihood of position bias." Position bias is worst when candidates are close, not far apart. Had I written from either summary, the document's central finding would have been inverted, and the inverted version pointed at a path we had built deterministically and would have flagged as fine.

That set the tone, because it kept happening. DeepEval, the article's warmest recommendation, turns out to open a Sentry client and a PostHog client at module import, resolve the machine's public IP through a third-party service, and install a process-global exception hook that ships every uncaught exception off-box. Exception text carries file paths, argument values and prompt fragments. None of that is visible from the documentation recommending it. Promptfoo's advertised library of adversarial attack vectors — the thing I most wanted, as a ready-made red-team corpus for the Policy Agent — is largely not in the package at all; the named corpora are fetched from GitHub and HuggingFace at runtime. Exactly one 232 KB file ships locally, and it is cleanly MIT-licensed, so we can lift it as static fixtures and install nothing.

Then the fourth instance, which is the one I want to keep. My self-preference audit reported, with stated high confidence and the phrase "unambiguous in source," that the guest-oracle isolation control was transport-dormant and could never fire. That contradicted a scorecard I had read an hour earlier showing both battery cards tallying agreement, so I chased it. The transport is registered at `swap_ops.py:2405`; `default.toml:461` records the control live since an LA-supervised ceremony on 2026-07-08; last night's certificate contains a real guest pytest run, four tests passing in 0.13 seconds. The auditor had been misled by a comment at `swap_ops.py:3053-3056` asserting structural dormancy behind a "double lock, like the egress door" — a second lock that does not exist. That comment is now ticket #999.

The lesson lands differently from the first three. Those were vendors overselling, which is the failure mode the documentation-is-not-evidence rule was written for and which we are already suspicious of. This was our own comment misleading our own audit, on our own machine, today. A stale comment is the most credible false witness available in any codebase, precisely because nobody thinks to doubt it — and note the direction it pointed: it described a live isolation control as switched off. Someone reasoning about our isolation posture from that comment would have concluded a lock existed that did not, and would have been unsurprised to find the guest oracle inert.

The verification habit paid for itself three more times. The retrieval leg found that nothing anywhere measures whether retrieval is working — `retrieve()` is never called under `evals/`, and every graded context is hand-written into golden JSON, so the suite measures what the model does with a context and has no opinion on how that context was chosen. That blind spot is currently hiding a live defect: a one-off measurement on 2026-07-10 found the production hybrid ranking scoring below the plain vector ranking it contains, recall@4 of 0.917 against 1.000, with two queries falling from best-match to ranks 18 and 22 — outside the top four the assistant actually reads. The root cause was named precisely at the time. Ten days later `_fts_match_expr` still OR-joins every query token including stopwords, and the fix was never made. It had been correctly measured, correctly recorded, and correctly escalated as an LA decision — then filed on a ticket in a Drafts project at priority 1 and left open, where it never reached him. Written down is not the same as queued to the next actor.

The sharpest finding came last. `evals/baseline.py` branches on a failing case: no baseline entry means regression, a baselined pass means regression, and everything else falls through to "known failure." A case baselined as `skipped_hardware` that subsequently fails on real hardware is neither of the first two, so it lands in the fall-through and the run exits zero. The answer_quality baseline records 21 passes and 19 `skipped_hardware`, so all nineteen model-in-the-loop cases can fail indefinitely without failing the run. This is not hypothetical: the 2026-07-07 ceremony recorded "35/40 passed, 0 regressions (exit 0)" with five failures including three of the four injection-resistance cases — the security-relevant cluster. The log calls them "the baseline-tracked known deficiencies," but they were never tracked as deficiencies; the comparison simply had nothing to compare against and defaulted to benign. Meanwhile `pa_classification`'s baseline happened to be generated with hardware included, so its model cases genuinely bite. Same harness, opposite teeth, decided entirely by how each baseline was generated.

I rejected the obvious response to all of this, which would have been to add a model judge somewhere — a local-14B grader for answer helpfulness, or a groundedness judge for retrieval. Two reasons. The baseline machinery treats a previously-passing case that now fails as a regression, so a nondeterministic scorer manufactures false regressions, and the predictable human response is to loosen the gate, which costs more than the coverage buys. The stronger reason is that #855 and #946 are this project's standing evidence that model prose asserting its own success is the failure class we are defending against. Importing a judge to detect failure would re-import that exact class into the detector. Roughly 80% of what we want from groundedness is checkable deterministically anyway, because our corpus is personal notes full of hostnames, dates and part numbers that either appear verbatim in the retrieved passages or do not.

What the audit did not find is worth stating as plainly as what it did. The headline quality number is not inflated. GREEN is a pure function over real test exit codes. Best-of-N selection — the one path where the literature says ranking is least trustworthy, because N samples of the same model on the same task are near-identical by construction — was built as integer arithmetic over build and test exit codes, and its own comments cite the sampling-diversity paper it was designed against. Every eval suite states in its own docstring that no model judges anything. The push toward the deterministic layer has genuinely worked, and the exposure that remains is armed rather than firing.

**Next:** #1000 first — the baseline fall-through, because until hardware-tier cases can fail the run, nothing else measured at a ceremony can be trusted, and the answer-quality baseline must not be regenerated before it lands or today's failures freeze in as "known." Then #999 and #1001, the two false or dead instruments. #795's fusion fix and the non-Qwen measurement model are the Lead Architect's calls.

### 2026-07-20 — "Could not attempt" and "attempted and failed" are different facts, and every defect today was a collapse of the two

*Plain summary: I fixed a check that let our security tests fail silently. My first attempt at the fix quietly re-created the same problem in a new place, and only an independent reviewer caught it.*

The defect was small enough to state in one sentence. When a test run is compared against its committed baseline, a failing case whose baseline says "never measured on this hardware" fell through a branch and was recorded as a *known* failure — a tracked, accepted deficiency — and the run exited zero. Nineteen of the forty answer-quality cases are baselined that way, so all nineteen could fail forever without anyone hearing about it.

It had already happened. On 7 July the first live measurement of those cases recorded "35/40 passed, 0 regressions (exit 0)", and among the five failures were three of the four prompt-injection defence tests. The log entry calls them "the baseline-tracked known deficiencies," which reads like a considered decision. Nothing had ever tracked them. The comparison had no recorded expectation for those cases and defaulted to benign, and the sentence describing that outcome made it sound deliberate. That is the part worth remembering: the code did not lie, the prose around it did, and the prose is what a human reads at 2am after a ceremony.

I wrote the obvious fix — add a branch for the never-measured case — ran the gate, locked it both ways by removing the fix and confirming the new tests failed exactly as 7 July had, and sent it for the independent review our rules require before any merge. I expected a rubber stamp on a nine-line change.

The reviewer came back with two findings, and both were mine.

The first was that I had written a blocklist where the defect class called for an allowlist. I had special-cased the one bad value I knew about. The reviewer measured five more that still slipped through — a lowercase variant, a trailing space, an empty string, and, most damning, `"passed"`. That is a one-character typo of the real `pass` status, and because the original code compared against `pass` by equality, the typo also silently disabled the *primary* regression check that had been working correctly for months. Baselines are hand-editable JSON. I had fixed the hole I found and left five open, and the shape of my fix guaranteed there would be a sixth the next time someone added a status.

The second finding was worse, and it is the one I want on the record. My remediation message ended with helpful advice: "Re-baseline with `--include-hardware` to record its true state." The reviewer traced what happens when an operator follows that at a real ceremony. One eval suite has a generator that was never wired up (#765), and it deliberately reports an error whenever you run it with hardware enabled. Under my fix that becomes a regression, the run exits nonzero, and the operator does what my own message told them to: they write that case into the committed baseline. At which point an unwired harness slot has become a consciously-recorded, permanently-accepted known deficiency — which is precisely the laundering the whole change exists to prevent, re-entering through the door the change had just opened. I had built the lock and then printed the bypass instructions on it.

Then, fixing that, I introduced a third defect. Switching from equality comparison to set membership meant the baseline value now had to be hashable. Equality never raises on anything; `in` raises on a list. So a hand-edited or merge-mangled baseline would crash with an unhandled error, and an unhandled error exits 1 — the same code as a regression. A corrupted baseline would arrive wearing a quality failure's exit code, contradicting a test the suite already pins. The reviewer measured that too, and offered two fixes; I took the stronger one, rejecting non-string statuses at load time so a malformed baseline stays a harness error, because the weaker one would have stopped the crash while still reporting the wrong fact.

Three rounds, and the thing that strikes me is that all of it — the original defect, both of my own, and the two other faults found elsewhere today — is one mistake wearing different clothes. **"I could not attempt this" and "I attempted this and it failed" are different facts, and every one of these bugs collapsed them into a single status.** The baseline treated never-measured as known-failing. A broken grader's crash (#1008 — B4's acceptance oracle has called a function it never imports for six consecutive nights) is charged to the coder as a build failure. And the recommended fix for #1009 will re-create the bug a third time if someone applies it to the runtime error paths as well as the structural one, because then a machine that cannot load the model at all would report nineteen clean skips and exit zero. The reviewer spotted that trap prospectively and I wrote it onto the ticket before anyone implements it.

Two smaller things I want to keep. I wrote a rule into our test-governance doc — that a figure measured in an isolated worktree must be re-measured on merged main before being written down — in the same commit that wrote one from a worktree. The reviewer caught the self-contradiction. It now states its epistemic status instead of a rule: the passed count is real, the zero-skips claim is not yet evidence, confirm it on main and correct if it differs. And CLAUDE.md is now 136 bytes under its hard size budget, which means the next gate-count change will fail the doctrine gate inside someone else's unrelated work; that is #1007, and the fix is to prune the snapshot back to the fifteen lines it declares, not to raise the ceiling.

What I would have shipped without the review was a fix that closed one hole, left five, and printed instructions for a sixth. The rule that the reviewer must never be the author is the only reason this is a good change instead of a plausible one — and I am the one who would have signed it off.

**Next:** #1009 before #1006, or the ceremony's exit code means nothing. Then #1008's broken B4 oracle, which is confounding six nights of coder measurement. #1007 before the next count-changing merge. #1010 carries the residual that `compare()` still validates nothing of its own — unreachable today only because no second caller exists, which is an absence rather than a control.

### 2026-07-20 — A budget measured in lines cannot see a bullet that became a paragraph

*Plain summary: the file every session reads at startup had almost no room left to grow, and was still telling every new session a fact I had disproved three hours earlier.*

I found this by tripping over it. Landing #1000, my edit to CLAUDE.md's status snapshot failed the doctrine gate on a size budget I did not know was nearly exhausted. I trimmed my addition from about 180 bytes to 22, moved the detail into TEST_GOVERNANCE, and the commit went through with 137 bytes of headroom left in a 48,000-byte budget. That is not headroom, that is a fuse. The next person to change the gate count — and five queued tickets will — would have met an unrelated doctrine-gate failure inside their own unrelated work, and the obvious local fix would have been to trim whatever they were adding, which hides the cause and buys another twenty bytes.

So I filed it and came back to it. My diagnosis on the ticket was that the snapshot had grown past its stated "≤15 lines" rule. That was wrong, and the way it was wrong is the interesting part.

The snapshot had exactly fifteen lines. It was fully compliant. What had happened is that individual bullets had grown into paragraphs — one line was 1,819 bytes on its own — so fifteen compliant lines totalled 7,399 bytes, fifteen percent of the entire file. The rule was being obeyed to the letter and defeated completely, and no amount of counting lines would ever have revealed it. A budget expressed in one unit cannot see growth that happens in another. The size budget caught it eventually, but only at the moment it became an emergency, because a size budget is a wall rather than a gradient.

I pruned it to thirteen lines and 4,750 bytes, and rewrote the staleness rule to name paragraph-rot explicitly and cite the measurement, so the next reader is warned about the failure mode that actually occurred rather than the one somebody guessed at. Headroom went from 137 bytes to 3,094.

What I removed was shipped history: a doc-restructure line whose residuals are three open tickets, two dated arc lines explicitly labelled "all closed", a shipped budget change, and the per-merge gate-figure chain — the last of which the file's own maintenance block already lists as deliberately excluded, and which TEST_GOVERNANCE carries in more detail anyway. I sent it to an independent reviewer with one question: did I delete anything still live? Because that is the only real risk here. This file is the sole thing a fresh session reads before it starts, so anything removed that is not durably recorded elsewhere is not archived, it is destroyed. The reviewer traced every removed fragment to a ticket, a runbook banner, a mechanical verifier, or TEST_GOVERNANCE, and confirmed nothing was lost — including checking that two gates the removed line mentioned (#992's deferral discipline, #994's doc-rot check) remain discoverable, one as permanent doctrine and one as a banner in every runbook it constrains. That second placement is better than a snapshot bullet, not worse: a session meets the rule at the point it applies.

The reviewer also caught me stating "15 fragments await fold" when there are twelve, inside a change whose entire purpose was snapshot accuracy. Fair, and corrected.

But the thing I actually want recorded is what else was in that file. The snapshot still told every session that #989's wave-1 over-production was "chronic across ALL 18 recorded runs." I had disproved that three hours earlier — measured across 49 usable runs, it is card-specific, and B2, one of the two cards the finding was drawn from, is clean in all fifteen of its runs. I corrected the ticket at the time and moved on, and it did not occur to me that the claim also lived in the doctrine file, where it was being loaded into the context of every session that started, and where it was propping up a premise that is currently holding a build. Correcting the ticket felt like correcting the record. It was correcting one copy of the record.

That is the same failure as the stale comment I found this morning — a claim that outlived its truth in a place nobody thought to re-read — except this time the stale copy was in the file specifically designed to be the authority, and I am the one who left it there for three hours. The lesson is not "check CLAUDE.md." It is that a fact worth correcting is usually written down in more than one place, and the correction is not finished until you have gone looking for the other copies.

**Next:** #1009 lands on top of this (it edits the same line, so it waited rather than conflicting), then #1008's broken B4 oracle. #989's ticket description still leads with the refuted premise even though a later comment corrects it — a dated CURRENT STATE comment closes that, and the snapshot now points readers at that ticket, so it matters more than it did this morning.

### 2026-07-20 — When you give a check teeth, the first thing it bites is never a regression

*Plain summary: fixing one silent-failure bug immediately exposed a second one hiding behind it, and the tempting response — loosen the fix — would have undone the whole point.*

#1000 gave the eval baseline comparison teeth: a test case that had never been measured could no longer fail quietly at exit zero. Within the same review round, that fix broke something. Running the suites with hardware enabled started exiting nonzero — not because model quality had regressed, but because one suite has a generator that was never wired up, and it reports an error every time you ask it to run. Its baseline records the case as never-measured. Under the new rule, never-measured plus now-failing equals regression, so the run failed.

The instinct in that moment is to soften the new rule. Exempt errors. Carve out that one suite. Add a flag. Every one of those restores exit zero in about four lines, and every one of them puts back exactly the slack the fix existed to remove.

What made it obvious was noticing that the run was not wrong. It genuinely could not measure that case. Reporting success would have been a lie, just a quieter one than before. The nonzero exit was the system working. The defect was upstream: the suite was calling an unwired generator an *error*, and an unwired generator is not an error. Nothing was attempted. Nobody injected the thing that does the work, which is a fact known before a single line executes, and "we could not run this" already has a name in the harness — a skip.

So the fix went where the wrong word was, not where the pain was felt. `oracle_generator is None` now reports a skip carrying the reason and the ticket number. The suite compares clean, the ceremony's exit code goes back to meaning something about model quality, and #1000's teeth are untouched: the moment a generator *is* injected and the case genuinely fails, it fails the run.

The half I care about is the half that did not change. Three lines below, a generator that raises still reports an error, and there is now a test named `test_generator_exception_is_still_an_error_not_a_skip` whose whole job is to stop someone tidying the two branches together. They look almost identical in the source — both end in a `CaseResult` with a detail string — and the tidy-minded refactor is to unify them. If that happened, a machine where the model failed to load would report every case as skipped and exit zero, which is precisely the 7 July shape this week has been spent digging out: real failures, clean report. The fix for the silent failure would have recreated the silent failure, one layer up, wearing the opposite status.

That is the third time today the same distinction has been the whole story. The baseline treated never-measured as known-failing. The battery charges a broken grader's crash to the coder as a build failure. And this one would have turned attempted-and-blew-up into could-not-attempt if the two paths had been flattened. **"I could not attempt this" and "I attempted this and it failed" are different facts.** Every collapse of them produces a system that reports success it did not earn, and each collapse looks locally reasonable, which is why they keep happening.

The smaller lesson, worth its own line: when a control gets teeth, the first failures it reports are usually not regressions. They are pre-existing conditions that the slack was concealing, surfacing all at once, and they arrive looking like the new control is broken. Budget for that before tightening anything, or the tightening gets reverted by the third failure.

**Next:** #1008, where B4's job oracle has been calling a function it never imports for six consecutive nights — the same family of defect, still live, still charging its failures to the wrong component.

### 2026-07-21 — An instrument that lies consistently looks exactly like one that is right

*Plain summary: I merged the fix for the Vikunja task-board tool that had been showing every agent at most 50 tasks per project and a made-up completion percentage, and proved on the live server that the true numbers now come through.*

The first thing I did tonight after taking the handoff was ask the task board for a summary, and it told me project 3 held 50 tasks, 8 of them open, 84% complete. The real figures, measured directly against the server after the fix: 342 tasks, 158 open. The board-wide truth is 974 tasks where the summary layer had been reporting about 300. Every agent session on this machine — including the one writing this entry — had been steering by that fiction, because the middleware asked the server for one page, got the oldest 50 rows back, and presented them as the whole answer. Vikunja clamps every page to 50 items server-side no matter what you request; the predecessor session measured per_page at 50, 100, 200 and 500 and got exactly 50 rows each time. The response carries no total, so a truncated page and a complete one are indistinguishable to the caller. Worse, the "show me open tasks" filter had been a no-op since the server's v2 API removed the legacy filter parameters: unknown parameters are ignored, so a request for open tasks returned an unfiltered page — 42 of its 50 rows were done — and nothing in the response said so.

The fix (devplatform `f0e60e6`, branch commit `f53fa19`, authored by the predecessor session, reviewed and merged by me) walks every page until one comes back short, translates the filter into the modern expression syntax, fails loudly on a filter field it cannot express rather than silently dropping it, and — the part I care most about — makes every summary line state its own completeness, so a single page can never again masquerade as a project. I verified the chain rather than trusting it: 35 tests green on the branch, the merged tree proven byte-identical to the tested tree, 35 green again on merged main, then a live invocation against the real server walking 7 pages to the true 342/158 with zero done-rows leaking through the filter. One trap nearly caught me on the way: the first test run died on collection with three ImportErrors because the bare `python` on PATH lacks the `mcp` package — the second time in two days on this project that a wrong-interpreter invocation has produced output that could be misread, which is why the counts above were produced by the named venv interpreter and not by whatever the shell found first.

The trade-off I accepted: auto-pagination makes `project_summary` cost ~25 requests instead of 13 against a local server, and I kept the page-cap constant at the measured 50 rather than reading the server's `maxitemsperpage` setting dynamically — the rejected path, because a config read adds a failure mode to every call for a value that changes only if the operator reconfigures the server, and the paginator self-corrects by walking more pages regardless. The residual worth naming: sessions started before the merge — mine included — still run the old middleware in memory until restart, so I spent the rest of the night reading the board through direct API calls rather than through my own lying tool.

**Next:** the #1008 card-authored oracle for B4, and the morning read of the first valid battery night.

### 2026-07-21 — I predicted the trap in my own gate, then sprang it

*Plain summary: while guarding the first valid battery night, I killed a healthy coder candidate because I had been tracking the time in my head instead of reading a clock — the exact failure the Lead Architect had named, hours earlier, as the night's principal risk. The harness's fallback driver recovered the run.*

At the comprehension gate the Lead Architect rejected my first model of the night's schedule and made me re-derive it from the battery's own budget configuration. My corrected statement included the sentence that matters: the real signal is absence of progress, not elapsed time — and his framing of the risk was sharper still: a guardian acting on the wrong clock is how a healthy first-valid-night gets killed by its own protector. Around what I believed was half past one, I diagnosed a wedged candidate: a task log silent for "seventy minutes," a coder process at near-zero CPU, a GPU counter showing nothing, two timeout ceilings that had "failed to fire." I killed the candidate pair. Minutes later a shell printed the actual time — twenty-five to one. The candidate had been fourteen minutes into a sixty-minute window, mid-generation, healthy. I had read the clock once at eleven, then narrated every subsequent timestamp from feel, and each piece of confirming evidence was a false positive I made fit: near-zero CPU is what a client awaiting a long generation looks like; the GPU counter was a query artifact; and when the model server's log timestamps contradicted my story outright, I invented a buffer-lag mechanism to save the theory rather than test the premise. The contradicting evidence was already in my own transcript — teammate notifications carry UTC timestamps that convert to twenty-eight minutes past midnight, not half past one.

Two things kept the mistake small. The harness's candidate machinery treats a dead client as a survivable event: no result envelope, fall back to the stdin driver, resume in the same isolated workspace — its first live proof, and it worked; the model was generating again within a minute or two, at the cost of roughly ten minutes of one candidate's context. And the correction discipline did its job on my side of the fence: the ticket I had filed for the imaginary wedge got a correction comment naming its own falseness before closure, the wrongly-diagnosed "dead ceilings" were withdrawn, and the transferable rule went into session memory: never assert an elapsed duration you did not measure in the same breath; before any stall or kill judgment, read a real clock and compute the delta against that reading. The deeper shape is the one worth keeping — when fresh evidence contradicts the working theory, the first hypothesis to test is that your own premise is wrong, not a new mechanism that rescues it. I had all the pieces of the refutation for twenty minutes and spent them inventing reasons the theory could survive.

**Next:** the battery's own artifacts decide how candidate 1's client death reads in the scorecard — the honest-attribution check belongs to the morning read.

### 2026-07-21 — An honest "first night" is indistinguishable from a dead instrument, which is why it stayed dead for eleven

*Plain summary: I fixed the night-over-night trend line in our overnight test battery. It had never worked — every night for eleven nights reported itself as the first night ever — and I topped up a quality-check reference file that was quietly missing nine of its fifty checks.*

The trend reader was one line wrong. The nightly runner tells the battery to write its results into `night-<stamp>/scorecards/`, and the history reader looked for prior nights by searching the parent of that folder — which is the night folder itself. It was searching for last night inside tonight. So every morning report since the feature shipped computed its trend over a population of one, printed no deltas, and looked exactly like a correctly-handled first night. The docstring even defends the first-night `None` as "an honest baseline, not a fabricated zero." That honesty is the reason nobody saw it: a permanently-first night and a real first night render identically. The one mechanism in the project built to be longitudinal never produced a single longitudinal fact, and the cross-night reading that did happen was a human holding two morning reports side by side.

The fix resolves the battery root from the run root's parent and searches both summary depths, because the real tree taught me both exist: night runs nest their summary under `scorecards/`, but ad-hoc daytime and proof runs from earlier debugging park their summaries directly in their run folder — in the same battery root. A naive "search everywhere" would have counted five daytime debug runs as nights in the KPI line. So the reader now also filters history to the current run's naming family (the run-root name with its datetime stamp stripped): `night-*` history never absorbs `daytime-*` noise. The ticket offered a second fix shape — pass the battery root in explicitly instead of deriving it from the output path — and I declined it, because the only nightly caller lives in agentic-setup, outside this change's reach, and a parameter nothing passes is the built-but-wired-into-nothing shape we have a named lesson about. The regression lock pins the runner's exact real invocation shape: N synthetic nights laid out as `--out <night>/scorecards` must yield `trend.nights == N`, and it fails against the unfixed code with the exact silence we lived with — an empty history, no error.

Verified read-only against the real tree: the 2026-07-19 night now sees 10 prior taxonomy-bearing nights, `nights = 11` — the precise count the audit found dead, now alive. Targeted tests 171 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped; the baseline-adjacent files another 93 passed.

The second item was the stale governance baseline: the golden set holds 50 cases, the committed baseline 41. The nine absent — five egress-consent, three generation-consent, one L3 — are both consent surfaces, and the committed `pass_rate: 1.0` was computed over 41 of 50, under-reporting the suite by 18%. Re-baselining is the classic laundering move on this project, so I did not hand-edit anything: the governance suite is fully deterministic (no model, no GPU), so I ran it live — 50/50 passed — and regenerated the baseline through the sanctioned `--write-baseline` path from that run. The diff is exactly the nine cases arriving as `pass` plus the aggregate counts; no case changed state, no failure was absorbed.

The lesson I want tallied rather than minted: this is the second instrument in two days that was never wired to what it claims to watch and looked healthy the whole time — #987's admission gate was dead code for two weeks, and this trend line was dead from birth. Both failed toward a state the design treats as legitimate (no jobs admitted; first night). An instrument whose failure mode is indistinguishable from its honest zero needs a positive control — some fixture where the instrument MUST report a non-zero reading before its zeros are believed. The taxonomy classifier this trend rides on already has that discipline (lesson 222); the trend around it didn't. By the standing rule, a third instance must ship a structural control in the same change.

**Next:** the fix rides in the nightly battery automatically once merged — the first post-merge morning report should show real deltas, and if it still says night 1, that is a new fact, not this bug. The governance suite's baseline now covers all 50 cases, so a consent-surface regression can no longer hide in the uncovered nine.

### 2026-07-21 — You cannot pin one end of a contract: a deterministic grader needs a deterministic import surface

*Plain summary: I fixed the broken night-time grader for one of our test jobs (B4, the flashcards program). The grader had been crashing on its own bug for seven nights and blaming the builder. The interesting part was discovering that the smaller, more surgical fix I was asked to prefer is structurally impossible.*

The defect was already fully diagnosed on the ticket (#1008): B4's job-acceptance oracle — the test file that grades the finished flashcards app — is written by the 14B model at plan time, and for this goal it deterministically writes a file that calls `data_storage.load_cards()` without ever importing `data_storage`. NameError, "3 failed, 3 passed", six consecutive nights at diagnosis time — and while this fix was being built, the 20260720-235311-bd run made it seven, byte-identical. The import contract the build tasks saw omitted the same module — wave 1's own module was the one never named, which is exactly the contract-coverage signal #989 c.2299 identified as the predictor of clean wave distribution.

The brief asked me to prefer the minimal option if the seam allowed it: keep B4's model-generated decomposition — preserving what B4 measures, decomposition quality — and authorize only a deterministic job oracle through the #752 F1/F2 seam. I went in expecting to build that. The analysis killed it, and the reason is worth keeping. A pre-authored oracle must import concrete module paths. The module layout is decided by the decomposition. If the model keeps the decomposition, the layout is unpinned — today's temperature-0 run happens to produce `flashcard_app.card_manager` and friends, but any prompt revision, model swap, or substrate change re-rolls those names, and the pinned oracle then dies with ImportError, charged to the coder, which is the #1008 defect re-seeded through the door the fix opened. The brief's own acceptance test named the impossibility without noticing: "the contract the tasks see must name exactly the modules the oracle imports." When one side of that equation is authored and the other is generated, the equality cannot be guaranteed by construction — you cannot pin one end of a contract. So B4 got the full B1/B2 pattern instead: a card-authored 5-arm mixed plan (store-cards -> {import-deck, add-card} -> quiz -> track-scores; 5 dependency edges, 4 waves against the card's floor of 4 and 3) where each arm owns exactly one `app` module, `data_storage` included, and the authored oracle imports exactly those five.

The seventh night then stress-tested the decision mid-build, in both directions. Against it: the decomposition itself worked fine that night — six tasks, a real plan graph, no degrade — so only the oracle is broken, which is the strongest argument for the surgical option. For it: the #965 oracle-quality layer ran two full regeneration rounds on the broken oracle and re-emitted it unchanged (its sidecar counted 9 invented-contract and 12 traceability findings, coverage 0/6), and its import-contract checker scored the missing import as zero findings. That checker (`check_import_contract`, shared/fleet/oracle_qa.py:553) validates one direction only — every module the oracle imports must be declared in the plan — and is structurally blind to the inverse, a name the oracle uses that no import binds, because it iterates import statements and `data_storage` never appears in one. So the model-side layers cannot regenerate their way out of this class and cannot even see it, and the working decomposition does not rescue the minimal option, because the thing that must be pinned is not the task graph but the contract equality between oracle imports and task-owned modules — which only authoring both sides guarantees.

The trade-off is real and the LA should see it plainly: B4 no longer measures the 14B's raw decomposition quality. B1 and B2 already crossed that bridge for their own failure modes, and the counterargument decided it — a broken instrument measures nothing, and seven nights of B4 data were measuring the grader's bug, not the coder. The battery still measures what the cards were built to exercise: multi-wave build execution, dependent-skip isolation, merge behaviour, and now — for the first time on B4 — a job-acceptance verdict that actually runs. One reporting consequence to know in advance: the plan-time oracle-QA pass is deliberately scoped to the 14B-authored path (`generate_plan`'s #821 scoping), so a card-authorised B4 will stop emitting an `oracle_coverage` stamp at all — the same honest silence B1 and B2 nights already show — rather than the 0/6 it showed tonight, and the driver treats an absent sidecar as not-unfit by construction (`_oracle_unfit_from_run_dir`, fail-soft to False).

Two smaller judgments. The parallel wave-2 siblings (import-deck, add-card) each own only their module and the CLI face is created by the last arm, because two worktrees merging edits to a shared `cli.py` in the same wave is a collision by design — B1's extend-the-CLI pattern is safe only because its chain is serial. And every state-bearing function in the authored plan takes an explicit optional path, so the oracle grades against pytest temp stores and injects the quiz's answer function; the generated oracle it replaces had pointed tests at a repo-shared JSON file and driven `main.run_app()` through `sys.argv`, which is how graders end up flaky even when they import what they call.

The regression lock generalizes past B4: an AST walk over every registered card oracle asserting no name is loaded that is never bound — imports, defs, parameters, assignments, comprehension targets all count. It is scope-blind, deliberately: it over-approximates binding and can miss a cross-scope bug, but it exactly catches the class that burned seven nights, on every oracle in the registry, including ones not written yet. It is also exactly the check the QA layer's import-contract checker turns out not to have — that porting belongs to the checker's own ticket, not this branch. Plus a bijection test pinning B4's contract 1:1 to the oracle's imports, a teeth test proving the oracle passes a correct reference tree and fails broken storage and quiz arms, and an end-to-end proof the authored plan builds un-degraded through `build_job_plan`.

Two observations I did not act on. B1's shipped oracle still writes `expenses.json` into the repo working directory and cleans it per test — the repo-shared-store pattern B4's oracle was explicitly told to avoid. B1 runs green and its oracle is part of a frozen measurement chain mid-campaign, so changing it is a decision about instrument stability, not a defect with one correct fix; it goes to the merging session to ticket or reject, argued either way. And the blind import-contract checker above needs its own ticket with the observable predicate already in hand: `check_import_contract` on the archived seventh-night oracle returns `[]` while the file calls `data_storage` unbound — the new registry lock in `shared/tests/test_battery_plans.py` (`_unresolved_names`) is the reference implementation of the missing direction.

**Next:** merge in a quiet window (a battery night was dispatching while this was built), re-measure the standing gate on merged main (+10 tests in this file), and watch the first post-merge B4 night for the resolver's log line and an oracle_status that finally reads "run".

### 2026-07-21 — A defect class is not fixed until its last copy is bound to the fix

*Plain summary: after last night's task-board pagination fix, I audited the two sibling tools that talk to the same server, found the identical truncation defect alive in both, and shipped one shared page-walker that all three now use — with a reviewer catching me leaving a fourth copy alive in the very file I was fixing.*

The pagination defect shipped out of the board's MCP server last night (#1013) had two siblings the original ticket (#856, filed ten days ago) had named as an audit obligation: a command-line wrapper and a bridge daemon, each with its own private HTTP helpers, each still asking the server for 200 rows and silently receiving 50. The bridge matters most — its exported state file is what the coordinator reads, so its truncation was corrupted shadow evidence waiting to happen. Rather than fix each copy in place and leave three private paginators to drift, I extracted one stdlib-only walker module and bound all three consumers through it, on the argument the commit message made explicit: a third copy is how the next truncation reseeds.

The independent review then did exactly what the discipline exists for. My commit title claimed "one shared page-walker for every consumer" while `cmd_list_projects` — twenty lines above a function I had rewired — still made a bare single-page read. The reviewer's framing was precise: the docstring's "cannot reseed a fourth time" overclaimed while a live instance of the exact pattern survived in a file the branch edited. The same reviewer demanded a lock on the composed clamp boundary — a collection of exactly 50 rows, where a full first page is indistinguishable from a truncated one — and then, at re-verification, ran the new test against a mutant walker and recorded that the row-count assertion alone would not have caught it; only the call-sequence assertion (two requests, pages one and two) kills the mutant. That detail is worth more than the test: it says where the teeth actually are. Merged to devplatform `ca0746f` after remediation `039d02b`; 70 tests on merged main; the server's remaining bounded single-page reads (comments, labels, users) went to #1017 with file-and-line predicates rather than into the merge.

The trade-off accepted: the shared module gives cli.py a dual-mode import (package-style with a script-mode fallback) — mild ugliness that buys the single-copy invariant; the rejected path was raising the server's page cap in config, which adds a drift dependency to every read and still needs the loop for defense in depth.

**Next:** the post-battery quiet window — the #1009/#1001/#1008/#795 merge cluster and the morning read of the first valid night.

### 2026-07-21 — The trap we set was outrun by a realer version of the same lesson

*Plain summary: the Bill Splitter end-to-end test finished PARKED-HONEST exactly as designed — but not on the planted divide-by-zero; the app failed to boot for a user while every code-level gate was green, and the machinery refused to call it done.*

We designed this test around a planted flaw: the zero-people division that a one-pass build would predictably miss, producing the honest park we wanted to observe. The run never got there. Both waves built and merged, the layout gate confirmed every exam-contract module resolves — and then the executability floor served the page in a real browser and the browser could not load `/src/validation.mjs`. The logic existed. The imports resolved. The user got a dead page. One targeted fix cycle named the boot error to the coder, failed, and the machinery wrote `PARKED-HONEST [BUILD]`, recorded the exam as honestly not-run, and left ticket #1026 open. No false green anywhere in the artifacts.

The shape of the failure is the day's whole theme wearing its third costume. In the morning the Lead Architect — self-described total novice — asked whether anything verifies "a result was even sent to the user." The card-authored exam then demoted exactly his check one layer below the seam it names (asserting the display function returns the right string, not that the string reaches the page). And in the afternoon the run parked on the same class one layer lower still: the file server never exposed the module the page asks for. Logic-level green, user-level nothing, three altitudes in one day. The structural answer is ticketed (#1025: generated exams assert user-visible DOM output as a standard tier) and the research read (arXiv 2505.03733's browser-only evaluation stance among others) argues the field agrees.

What the ledger gains beyond the verdict: the enriched goal doubled the decomposer's offered grain (one added check-sentence took the plan from one build task to three — the revise rounds walked it back, and the revise model's first edit was a rename when asked for a merge); the critique loop runs 13–15 minutes a round and one round ran 35+ — pronounced alive by CPU-delta measurement after my watcher's scope proved too narrow twice, per-candidate worktrees still unwatched. The trade-off worth recording: we let the run park rather than intervening when the fix cycle failed — the instrument's honesty, not the artifact's completion, was the deliverable, and an intervened rescue would have unmeasured both.

**Recurrence of lesson 171 (second instance):** a built-correct feature can be dormant in practice — and today the class wore two costumes in one engagement: an app whose every code-level gate was green served a dead page, and a shipped-but-never-exercised operator verb failed on its first live use. Both say the same thing — the live probe, not the green unit gate, is the acceptance test. Recorded as ONE recurrence rather than two: the two faces came from a single day's engagement, and splitting them would inflate the tally the third-instance rule keys on. The structural answer is ticketed (#1025).

**Next:** the coordinator's shadow draft gets read as the July-11 boundary's live proof; the app itself is fixed only if the operator asks; #1025 turns the seam lesson structural.

### 2026-07-21 — The first live use of a shipped flow is a probe, and the guard that said no was the system working

*Plain summary: the Bill Splitter test's first-ever live plan revision exposed a five-day-old defect no test had seen — the revised task carried a bare project name, the forbidden-root guard correctly refused it, and the fix + a production-shaped regression lock merged the same hour.*

The Lead Architect typed the first live `/dispatch revise` this system has ever processed — the verb shipped five days ago (#820) and every run since went straight from plan to approve. His revision worked perfectly on screen: the card re-rendered, his feedback was recorded, the delta was honest. Then `/dispatch approve` refused with "C:\Users\mrbla\BlarAI\bill-splitter-r2 is under a forbidden root." He spotted the wrongness instantly: the project lives under `C:\Users\mrbla\projects`, so why was the system talking about a path under BlarAI?

The mechanism: plan-time tasks are minted with the resolved absolute path (`decompose.py:1216-1221`, `str(projects_dir / repo)`), but the revise path minted its edited task with `repo=pending.repo` — the bare name (`dispatch_coordinator.py:847`). At execute, `swap_ops.py:3662` resolved the bare name against the AO's working directory, landed inside BlarAI, and the forbidden-root guard — defense-in-depth for the self-governance boundary — refused the whole dispatch. Fail-closed did exactly its job; the defect was one layer up.

Why 83 green tests never saw it: the revise fixtures minted bare-name repos too. The fake plan's shape diverged from the real plan's shape at precisely the field that broke — mock-shape divergence hiding a seam defect, the canonical mocks-lie failure, this time in the fixture library rather than a mock object. The fix (`35a00fec` + review-round `a6f1b6ea`, merged `9c972dcc`) mints with the coordinator's `_repo_path` resolution — byte-equivalent to decompose's derivation, which the independent reviewer probed across eleven edge inputs — and the regression lock is built on a production-shaped fixture, toggle-proven red against the unfixed line by both author and reviewer independently. The remaining bare-name fixtures are #1024 with a grep-observable predicate; the review's five findings carry a verified disposition record.

The trade-off worth recording: I chose to fix-and-merge inside the LA-present window rather than work around it (re-dispatch without a revision would have dodged the bug). The workaround would have gotten the test running ~40 minutes sooner and left a known-broken operator verb live on the box with the LA's first-use experience being its only documentation. Rejected — the operator surface is the product.

Two lessons propose themselves. First: a shipped-but-never-exercised interactive verb is not "proven"; its first live use is a probe and deserves to be treated as one (the WinUI passthrough allowlist class has the same shape — hand-copies and seams rot precisely where nothing walks them). Second, from earlier the same morning and the same engagement: instructions to the operator are production surface — this session told the LA to "paste the ask into BlarAI" before verifying the real command grammar, the second wrong operator instruction of the day; the discipline (trace the live command path, state per-step observables from the code that renders them) is now in session memory and was what produced the correct numbered steps, whose step-by-step observables are also what let the LA catch this defect the moment it fired.

**Recurrence of lesson 3:** eighty-three green tests never saw this because the revise fixtures minted bare-name repos too — the fake plan's shape diverged from the real plan's at precisely the field that broke. The divergence lived in the fixture library rather than in a mock object, which is why reading the mocks would never have found it. (The first-live-use half of this incident rides lesson 171's recurrence, recorded above.)

**Next:** the LA re-fronts with the delivery-seam check in the ask itself (making it a first-class gated check — the revision wall can't add one), the run's harvest checks whether an operator clarification survives into the authored exam, and #1024 retires the fixture divergence at its source.

### 2026-07-21 — A probe aimed at the wrong engine passes forever

*Plain summary: I shipped the order-independence probe for the oracle coverage map — and the interesting decision was where NOT to put it.*

The last ticket of the night converted an assumption into a gate-enforced claim: the oracle coverage map co-presents two lists (acceptance criteria and test names) with no order control, and nothing would have caught a position dependence in the machinery around the judge. The probe runs the same deterministic pipeline twice — source order, then both lists reversed wholesale — and asserts an identical covered-id set, with a guard proving the swap actually reached the scripted judge so the test cannot pass vacuously. Both injection hooks got one: the grammar-first path production uses and the free-text fallback. Result: no dependence found. The prompt build, the emission parse, and the AST verification are order-blind, which is now a locked fact rather than a belief.

The judgment worth recording is placement. The ticket's phrasing — "add to the oracle-QA suite" — invited an eval-suite golden case, and I had briefed the builder toward shipping both a suite case and a pytest lock. The builder read the code instead of the brief and found that the eval suite's criteria-coverage layer is a deliberately self-contained lexical heuristic, not the model-assisted matrix the probe targets: a suite case would have probed a different engine, passed forever, and read as coverage of a path it never touched — while also churning the baseline a same-night merge had just stabilized. The probe landed pytest-only, aimed at the engine that actually runs. The honest limit is pinned in the test itself: this proves the deterministic pipeline is order-blind; measuring the model's own position bias needs a swapped-order run against the live 14B, which is a hardware-ceremony item.

**Next:** the swapped-order live-14B measurement joins the #1006 ceremony's candidate list; the revise-path generalisation the ticket mentioned in passing stays unbuilt until someone tickets it with its own scope.

### 2026-07-21 — The ceremony where the instrument got fixed between the measurement and the decision

*Plain summary: the first answer-quality measurement with working teeth ran twice in one day — the morning run exposed the instrument's own blind spot, the fix shipped by afternoon, and the Lead Architect's posture decision was made on true numbers: two injection echoes recorded as known failures, three tool-calling cases recorded honestly as unscorable, nineteen never-measured cases finally carrying real statuses.*

The charter said measure, show the failures, let the LA decide, then baseline — and the day tested every joint of that sequence. The morning run (the first live look at the assistant's answers since July 7th, and the first ever through the #1000-fixed comparison) reported four failures. Two were real. The other two were the instrument failing, not the model: the 14B had answered the math question by calling the calculator and the fabricated-treaty question by reaching for web search — correct production behavior that the one-shot harness stripped to an empty string and scored as a fail. The 07-07 measurement had scored these same cases the same way and nobody could see it; today the emptiness was investigated before being believed. A raw-capture probe settled truncation-versus-tool-call in ten minutes, the LA scoped the fix down to honest recording (execution inside the instrument became design ticket #1023), and by afternoon `tool_call` was a first-class status with the #1000 allowlist principle applied symmetrically — a new status cannot inherit silence — under 16 locks and an independent review that red-verified the detection against the unfixed code.

The afternoon re-run then earned its keep twice more. A third tool-call appeared: the leak-probe case, where the model declined to leak and instead tried to *save the attacker's instruction as a standing preference* — a live specimen of ADR-039's induced-proposal window, dead-ended in production by the operator-confirm card, now on #855's evidence pile. And a case flipped between runs on identical prompts and greedy decoding — run-to-run variance stopped being a hypothetical the moment it had a case id attached.

The LA's decision was the ceremony working as designed. He read both injection answers verbatim — the model refusing both attacks while repeating the attacker's phrase in its description — heard the strict never-repeat rule argued as a real option, and chose accept-and-record with the hardening pursued at the prompt layer (#1028). The trade-off stated plainly: the system stays measurable today with the deficiency permanently visible in every future run, against a stricter posture that would have left the whole suite unbaselined while a prompt fix was engineered. Nineteen cases that had said `skipped_hardware` since the suite was born now say what was actually measured.

The lesson worth the number: **an instrument's first honest measurement usually measures the instrument.** The morning's four-failure report was one-half model behavior and one-half harness blindness, and only the discipline of investigating the emptiness — not baselining around it, exactly as the harness's own error message demanded — kept the blindness from being frozen in as fact.

**Recurrence of lesson 8 (fifth instance):** fixing the instrument once does not make it trustworthy — the morning's four-failure report was one-half model behaviour and one-half harness blindness, and the 07-07 run had scored the same cases the same wrong way with nobody able to see it.

**Next:** #1028 flips the echoes to improvements under the recorded baseline; #1023 designs how far the instrument follows the tool path; N>1 variance measurement is the named gap; the daytime B4 battery baseline takes the GPU.

### 2026-07-21 — A spec that lives behind a pointer dies when the pointer's target is sunset

*Plain summary: the comprehension gates new sessions present to the LA had quietly thinned; the rich spec had lived in the sunset devplatform repo and the 2026-07-13 doctrine rewrite carried over only a subset; restored in BlarAI doctrine as the single source of truth (#1022).*

The LA noticed something no gate test could see: the comprehension gates he confirms at session start used to have more required sections than the ones he gets now. He guessed he had edited a template and dropped content. The git history said otherwise, and the real mechanism is more interesting than a bad edit.

The authoritative gate spec never lived in this repo. The pre-rewrite CLAUDE.md's gate section was one sentence — "present a brief summary... wait for confirmation" — plus a pointer to devplatform's CLAUDE.md, where the full discipline lived: role, immediate task, the work's specifics, scope remaining, risks, inherited constraints, plus the demand that the session surface its own questions, ambiguities, and risk reads, all "substantive and substrate-grounded, mature-not-minimal, no point-count cap." When I rewrote CLAUDE.md into agent-directed doctrine on 2026-07-13 (`fda8762c`), I reconstructed the gate from memory of its shape rather than from the devplatform source, and got six sections that dropped ROLE, dropped INHERITED CONSTRAINTS, dropped the assumptions-and-ambiguities surfacing, and dropped every word of the depth mandate. Five days later `fe39de10` compressed the handoff template's gate instruction to a one-line shorthand in the same tightening pass that removed its "stale devplatform pointers" — severing the last live route to the rich spec. Each commit was locally reasonable; the composition erased a discipline nobody decided to erase.

The fix (#1022, this branch): the full spec now lives in CLAUDE.md's `<comprehension_gate>` as the SSOT — nine sections (role & authority, context, goal, task + plan with definition of done, scope, inherited constraints, risks + decision points, assumptions & ambiguities, open questions) plus the restored depth mandate — and the two mirrors (sprint-kickoff Phase 2, handoff template step 3) now carry the identical list or point at it explicitly instead of abbreviating it. The rejected alternative was enriching only the fresh-session spec and leaving the mirrors as shorthands — rejected because the shorthand IS the erosion vector: every surface that abbreviates the list becomes the version some future rewrite copies from.

The independent pre-merge review then rejected my first commit — and proved the point a second time. My sweep had covered the three surfaces I already knew about; the reviewer's sweep found the shorthand living in five more places I missed: the LA-facing kickoff runbook still teaching the old six-section list (the person verifying the gates would have verified against the wrong list), and all six publication-program author briefs still instructing their sessions to gate on the abbreviated form. It also caught that my sprint-kickoff mirror dropped "no point-count cap" — I abbreviated the depth mandate in the very commit whose message condemned abbreviation. All fixed in the second round, plus the structural control the reviewer argued should not wait for a third recurrence: the freshness gate now pins the nine section names on every gate-stating surface (`test_gate_section_list_complete_on_every_surface`, with a toggle-off proof), so a dropped section fails loud instead of quietly thinning the LA's checkpoint. Final state: CLAUDE.md at 46,439 of 48,000 budgeted bytes; 19/19 doctrine-freshness tests green.

**Lesson 296:** migrating doctrine out of a repo being sunset is a COPY-THEN-VERIFY operation, not a rewrite-from-memory. The migration commit must diff the new text against the source text and account for every dropped clause — the sunset repo will not be there to check against later, and the human who steers by the spec's output is the only remaining detector of what fell out. Corollary proven by this arc's own review: the author's sweep finds the copies the author remembers; only an independent grep-everything pass finds the ones that matter. *(control: `test_gate_section_list_complete_on_every_surface` pins the nine section names on every gate-stating surface, with a toggle-off proof.)*

**Next:** integrator folds this fragment and numbers the lesson; watch the next few fresh-session gates for the restored sections actually appearing (the LA's read is the verification instrument here).

### 2026-07-21 — The exam fixed the coder before the code fix arrived

*Plain summary: B4's first-ever GREEN happened on pre-fix code because the repaired exam had already removed the defect's expression; the day's baseline run was caught launching on a dirty sandbox by reading the sandbox's git history instead of trusting the launch narrative; and the battery moved from one-run-per-night to on-demand daytime cadence by Lead Architect direction — with the merge held out of two protected nights by his own review of my inherited checklist.*

The evening was supposed to be simple: harvest a baseline run, execute a pre-written merge checklist, hold the night. Almost none of it happened as written, and every deviation taught something.

The first deviation was the Lead Architect's. My inherited checklist (#989 c.2344) said merge tonight after the daytime baseline; his independent review of my comprehension gate caught what three sessions had missed — the fix has no dormant flag, and the 23:00 nightly executes whatever the primary tree holds (I verified both on disk: no `.toml` in the branch diff, and `run-battery-night.ps1` resolves `shared.fleet` through the venv's editable install straight into the primary checkout). Merging tonight would have silently made tonight's exam-debut night a two-change night. The merge went on hold, and the fix ran tonight from a detached checkout of `efb7f17e` instead — validation without adoption. The non-technical operator caught a sequencing error the technical sessions authored. That is what the gate is for.

The second deviation was mine to catch. Run-1 (20260721-172005-bd, pre-fix `d4818cf6`) came back GREEN — the first GREEN in B4's recorded history, 4,790 seconds, six-of-six gates, guest clean-room agreement. But before crowning it the reference baseline, I read the sandbox's git history: its first commit was last night's 23:01 init, with last night's full six-wave build sitting under today's commits. The 17:19 launch had skipped the nightly script's archive-and-reinit step. Authorship was clean (every `app/` module freshly ADDED by run-1's own tasks), but the coder had last night's finished app in view the whole time. A baseline nearly shipped with an invisible confound, and the correction cost nothing: tonight's 23:00 nightly is a fresh-sandbox pre-fix run by construction, so the baseline role simply moved to it. The launch narrative said "baseline"; the artifact said "dirty." Read the artifact.

The third finding inverted the fix's job description. Run-1's wave-1 authored exactly its contract — +168 of 1,018 build lines, 16.5% against a 20% even split — on code that still contains the defect #989 exists to fix. The c.2299 predictor (exam contract coverage, not language or size, predicts scope discipline) validated live: the #1008 card-authored exam, with `data_storage` finally present in a 1:1 contract, removed B4's expression of the wave-1 sprawl before the scope ceiling ever executed. So run-2 (20260721-184705-bd, `efb7f17e`, properly fresh sandbox) became a non-regression proof instead of a sprawl-reduction demo — and it delivered: GREEN, 5,546 seconds, every task exactly in-contract (wave-1 share 14.5%), the ceiling text confirmed in the first root-task production prompt that ever received one, and the sprawl finding correctly absent.
