Nine ceremony flags were declared in the first pass, each verified by reading the wiring before writing the claim: web_search (LiveKagiAdapter), image_generation (both generate entry points), fleet_dispatch (enqueue/run/read-summary), advanced_intake (`_apply_realism_guard` + `_ensure_delivery_floor` — the two rulers, nothing more), vikunja_bridge, guest_oracle_enabled, coordinator.enabled, heartbeat_enabled, require_signed_draft_manifest. The comment edits are provably comment-only: the tomllib parse of default.toml hashes identically before and after (sha256 45acdc90…, re-verified after every editing pass, including after a line-ending repair when sed silently converted the file to LF). The gate went green first run — which lesson 293's tally says is evidence of nothing — so I mutated the real file (renamed the realism-guard symbol in the declaration) and watched the gate fail naming the flag, the capability, and the missing symbol, then reverted. The test also ships planted-violation proofs over the real file plus a synthetic section (phantom symbol, phantom file, test-file claim, missing declaration, runbook-armed dormant shape, binding mismatch, malformed declaration, unconsumed flag), an honest-form-passes control (a planted TRUE claim raises nothing but the correct unconsumed finding), and a toggle-off proof that doubles as the stated limit: the identical lie with every trigger stripped escapes, and the test says so instead of hiding it.

The independent review then did what my own planted violations could not — it attacked the checker with my blind spots instead of through them — and its verdict was do-not-merge with three findings that all held. The sharpest one reproduced lesson 47's shape INSIDE the control built to close it: my declaration parser treated a prose line inside the entry list as the end of the declaration, so every entry-shaped claim below it was silently never checked — a planted lying entry after a prose comment sailed through green. A truth gate with a fail-open is a comment claiming a property the code does not enforce, one level up. The fix makes any entry-shaped line outside the checked run a loud failure (I re-ran the reviewer's exact repro against both parsers: old — zero errors, phantom unchecked; new — caught by name), with regression tests for both shapes. The second finding was a membership escape my own measurement had rationalised: two real ceremony flags never trigger past-tense flip vocabulary — `knowledge.images_enabled` (future-tense, lowercase "go-live ceremony") and `coordinator.shadow_mode`, whose go-live direction is flip-to-FALSE, a polarity no flip-phrase list can ever match. The structural answer was to stop enumerating tenses: any mention of "ceremony" in a flag's bound comment, case-insensitive, now makes it a member. Measured over the real corpus that net swept in seven more genuine ceremony surfaces I had walked past (the two named, plus both signed-manifest gates, the signed-policy gate, the doom-watchdog flag, the autonomy ladder) and one I had not even considered — `jwt_ca_cert_path`, whose inline comment says "ceremony-generated" — and zero non-ceremony flags. Each got a wiring-verified declaration; the AO file now carries seventeen. The third finding was scope honesty: the gate read one file while the launcher's guest-parser config — the UC-003 weld, an LA-held ceremony surface — sat unread. Extending it was cheap because the checker was already pure over text: both configs are now pinned in the gate, the launcher's weld flag and its mTLS hard-gate material carry declarations, both files hold tomllib round-trip equality (launcher sha256 8bd302b4…), and a renamed-symbol mutation of the launcher file was watched failing by name before being trusted.

What the control provably misses is written in its docstring rather than discovered later: English prose above a truthful declaration can still lie (prose stays reviewer territory — that is the price of rejecting the scanner, paid openly); symbol existence is not reachability (per-flag behavior tests own the flag-actually-gates-it seam, e.g. `test_advanced_intake_flag_reaches_generate_plan`); and the direction is claims⊆code only — a flag enabling MORE than it declares is not caught, because catching it needs the code-side registry I rejected. One deliberate non-change: the advanced_intake prose block that #1042's review landed as the corrected ceremony artifact was left byte-intact, declarations added beneath it — rewriting a reviewed ceremony record to satisfy my own taste is lesson 292's overcorrection trap, and the S2-era rot in its "still unbuilt" phrasing will be forced out by this very gate the day an S2 flag arrives with its runbook.

Lesson 47's index line goes back from `✓ctrl(partial)` to `✓ctrl` in this change, and the archive tally now cites the shipped control instead of an open predicate. No new lesson proposed: this entry is lesson 47's sub-class control arriving plus lesson 293 applied as written — minting a number for "we followed the existing lessons" would be the over-minting Rule 2 exists to stop.

**Next:** reviewer re-verification of the three fixes (author ≠ verifier — I wrote them, so the re-check is not mine to claim), then the coordinator's serialized post-battery gate run and merge. The residual attack that survives by design: a declaration naming a real symbol the flag does not actually gate — this control verifies existence, and only a per-flag reachability test catches wiring.

### 2026-07-23 — The pairwise check that flagged two of three, and the diagnosis that missed one

*Plain summary: fixing the journal's duplicate entry turned up a third misplaced entry the ticket's own careful diagnosis never saw — because consecutive misplacements hide each other from a pairwise date check — so the fix shipped with a checker that compares against the running maximum instead.*

#1021 arrived fully diagnosed: a degraded duplicate of the 07-01 "Packing the house" entry at line 3755, and a stranded 07-04 entry behind it, both residue of folds that appended backdated content at the then-tail (`1cc26adb`, `9aa92ece`). The diagnosis was right about everything it named — the duplicate's 54 lines were strictly dominated by the complete copy at line 141, verified by extracting and diffing both blocks — and it still under-counted, because the sweep it recommended was the natural one: check each entry's date against its predecessor. Run that way, the file showed two violations. But a 07-04 entry sitting after a misplaced 07-01 entry is locally *increasing* — the pair masks itself — and only a check against the maximum date seen so far surfaced the third misplacement: a 07-07 entry parked after two 07-08 entries, from a different fold. The surgery itself was done as segment arithmetic over the original file with a multiset identity check proving delete-only-the-duplicate, and the moves were verified block-hash-identical by the independent reviewer afterward.

The judgment worth keeping: a pairwise invariant check under-reports exactly when the defect it hunts occurs twice in a row, and the fix — carry the running maximum — costs one variable. The chronology gate now in `tests/security/` locks the masked shape with a planted test that requires BOTH hidden entries flagged, and its docstring names its own honest limit: it can say an entry is out of order, never where it belongs; only history can. The reviewer's probes added two accepted limits to the record — a duplicate re-folded under a different date evades both checks, and a dated header quoted inside a code fence would false-trip — neither present in the corpus today, both written down rather than discovered later.

**Next:** the checker earns its keep at every future fold; the two named evasion shapes are the first things to check if it ever goes quiet while a fold feels wrong.

### 2026-07-23 — Seven drifted flags, six of them born in one hand, and only one of them wrong

*Plain summary: the seven LESSONS.md index tally flags that disagreed with the archive (#1059) were reconciled by per-lesson history archaeology — five flags turned out to be RIGHT with the archive missing the marker, one flag was an over-count, and one drift was a marker written in the wrong dialect; the checker then went into the standing gate with toggle-off proofs driven through its real command line, including a run against the pre-fix corpus reproducing the exact 7-row table at exit 1.*

The drift table looked like seven sessions each forgetting the archive half of Rule 2. It was not. `git log -p` on LESSONS.md showed six of the seven flags were set in a single motion — commit `f551b370`, the 2026-07-19 three-tier restructure that created the index by hand — and the seventh (lesson 64) drifted two days later when a fold wrote its third instance as `*(THIRD INSTANCE: 2026-07-21 — …)*`, a dialect the canonical counter deliberately does not count. That reframing changed the whole job: I was not chasing seven forgetful sessions, I was auditing one session's bulk derivation against the corpus it derived from. And that session turned out to be a better reader than the regex — five of its six "wrong" counts were counting real recurrences the archive had simply never been given markers for.

The per-lesson verdicts, each from the journal record rather than from either drifted surface. Lesson 52 (↺4 vs 3): the fourth recurrence is narrated inside the lesson's own ratified text — "the branch-guard added afterward caught the very next recurrence" — and the June archive dates it: 2026-06-05, EA-7's merge, blocked wrong-branch land, merged `6e6a0c6`. A real tally written as narrative instead of as a marker, exactly as the ticket suspected. Writing it as a proper marker made the 07-12 tally's "Fourth instance of the class" arithmetically false, so that ordinal was corrected to Fifth in the same change (in both the archive and the Canon-32 hot copy, which still carries the legacy marker prose) — the same correction class the fold review applied to lesson 46's SEVENTH→EIGHTH. Lesson 72 (↺2 vs 1): the second recurrence is the 2026-06-07 entry "The control that guards a different door," which names its incident this lesson's sibling in so many words. Lesson 45 (↺1 vs 0): the recurrence is lesson 196's own mint — 196's full text says "lesson 45 … applied to the journal itself," dated 2026-07-03. Lesson 196 (↺3 vs 0): the three recurrences are the 2026-07-13 CLAUDE.md diet (an Active State section appending per-merge chapters until the history outweighed the instructions) and the restructure's two surfaces on 2026-07-19 — the 2.26 MB journal rotated (D1) and the 326 KB lessons file tiered (D2); the index creator set ↺3 in the very commit that did D1/D2, so the enumeration reconstructs the creator's own count. Lesson 214 (↺3 vs 2): the third recurrence is the 2026-07-13 doom-watchdog build, where wiring the new stop source naively would have reported every doom kill as "the overall run budget elapsed" — the #757 mute reintroduced while the module doc cited the lesson; caught pre-ship, which this corpus counts (lesson 52's 07-12 tally is likewise a proactive catch). Lesson 64 (↺2 vs 1+1 ordinal): the flag was right and the marker real; only the dialect was wrong — normalised to `*(recurred: 2026-07-21 — THIRD instance — …)*`.

Lesson 237 is the one genuine over-count, and the reasoning that separates it from 52 is worth keeping. Its full text narrates two failed passes — the 223k-token 400 and the 28-minute socket-timeout restart — and I could find no post-mint recurrence anywhere in the record; the two passes ARE the mint's own material, one arc, one entry, minted once. The distinguishing rule the seven verdicts converged on: the corpus's own vocabulary decides. Text that NAMES a recurrence ("caught the very next recurrence") records one; text that narrates the mint's own failure arc ("the second pass died") does not. So 237's flag came off, and the index shrank seven bytes doing it.

Every reconstructed marker carries a provenance clause naming the #1059 reconcile and what the index creation did or did not write — a marker dated June but authored in July that pretended to be contemporaneous would be exactly the sounding-established-without-being-established failure the authority-first rule exists to stop. The rejected alternative for 196 was collapsing D1/D2 into one recurrence and "correcting" the flag to ↺2; rejected because both surfaces' dilution is separately narrated and separately measured in the restructure entry, and because erasing a deliberate count on a granularity guess is the exact silent-erasure the ticket forbids. Lesson 222's nonstandard control marker was normalised to the Rule-3 `*(control: …)*` form in both its archive line and its Canon-32 copy, discharging the disposition row that made normalisation a prerequisite to gating.

Then the promotion. `check_lesson_tallies.py` grew `--hot`/`--archive` overrides and an extracted `tally_drift()`, and `tests/security/test_lesson_tally_sync.py` now drives the real functions AND the real CLI in the standing gate: the live corpus must reconcile, the ticket's own predicate (`--strict` exit 0) runs through the actual entry point, and five toggle-off probes plant an over-counted flag, an uncounted marker, a Canon-32 headline divergence, and an ordinal-dialect marker — each refused by the shipped detector, none by a reimplementation (the fold review's toggle-off lesson, honoured). The decisive evidence run: the promoted checker against the pre-fix corpus extracted from `1b3522ee` reproduces the exact seven-row table and exits 1 — the gate demonstrably fails on the defect it was built for, which is why the ticket sequenced reconcile-then-gate; a gate failing on arrival gets disabled rather than obeyed. The honest limit rides along as an executable test: a wrong-but-consistent pair passes by design, the #970 class, so a green run means "the surfaces agree," never "the count is true."

**Lesson 301:** a bulk-derived index is a measurement event — when one session sets hundreds of counts in a single motion, its errors are systematic, not random, and its corrections are systematic too; reconcile a bulk-derived surface against its authority the day it is born, with the same instrument that will later gate it, because a reconcile deferred to the first drift report arrives after the surface has already been read as fact. The second proposed judgment — the corpus's own vocabulary as the arbiter of what counts — was deliberately NOT numbered: its operational content now lives in the checker's docstring where the next reconciler will actually meet it, and a near-corollary number would dilute the index that Rule 2 makes the pre-mint search surface.

**Next:** the merge train continues; #1059 closes citing the shipping SHA now that `--strict` exits 0 on merged main. *(Builder branch 42c4d318; the archive gained SEVEN reconstructed markers across five lessons plus one dialect normalisation, one ordinal correction, and 222's control-marker form; targeted suites 57/0 at build, standing gate 8800/0/0 on merged main.)*

### 2026-07-23 — An opt-out must be narrower than the gate it opens

*Plain summary: the battery harness now refuses to spend the GPU on a used sandbox — and the
one escape hatch can waive a condition we measured, never a condition nobody read.*

I built #1058 tonight: the sandbox-freshness precondition inside the battery harness, lesson
225's third-instance control, the one the 2026-07-22 fold deliberately held a fragment open
for because it changes the measuring instrument and could not land inside a battery
attribution window. The shape of the incident it retires is simple: the nightly wrapper
archives and re-inits every sandbox, so wrapper-launched runs are fresh by construction — but
a direct `python -m tools.dispatch_harness.battery --jobs B1` has no such step, and on
2026-07-21 at 17:19 a daytime run launched onto a sandbox carrying four prior `agent:`
commits and nearly crowned a confounded baseline. The gate now reads each card's sandbox git
history BEFORE the per-card spend — before even the AO re-ensure, which can boot a launcher
and load the resident model — and refuses anything that is not provably fresh, as a fail-loud
STALLED [HARNESS] scorecard that costs that card and never the night (the lesson-220
containment contract, made explicit in the loop rather than left to an unwind).

Three design calls carried the judgment. First, freshness is an ALLOWLIST, not an
`agent:`-blocklist. I enumerated the commit subjects a legitimately fresh sandbox can carry by
reading the writers themselves — `init battery sandbox` from the wrapper's re-init block,
`Initial commit (created by BlarAI)` from the fleet scaffold, the protected `seed:` prefix
from new-agent-task.ps1 — and anything else dirties, including subjects with no `agent:`
prefix at all. The rejected path (grep for `agent:` and call the rest clean) would have waved
through any prior work that happened to be committed under a different subject, which is
deny-by-default inverted. Second, the probe reads `--all` refs, not HEAD: a PARKED run leaves
its work on an unmerged branch, so a HEAD-only read — which is exactly what the runbook's
manual `git log --oneline | head` probe does — can show a seed-only history on a sandbox that
carries a full prior task on a side branch. The structural control is strictly stronger than
the manual habit it replaces, and the runbook now says so. Third, the probe is ANCHORED: it
requires a `.git` at the sandbox root before invoking git at all, because `git -C` on a
repo-less directory walks UP and would happily report a parent repository's history — a
wrong-subject probe that reads as a pass. That failure path never ran in production; I found
it by asking what the probe would do on each unreadable shape, and it is now a pinned test.

The opt-out is where the lesson-shaped judgment sits. The spec demanded an explicit escape
hatch for a deliberate dirty-sandbox experiment, recorded in the scorecard so the run's
condition rides its own evidence — that recording is lesson 225's actual content. I made the
opt-out deliberately NARROWER than the gate: `--allow-dirty-sandbox` waives a DETERMINED
dirty condition, which the scorecard then records (`sandbox_freshness: dirty`,
`sandbox_opt_out: --allow-dirty-sandbox`, the offending subjects, the `agent:` count), but it
cannot waive an UNDETERMINED one — a git failure, a missing repo, a timeout still refuse even
with the flag set, because a condition that was never observed cannot be recorded, and an
unrecordable condition is precisely what the control exists to keep out of the evidence
stream. The alternative (opt-out waives everything) would have converted fail-closed into
fail-open at exactly the moment the operator is improvising. Every probed card's condition
now rides its record — fresh passes included — so a scorecard is honest about its own
preconditions without anyone remembering to say so.

What I did not do: put the gate on the `--dry-run` path (the fake AO drives a fake projects
dir and spends nothing the gate protects — and the standing dry-run smoke would refuse
everything), or make the gate a separate silent disable parameter (the only off-switch is the
recorded opt-out; the toggle-off proof in the tests monkeypatches the probe instead). The
proof obligations shipped in the same change: eleven tests, including the both-ways pair — the
dirty sandbox refused before any spend with the re-ensure never called, and the SAME fixture
sailing through with the control monkeypatched off, so the refusal is attributable to this
control and nothing else — plus a CLI-path reachability test driving the real `main()` down
the live branch, because a gate reachable only from a keyword argument is
built-but-wired-into-nothing. The probe's 30-second git bound is registered in
`shared/timeout_registry.py`; a timeout classifies UNDETERMINED and refuses, so the bound
protects the runner, never the gate's strictness. One honest limit: the gate trusts commit
SUBJECTS. A prior run that rewrote history to a seed-shaped subject would pass — but the
adversary here is momentum and forgetfulness, not forgery, and the sandboxes are throwaway.

**Lesson 304:** an opt-out from a precondition gate must be narrower than the gate — it may waive a condition the system OBSERVED and can therefore record, never a condition it failed to read; a waiver that covers "unknown" turns fail-closed into fail-open at the operator's weakest moment, and the recording is what makes the waived run honest. (Adjudicated a mint: the fail-closed family — 63, 235 — governs a gate's own posture; none of it speaks to the escape hatch's width, which is where this incident's judgment lives. The lesson-225 third-instance tally rode the held fragment's fold, above.)

**Next:** the held fragment folded, lesson 225's tally recorded with this control as its discharge, and the fold-plan deleted — all in this same merge motion; the gate's first live firing (or first clean pass) on a real targeted run is worth a
line in the ticket when it happens.

### 2026-07-23 — An instrument that demands output will be given output: retiring the produce-something pressure (#1049)

*Plain summary: The coder fleet's wave/retry machinery could not accept "the work is already done" as an answer — so before this change it either burned ~27 minutes of GPU rediscovering finished work or, worse, manufactured junk diffs to satisfy the produced-changes detector. Two fixes shipped together: the driver now asks the job's own acceptance oracle whether the remaining waves are already satisfied before spending a coder on them, and the retry prompt now lets the coder honestly declare "NO CHANGE NEEDED" and stop.*

The 2026-07-14 seed run left two entries in the dispatch-quality ledger that sat un-ticketed for eight days until a mechanical sweep recovered them (#1049 exists because of that sweep — the fourth ledger candidate rescued this way). The measured facts: wave 1's fix pass over-delivered and built the entire page, which converted waves 2 and 3 into no-ops; the fleet then spent roughly 27 minutes of 30B GPU time attempting the finished work three times each; and — the sharper finding — the retry pressure converted two honest "already done" situations into manufactured diffs. Wave 2 scribbled a comment into the protected oracle file it was told never to touch, and wave 3 committed a scratch verification script and deleted it in the next commit, a pair that existed solely to satisfy the produced-changes detector and exit the retry loop. Both junk diffs MERGED, because green gates cannot tell a comment scribble from work. An instrument that demands output will be given output; the question is only whether the output is real.

**Candidate (a) — the already-satisfied pre-check — went into the BlarAI driver, not the dispatch scripts.** The design question with teeth was: what artifact can honestly certify "nothing left to do"? I rejected evaluating the plan's task contracts (`{creates, exports}`) against the tree — file existence proves nothing about content (wave 2's deliverable was a *section inside* an existing file), and import-resolution can be satisfied by stubs; a pre-check keyed on those would eventually skip real work, which is a quality regression wearing an efficiency hat. I also rejected a dispatch-side per-task-oracle check, because plan-graph nodes deliberately seed no per-task oracle (the job oracle grades only at integration) — the check would never fire on the exact machinery the incident ran on. The instrument that means the same thing as "done" is the job's own acceptance oracle, so that is the pre-check: before dispatching each wave (only once at least one task has merged and no wave gate has failed), the driver runs the same restore-before-grade, clean-environment oracle the finish line uses, through a new `precheck_job_oracle` seam. A pass records every still-pending task as an honest SKIP — reason and oracle evidence on the outcome row, `evidence.already_satisfied` on the scorecard, task status `skipped`, never a pass — and spends no coder candidate. One deliberate economy: the pre-check is a *single* oracle run, not the #829 flake-checked differential — the differential exists to protect convictions, and a non-passing pre-check convicts nobody (the wave just dispatches normally).

The verdict arithmetic was the trap. A skipped task made `compute_job_verdict`'s all-merged condition false, and an otherwise-green run would have fallen through every branch to STALLED [VERIFY] — the honesty feature would have punished the runs it helped. So the verdict now counts driver-recorded satisfied skips (and only those — a park-propagated skip still falls through) as delivered, while GREEN stays gated on the finish-line oracle independently passing: a pre-check pass can never mint GREEN by itself, and a satisfied-skip run whose real oracle never ran stays STALLED, because delivered-but-unverified is precisely the false-done class. Fail-closed is structural at every layer: the config knob defaults off in the dataclass and in every spec re-read (a pre-#1049 crash-recovery spec dispatches normally), the noop seam returns not-run, and only a literal `"passed"` skips.

**Candidate (b) — "no change needed" as a first-class outcome — went into the dispatch scripts.** The retry prompt (attempt 2 onward, never attempt 1 — the escape is offered exactly when the coder has already produced one verified no-change attempt, not as an easy first exit) now states that replying `NO CHANGE NEEDED: <evidence>` and stopping is a legal, honest answer. A verified zero-diff attempt carrying that declaration ends the inner retry loop *and* best-of-N sampling — the trade-off I weighed hardest, because stopping sampling forfeits the fresh-candidate recovery that exists for lazy no-op'ers. I took the ticket's side: a declaration is an answer, not a slip; a fresh candidate re-asks the same question under the same pressure, and the measured cost of re-asking is a junk diff that merges. Two details earned their keep in review-of-self: the transcript detector refuses the prompt's own echo (the instruction line's `<placeholder>` can never read as a declaration, and a marker in a transcript whose prompt never offered the escape is ignored outright), and the coder-authored evidence line rides a dedicated report line rather than the `RESULT:` line — the driver-side classifier keys on substrings, and an evidence like "already merged in wave 1" would have classified the task MERGED. The RESULT line stays fixed-vocabulary and still classifies NOTHING, which the ledger itself blessed as the honest class for a no-change wave.

The two candidates interlock: (a) removes most *occasions* for (b) (a satisfied wave never reaches a coder), and (b) is the backstop for every retry from any other cause. Both carry toggle-off proofs that drive real entry points — the driver tests prove the pre-check seam is never consulted with the knob off and that non-pass/raise/malformed results dispatch normally; the PowerShell dry harness drives the real `Invoke-CandidateBuild` against a fixture repo with a scripted coder stand-in and proves the kill-switch (`BLARAI_NO_CHANGE_ESCAPE=0`) restores the legacy three-attempt loop even with the declaration sitting in the transcript. One pre-existing regression lock needed amending, not obeying: the #700 single-gate-body check finds `Format-VerifyError` within 12,000 characters of `Invoke-CandidateBuild`'s header, and the escape wiring grew the function to ~14.4k — the bound is a proxy for "inside this function", the property itself is intact, so the proxy widened to 18,000 with the reasoning recorded beside it.

No new or changed timeout: the pre-check reuses `real_run_job_oracle`'s existing 600 s grade bound (the same reuse-the-grade-bound reasoning the registry's flake-differential row documents), and its worst-case cost — one extra oracle grade per merging wave, on the non-satisfied path — stays dominated by the run-level budget watchdog.

Sequencing: this is a battery-path instrument change and merges in its OWN attribution window per the #740 cadence — never stacked with #1036/#1048/#1044/#969. The config ships `already_satisfied_precheck = true` and the escape defaults on precisely because the merge *is* the A/B flip; the toggle-off levers exist for the differential, not for dormancy.

**Lesson 305:** An instrument that demands output will be given output — a detector that treats "no change" as failure-to-comply doesn't just waste retries, it *manufactures* the evidence it demands, and the manufactured evidence merges. When a loop's only legal outcomes are "produced X" and "failed", audit what the loop does to an actor for whom neither is true.

**Lesson 306:** A skip that the verdict arithmetic can't represent becomes a punishment — before adding an honest new outcome class to any recorded pipeline, walk the verdict/aggregation code for where the new class falls through; the default fall-through is almost never the honest label.

**Next:** the coordinator merges this branch pair in its own battery attribution window after a baseline banks; the window's morning read should check wave-1-share and the skip rows' evidence against the ledger's 27-minute baseline shape.

### 2026-07-23 — The instrument was grading our own furniture: the scaffold seed turns neutral

*Plain summary: The Python skeleton the coding fleet plants into every fresh build carried a placeholder function that shipped, dead, into every finished project — and the quality jury kept correctly flagging it, so part of our quality metric was measuring our own scaffold instead of the coder. The seed is now neutral (it proves the toolchain works and asserts nothing about the product), and the two testability rules the ledger had been sitting on — injected input for interactive code, temp-path-isolated test data — now ride in the scaffold as guidance with machine locks.*

Tickets #1036 and #1048 were built together tonight as one scaffold change, in the agentic-setup worktree branch `feat/1036-1048-scaffold-hygiene` (commits `eed5cc8` + fix-round `e508a52`), because two separate scaffold edits would each have demanded their own battery attribution window for no benefit. The finding behind #1048 was three consecutive B4 runs whose green-quality band B — "unused starter code that was never cleaned up" — traced not to the coder but to `app/core.py`'s seeded `summarize()` placeholder, which survived into every shipped tree. The subtle part, worth keeping: it was invisible to dead-code tooling because its own seeded test imported it. A live reference from scaffolding we also seeded is not evidence of life; only the jury's judgment layer ever saw it, three runs running.

The Lead Architect chose the neutral-seed shape (option 2 on the ticket) over requiring removal plus a gate (option 1), and explicitly rejected exempting the seed from the jury's view (option 3) — suppressing a true finding to raise a score is the shape this project treats as a defect. The build honors a second instance of the same principle inside #1036: the ticket's injection-seam rule (`run_quiz(answer_fn)` — the command line passes `input`, tests pass a stub) is delivered as a worked exemplar in the seeded README and restated in the smoke test's docstring, deliberately NOT as a seeded exemplar module, because a seeded `run_quiz` would itself be dead domain code — the exact #1048 shape wearing a helpful hat. The temp-path rule (#1036 d) got the stronger treatment: it is an executable seeded test (`test_tmp_path_isolated_data`), so the pattern is demonstrated by running code that stays true for the life of the project.

What the seed now is: `pyproject.toml`, `README.md`, `app/__init__.py`, and `tests/test_smoke.py` — an import smoke test plus the tmp-path pattern test. Verified out of the box under the fleet's own gate command (`uv run --no-project --with pytest --with hypothesis pytest -q`): `2 passed in 0.58s`, exit 0, and the canonical-package rename form (`flashcard_app`) identically green at `2 passed in 0.54s`. The verify-scaffold lock suite grew from 101 to 115. Precisely: C4 now asserts the NEW file set lands; the kill-side lives in C5/C5c (no `app/core.py`, no `summarize` residue in the seeded tests) and C12's canonical import-line lock; and a new hygiene section locks three properties mechanically — zero `def`/`class` in seeded package modules, a byte-identical tree fingerprint after the seeded tests run, and (from the reviewer's fix round) the seeded file set equal to the committed reference list, backed by a litter filter in the seeder itself so cache directories a stray tool run leaves in the scaffold SOURCE (`__pycache__`, `.pytest_cache`, `.hypothesis`) can never ride into a coder baseline — the habit rule now written at the filter: never run pytest inside the scaffold source. Each property carries a negative proof through the seeder's injectable `-ScaffoldRoot`: a reintroduced `def summarize` IS flagged, a seeded test writing a repo-root store IS flagged, and planted source litter is NOT seeded. The out-of-box gate proof runs BOTH seed forms — generic and the canonical `-PackageName` form production plan-graph jobs actually dispatch — and fails loud if `uv` is missing rather than skipping, since the fleet gate itself cannot run without it. One honest stumble stays in: my first version of the hygiene locks failed its own first run at 108 passed / 2 failed — PowerShell's unary-comma array return double-wrapped when the call site added `@(...)`, so the detector reported one nested array instead of its contents; the fix was consuming the return the way fleet-lib's own callers do.

The reviewer's round also surfaced a BlarAI-side companion the pair would otherwise have shipped without: `shared/fleet/acceptance.py` still asserted the OLD seed as a live premise in prompt text — the oracle-authoring template told the grader "`app/core.py` holds the starter module", and the coder contract line said "extend `app/core.py`". Post-merge that file never exists, so every single-task python dispatch would have carried a false premise contradicting the seeded README. Fixed on the BlarAI branch `feat/1036-1048-neutral-seed-companion` (commit `b6070b66`) with a lock driving the real `compile_prompts` entry point; 190 targeted tests pass. It merges in the SAME attribution window as the scaffold pair — one coherent change, three commits, two repos.

The trade-off named: the neutral seed still leaves two of OUR test functions in every shipped tree. The rejected alternative — seeding no tests at all — fails the gate out of the box (pytest exits 5 on zero collected tests) and proves nothing about the toolchain; two neutral, permanently-true tests are the smallest seed that keeps the gate green and the band-B readings attributable to the coder again, which is what the metric is for. This is a battery-path change: it is built and lock-verified, NOT yet measured — it merges in its own attribution window, never stacked with another battery change.

**Recurrence of lesson 298 (second instance):** an instruction is not a control — the old seed's own docstring said "replace or extend this" and it was reliably ignored for the life of the profile; an instruction that demands a property nothing establishes manufactures the impression the property holds. The property only held once it became a machine-checked lock with a negative proof.

**Lesson 307:** dead code with a seeded test looks alive to every tool except judgment — reference-counting cannot distinguish product logic from furniture you bolted to the floor yourself; a live reference from scaffolding we also seeded is not evidence of life, and only a judgment layer (here, the green-quality jury) ever saw it.

**Next:** the #1036+#1048 attribution window — one clean battery run with only this scaffold change in play, then the band-B green-quality reading tells us whether the constant we were injecting is gone.

### 2026-07-23 — A night of being corrected by my own controls, which is what they are for

*Plain summary: the overnight drive shipped seven reviewed merges, three battery runs, and four journal folds — and the record worth keeping is how many of my own mistakes were caught by machinery this project built, some of it hours old.*

The shape of the night was a pipeline: builders in isolated worktrees, adversarial reviewers who wrote no fixes, a serialized merge train with the full gate measured on merged main after every landing, and one battery attribution window per instrument change, chained back-to-back after the Lead Architect's before-bed direction that one-change-per-RUN, not per day, is the cadence. The gate figure walked 8780 → 8785 → 8790 → 8800 → 8816 → 8828 → 8851 → 8852, every step measured, never derived.

What the record should keep is the catches. My shadow-precision report survived independent verification on every load-bearing rate — and was corrected three times on labels: a graded-window boundary I had named after the wrong event twice (first the first guarded digest, then a "guard-live boot" the journal's own restart signature contradicts — it is a chosen boundary, and it says so now), and a denominator that excluded the refused statement from the population it was refused out of. My lexicon fix for the guard's live miss shipped with a negation carve-out the reviewer defeated in five sentences — the lookbehind protected the exact adjacency "no tests" and missed every qualified form, a carve-out covering the rare case and not the likely one — so it came out entirely: uniform refusal bias, uniformly priced, the loosening decision left where it belongs. The tally gate #1059 shipped caught my own archive-header dialect slip minutes after it merged, during the very fold that minted its lesson. And the merged-main gate re-run — the rule that the merge itself can break what both parents passed — earned its keep once tonight: the scaffold companion legitimately retired app/core.py as the canonical oracle example, and a lock in a file no pre-merge search had surfaced still pinned the old literal; the re-run failed loud, the lock was re-pinned to the intent (the app-package pin, not one module's name), and the gate re-measured green.

Two instrument honesty notes ride along. My first two battery watchers were broken instruments — a schtasks query that returned empty under the shell's path mangling, misread as "task left Running" — and the replacement rule was the ledger's own: key on artifacts, never on task state; the 45-minute quiet warning that followed was measured against the fleet-run artifacts and found to be a blind spot in the watcher, not a stall in the run. And tonight's B4 story is a diagnostic, not a regression: it parked at the identical three-module fingerprint on both the baseline night and the #1049 window — a recurring plan/contract shape now flagged for the exam-authoring thesis, which is the Lead Architect's S3 scoping decision, not tonight's.

*(Correction, 2026-07-23 — the entry stands as written; this note is appended, not substituted. "A recurring plan/contract shape" is **retracted**: the plan and contract are byte-identical to the GREEN nights', so the shape never changed. The 07-23 forensics found two different failure classes behind one fingerprint — the coder wrote real code that was lost at a silently-swallowed commit step, and the two later runs never ran that task at all because a file named `nul`, created when the model emitted a cmd.exe idiom under bash, leaked into the worktree and survives every removal path git has. The routing to the exam-authoring thesis is retracted with it: the causes sit in the build/repair machinery, not in exam authoring. #1066 c.2437 and c.2446.)*

The residual worth naming as a trade-off: I compressed the train's branch-side full gates into the per-merge merged-main gates (each branch carried targeted suites and independent review instead). The protection that matters — nothing lands unmeasured — held, and the one failure the compression could have hidden was caught by exactly the re-run the doctrine mandates. But it is a compression, recorded here rather than silently normalized.

**Next:** the scaffold window's run closes the night's measurement; the morning report and a fresh handoff brief carry the queue — including the #855 graduation packet, now with a verified report — and the 23:00 anchor is re-armed for tonight.
